topic: Topic: Geotechnical Monitoring and Disaster Prevention
summary:
Geotechnical monitoring is a critical component of disaster prevention and mitigation, as it provides essential information on the stability and safety of geotechnical structures built on or in the ground. The monitoring of geotechnical hazards, such as landslides, liquefaction, and subsidence, is complex and requires the integration of various data sources and analytical techniques. With the increasing frequency and severity of geotechnical hazards worldwide, there has been growing research interest in geotechnical monitoring and disaster prevention.

In recent years, there have been significant developments in hardware and software technologies, which have revolutionized geotechnical monitoring and disaster prevention. These include the use of advanced sensors, remote sensing, big data analysis, and artificial intelligence, among others. These new technologies have expanded the scope of geotechnical monitoring and disaster prevention.

This Special Issue on "Geotechnical Monitoring and Disaster Prevention" aims to cover recent research progress, the latest developments, and open challenges in geotechnical monitoring and disaster prevention. The Special Issue welcomes state-of-the-art reviews, new theories, novel methodologies, innovative and significant applications, and case studies related to geotechnical monitoring and disaster prevention. The topics of interest include, but are not limited to, the integration of sensors and remote sensing, data processing and analysis, machine learning and artificial intelligence, and risk assessment and management. We invite researchers and practitioners from various fields to share their expertise and contribute to this Special Issue.
keywords: ["Geotechnical hazards", "geotechnical monitoring and prevention", "integration of sensors and remote sensing", "data processing and analysis", "machine learning and artificial intelligence"]

topic: Topic: Gut-Immune-Brain Axis in Neurological Disorders
summary:
Special Issue Webinar: Gut-Immune-Brain Axis in Neurological Disorders
The gut microbiome plays a fundamental role in our intestinal, metabolic, and cardiovascular health. Emerging studies show that the microbes residing in our gut also play a key role in our neurological health via the intricate gut-brain or gut-microbiome-brain axis. The precise cellular/molecular mechanisms underlying and regulating this axis are only beginning to be understood. Although the gut and the brain crosstalk directly and bidirectionally via vagus nerve, emerging evidence has also revealed specific indirect mechanisms mediating this interface, including microbiome-regulated metabolites (e.g., short-chain fatty acids), neurotransmitters, hormones, and enteric nervous system. Recent studies have also highlighted the crucial role of both innate and acquired immune and inflammatory pathways involved in this gut-brain axis. Studies have shown that specific perturbations (dysbiosis) in the gut microbiome due to imprudent dietary exposures, chronic drug overuse, specific infections, endotoxemia, sepsis, or leaky gut may trigger systemic inflammation, which may in turn instigate neuroinflammation leading to neurological or neurocognitive impairment. This is in line with studies reporting that specific dietary components with anti-inflammatory or proinflammatory properties may improve or impair neurological function via gut microbiome dysbiosis. Accordingly, the gut-immune-brain axis has emerged as an important area of interest for understanding pathophysiological mechanisms and discovering novel targets and therapies for specific neurological disorders. To this end, a multidisciplinary approach covering diverse mechanisms and elements of the complex gut-immune-brain axis is imperative. In this context, this Special Issue aims to bring together state-of-the-art studies on the role of the bidirectional communications of the gut-brain axis in host neurological health and diseases, with an emphasis on microbiome-related factors and mechanisms. Researchers are invited to contribute to this Special Issue with original research articles, systematic reviews, or meta-analysis reports that can advance our understanding of the gut-immune-brain axis in relation to various neurological and neurocognitive disorders.

Topics include (but are not limited to) the role of the gut microbiome and the gut-immune-brain axis in host health and disease, as follows:
● Gut microbes and the gut-brain axis;
● The role of gut microbiome and microbial metabolites in the gut-immune-brain axis;
● The role of gut microbiome in the regulation of host neurological health via the gut-brain axis;
● Diet-microbiome-brain axis in Alzheimer's disease and related dementia;
● Gut microbiome in Multiple Sclerosis;
● Mechanisms linking the microbiome and gut-immune-brain axis in host brain health and disease;
● Gut microbiome-related neurotransmitters in gut-brain axis and neurological health;
● Microbiome and gut-brain axis in aging-associated neurocognitive decline;
● Microbiome and gut-brain axis in Parkinson's disease;
● Probiotics and Prebiotics in neurological health and disease;
● The influence of dietary and lifestyle factors on host neurological health via the gut-microbiome-brain axis;
● The role of gut microbiome and metabolites in central nervous system diseases;
● Microbiome and gut-brain axis in mental health;
● Gut-microbiota-brain axis and neurodegenerative disorders;
● The development of microbiome and gut-immune-brain interaction in early life;
● Microbiome-based therapeutic strategies to improve brain health;
● The role of the gut-immune-brain axis in anxiety and depressive disorder;
● Microbiome-immune connections in neuroinflammation, neurodegeneration, and neurological conditions;
● Microbiome and gut-brain axis in psychological stress and related disorders;
● Microbiome and gut-brain axis in major psychiatric disorders;
● Enteric nervous system in gut-brain interaction in neurological disorders;
● Microbiome and gut-brain axis in relation to brain aging;
● Leaky gut in neurological disorders;
● Microbiome and gut-brain axis in Bipolar Disorder;
● Microbiome and gut-brain axis in aging-related neurocognition decline;
● Cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying the gut-immune-brain axis.
keywords: ["Diet-Microbiome-brain interaction", "gut-brain axis", "gut health", "microbiome and mental health", "neurological disease", "neurodegenerative disorders", "neurocognitive health", "neuroinflammation", "neurotransmitters", "nutrition neuroscience"]

topic: Topic: Advances in Cognitive Disorders
summary:
Cognitive disorders, such as Alzheimer's disease, frontotemporal dementia, and vascular dementia, are a growing public health concern with significant social and economic implications. Unfortunately, the therapeutic options for these conditions remain limited and often ineffective, largely due to a lack of understanding of the underlying mechanisms and early detection strategies.

Recent advances in molecular and imaging techniques have enabled the identification of several biomarkers associated with cognitive disorders, providing valuable insight into the underlying mechanisms and neuroprotective therapies. In addition, epidemiological studies have shed light on risk factors and potential preventative measures for cognitive disorders. Furthermore, new treatment approaches such as gene therapy, stem cell therapy, and immune-based therapies have shown promise in preclinical studies and clinical trials, offering hope for improved patient outcomes.

This Special Issue welcomes original research articles, reviews, and perspectives on topics including but not limited to the identification of novel biomarkers, the role of genetics and environmental risk factors in disease pathogenesis, the prevalence in different populations, advances in molecular mechanism, and emerging therapeutic targets for neurodegenerative diseases and other cognitive disorders.
keywords: ["Cognitive disorder", "biomarker", "epidemiology", "mechanism", "treatment"]

topic: Topic: Bio-inspired Soft Robotics
summary:
Bio-inspired soft robotics is an exciting and rapidly growing field that draws inspiration from nature to develop robots that are flexible, adaptable, and safe to work alongside humans. This approach has emerged as an important area of research due to the limitations of traditional rigid robots, which are less suitable for tasks that require interaction with living organisms, delicate objects, or unstructured environments.

The development of bio-inspired soft robots that can deform, change shape, and adapt to their environment has the potential to revolutionize many industries, including healthcare, manufacturing, space exploration, and disaster response. For example, soft robots could be used to manipulate delicate tissues during surgery, creating less invasive procedures and reducing pain and recovery time. They could also be used in manufacturing to handle fragile products or to work alongside humans on assembly lines, reducing the need for protective barriers and improving efficiency. In space exploration, soft robots could be used to explore difficult-to-reach areas on other planets, such as caves or crevices, or to perform maintenance on space stations.

Soft robotics also has applications in disaster response, where robots that can navigate through complex and unstable environments could be used to search for survivors or to clear debris. Additionally, soft robots could be designed to mimic the movement and behavior of animals, such as snakes or fish, to create machines that can navigate environments that are inaccessible or dangerous for humans.

This Special Issue on bioinspired soft robotics will display cutting-edge soft robotics by featuring technologies including but not limited to:
1.Bioinspired soft robots, sensors, and actuators;
2.Flexible optical or electronic systems capable of providing distributed sensing, computing, or cognitive abilities to soft machines;
3.Conformable cyber-physical systems reducing current barriers in human-robot and human-machine interactions.

As bioinspired soft robotics continues to evolve, this Special Issue aims to serve as a catalyst for even more innovative and groundbreaking applications of soft robotics and the transformation of many industries in the future.
keywords: ["Soft robotics", "conformable machines", "bioinspired soft actuators", "flexible electronics", "soft sensors", "distributed sensing", "human-like robots", "biohybrid living machines", "soft cyber-physical interfaces", "stretchable control systems", "wearable soft robots", "soft haptic devices", "morphing robots"]

topic: Topic: Exposure to Chemical Additives Associated with Plastics: Impacts on Wild Animals and Humans
summary:
Plastic pollution has become one of the biggest environmental challenges faced by our society. Plastics are widely used in a variety of products, such as food packaging, personal care products, medical materials, electronic products, textiles, tyres, etc. Since the beginning of its manufacture in the 1950s, more than 8 billion tonnes of plastic have been produced, of which more than 6 billion tonnes are waste distributed among the different environmental compartments. This waste is causing an impact on living beings, including humans, because of the polymer and/or the different chemical additives associated with these polymers. There are more than 10,000 different chemicals associated with plastics, of which 2,400 are of concern and more than 60 are characterized as substances of high health risk. From these additives, plasticizers, flame retardants, perfluorinated compounds, UV filters, biocides, toxic metals, etc. deserve special attention due to their widespread presence and their toxicological effects. Biota and human exposure to these compounds may occur through ingestion, inhalation, and dermal contact. The correct knowledge of the exposure pathways and their magnitude is essential to take measures to reduce our exposure to these toxic compounds.

In this Special Issue, we welcome full research papers, short communications, and systematic or critical reviews covering any of the following topics:
1. Occurrence in terrestrial and aquatic media;
2. Occurrence in virgin and recycled materials;
3. Bioaccumulation and biomagnification along food webs;
4. Impact of plastic debris on the contamination of marine biota;
5. Exposure assessment through ingestion of food, beverages and dust;
6. Exposure assessment through inhalation: indoor and outdoor microenvironments;
7. Development of new biomonitoring tools for exposure assessment;
8. Exposure assessment in recycling processes.

We cordially invite all scholars and researchers in these fields to submit your contributions for publication in this Special Issue.

All submissions MUST consider the implications for human and/or wildlife exposure to contaminants. Please see the Aims and Scope of JEEA for further guidance.
keywords:
["
Plastic waste", "chemical additives", "Phthalates", "BPA", "PFAs", "POPs", "human exposure", "wildlife exposure", "risk assessment
"]

topic: Topic: Monitoring of Microplastics in the Environment
summary:
Microplastics and nanoplastics have become a growing concern in recent years due to their potential negative impacts on the environment and human health. These tiny plastic particles, smaller than 5mm in size, are found in various environmental matrices such as water, sediment, soil, and biota. Among them, microplastics and nanoplastics in water have received significant attention due to their potential effects on aquatic organisms and the food chain. The aim of this Special Issue is to provide an overview of the current state of research on monitoring microplastics and nanoplastics in the aquatic environment and the soil environment. This special issue also aims to explore the real issue of microplastics (MPs) and nanoplastics (NPs) in the Southern and Southeast Asian countries. The issue will collect original research articles, reviews, and case studies related to various aspects of microplastics and nanoplastics monitoring, including sampling and analysis techniques, sources and fate of microplastics and nanoplastics, and the ecological and health impacts of microplastic and nanoplastics pollution.

The articles in this Special Issue will cover a wide range of topics, including the development and validation of new methods for detecting microplastics and nanoplastics in water and soil environmental samples, the identification of new sources of microplastics and nanoplastics, and the assessment of the effects of microplastic and nanoplastics pollution on aquatic organisms. Additionally, the potential use of microplastics and nanoplastics as tracers to identify sources of pollution and the role of microplastics and nanoplastics in the transport of contaminants in environmental systems will also be included. Additional factors influencing the transportation of microplastics and nanoplastics are also of significant interest, including the impacts of physicochemical properties, biotransportation, and biofouling, which may be represented by modeling.

Overall, this Special Issue aims to provide a comprehensive overview of the current state of knowledge on monitoring microplastics and nanoplastics in the aquatic environment and the soil environment. We hope that articles in this issue will contribute to the development of effective strategies for monitoring and managing microplastic and nanoplastics pollution in aquatic systems and soil systems.
keywords: ["Microplastics", "nanoplastics", "water", "soil", "aquatic systems"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Immune Subtypes and Immunotherapeutic Responses in Malignancies
summary:
This topic aims to explore the immune microenvironment of cancers through multi-omics analyses, so as to provide reference for precise treatment of cancer. We welcome the submission of original research and review articles that include biomarkers in clinical studies and applications, as well as technologies or discoveries in experimental approaches.
Potential topics include but are not limited to the following:
1. Proteomics, transcriptome analysis, single cell sequencing analysis and other high-throughput approaches to define distinct immunological subtypes of cancer.
2. Transcriptome and single-cell sequencing studies that help discover new cancer prognostic markers, associated prognostic signatures, and immunotherapy prediction.
3. Investigating carcinogenicity of environmental disruptors, their potential detrimental influences on human immune system, and crosstalk with cancer immunity.
4. Genetic vaccine for the effective cancer treatment as a novel immune therapy.
5. Single-cell sequencing and spatial transcriptome analysis to clarify the spatial localization relationship between cells, explore the potential immune mechanism of tumors.
6. Novel high-throughput screening methodologies for mapping cancer landscape.
keywords: ["Immunotherapeutic", "oncology", "multi-omics", "single cell sequencing", "proteomics", "transcriptome."]

topic: Topic: Indoor Environment and Human Exposure
summary:
The built environment, particularly indoor spaces, plays a significant role in our daily lives. As people spend up to 90% of their time indoors, it is crucial to understand how the indoor environment affects human health and well-being. This Special Issue, "Indoor Environment and Human Exposure," aims to bring together a collection of high-quality research articles, reviews, and short communications that address all aspects of human exposure to various organic and inorganic pollutants from indoor environments. The issue will address the latest advancements in research and identify knowledge gaps to inform future directions.

Main Focus:
The main focus of this Special Issue is to investigate the multifaceted relationships between indoor environmental factors and human exposure, as well as their impacts on human health and well-being. This encompasses a wide range of topics, including but not limited to indoor air quality, distribution of indoor pollutants, and assessment of pollutant exposure.

Possible topics:
1. Indoor exposure modelling;
2. Indoor air quality and its impact on human health;
3. New pollutants in indoor environments;
4. Concentration, distribution, sources, transport, and fate of indoor pollutants;
5. Pollutants from building materials and indoor environmental quality;
6. Indoor inorganic and organic pollutants and their impact on health;
7. Human exposure assessment and biomonitoring in indoor environments;
8. Other studies related to indoor environments.

All submissions MUST consider the implications for human and/or wildlife exposure to contaminants. Please see the Aims and Scope of JEEA for further guidance.
keywords: ["Indoor environment", "human exposure", "exposure modelling", "indoor air quality", "indoor pollutants", "building materials", "new pollutants", "inorganic pollutants", "organic pollutants", "exposure assessment", "intervention strategies"]

topic: Topic: Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease
summary:
Alzheimer's disease (AD) is affecting tens of millions of people worldwide, incurring trillions of expenses. AD is a progressive neurodegenerative disease that is considered to be unreversible. Therefore, early diagnosis and early intervention are critical for controlling the progression of the disease. Preclinical AD is an entity characterized by the presence of a pathophysiological biomarker signature characteristic of AD in the absence of specific clinical symptoms. It is assumed that abnormal alterations of AD pathologies begin 20 years or more before the detectable symptoms. Therefore, understanding the pathological features and evolutionary patterns of AD, especially during the asymptomatic stage of AD, is clinically meaningful. In addition to the two key hallmarks of AD, β-amyloid plaques and tau neurofibrillary tangles, other characteristics, such as faulty autophagy, abnormal metabolism, synaptic loss, vascular diseases, and neuroinflammation may also be significantly involved in AD. Investigating the characteristics of Aβ, tau, synaptic loss, neuroinflammation, vascular risk factors, and metabolism dysfunction across the spectrum of AD may provide novel insights into the early diagnosis and intervention of AD.

This Special Issue will present novel insights, biomarkers, and neuroimaging findings related to preclinical AD, which will allow us to have a better understanding of the characteristics of AD and may provide more meaningful references for early diagnosis and intervention of AD. Therefore, we call for authoritative and informative articles on preclinical AD, which will generate further interest in this field.
keywords: ["Alzheimer’s disease", "Aβ", "tau", "early detection", "preclinical findings", "clinical trials", "synapse", "neuroinflammation", "vascular", "neurodegeneration"]

topic: Topic: Mineralogical Properties of Graphite and Its Application in the New Materials Industry
summary:
Graphite is a key member of the non-metallic mineral family. Due to its special properties, such as high conductivity of heat and electricity, excellent lubrication, high-temperature resistance and thermal shock resistance, etc., graphite has been widely used in metallurgy, chemical industry, machinery, electronics, aerospace, nuclear energy, national defense, and other industry.

This Special Issue concerning graphite will focus on a unique field of research in mineral materials. The aim is to update the advances in the mineralogical properties of graphite and its application in the new materials industry. Topics of discussion include mining and dressing, purification of natural graphite mineral, its deep processing technology, and a variety of new materials derived from graphite, such as graphene, nanographite, nanocomposite, etc.
keywords: ["Natural graphite", "new materials", "preparation & processing", "function and application"]

source: Journal of Surveillance, Security and Safety
topic: Topic: Security and Privacy of Edge Computing
summary:
In order to meet the requirements of future bandwidth-aggressive or delay-sensitive applications, edge computing (EC) has been envisioned as a key technology. By deploying computing modules near data sources and providing services around users, EC gets the ability to improve task processing efficiency and alleviate core network overhead. Moreover, the risks of information leakage and data tampering in EC can be reduced to a certain extent by shortening the data transmission path, and the distributed nature of EC makes it less susceptible to large-scale service breakdown or privacy breaches. However, the rise of EC also introduces new challenges in terms of security and privacy, especially the presence of DoS attacks, malware, poisoning attacks, eavesdroppers, and other threats. Although there are numerous powerful security and privacy solutions, the limited resource of edge servers constrains the use of these computation-aggressive solutions. In addition, compared to centralized cloud servers, the management of distributed edge servers also faces various challenges, including unreliable edge servers, misconfigured third-party edge-based real-time databases, and other uncertain factors that can gradually become new potential network threats.

This Special Issue addresses all aspects related to the security and privacy of EC. Specifically, it covers the following topics:
● Security architectures in EC
● Security considerations of emerging edge networks and infrastructure, such as O-RAN and IRS
●Adaptive, multi-level, and multi-tiered security and privacy protection in EC
● Network and system security in EC
● IoT security and privacy in EC
● Secure communications in EC
● Lightweight authentication and access control mechanisms for EC
● Lightweight encryption and decryption mechanisms for EC
● Randomization, anonymity, and other privacy preservation techniques in EC
● Intrusion detection and prevention in EC
● Threat modeling and risk assessment in EC
● Trust management and reputation assessment in EC
● Trusted task offloading and storage in EC
● Trade-off between EC system security/privacy protection and QoS
keywords: ["Edge computing (EC)", "emerging edge networks", "security issues", "privacy", "security architectures"]

source: Journal of Surveillance, Security and Safety
topic: Topic: Network Security in the Era of AI: Trends, Issues, and Challenges
summary:
In the past few years, more than 20 million new malware infiltrations have been recorded on computers running major brand antivirus software. In global aggregate numbers, cyber-attacks generate 10G bits per second of malicious data on enterprise and government servers -- with the nefarious goal of consuming network bandwidth and disabling the servers. At the same time, there are hundreds of millions of network-attached security cameras, Wi-Fi printers, personal computers, digital assistants, and Internet of Things (IoT) gadgets that often lack current security patches. These IoT devices can act as entry bridges for criminal states and individuals seeking access to confidential information of an organization. Consequently, all too often, this results in security breaches of important confidential data.

Given the incredible scope of the increasingly complex threat – conventional security methods are no longer sufficient to protect against today's sinister state-sponsored security threats. The solution is to employ a combination of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (a subset of AI) on secure hardware designed and manufactured in the US and Europe. AI duplicates the rational human intellect at machine speed – enabling computers to rapidly identify and accurately scope the threat magnitude. AI utilizes an ML model using Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), which use computing elements to construct a system that is modeled after the operation of neurons in the human brain. In ML, models are fed huge quantities of information, and the system then analyzes the information and optimizes processes based on new strategies and competencies utilized by an attack or malware attack.

In this Special Issue, we will publish a series of excellent ideas on the use of the latest AI models, such as deep learning (DL) and federated learning, for the detection and prevention of network attacks. The Topics could include any of the following items or unlisted ones:
● Identifying the Cyber Threats using ML models
● AI-Based Antivirus Software
● User Behavior Modeling for malicious event detection
● IoT security via federated learning
● Wireless network security based on AI
● Unmanned aerial vehicle network security
● Vehicle network security via ML/DL
● Telemedicine protection and privacy preservation via AI
etc.

All papers should be original contributions instead of previously published materials. The length of each journal paper should be between 10 and 20 pages long, with clear figures and results. All submissions will go through peer review.
keywords: ["Network security", "artificial intelligence (AI)", "machine learning", "security issues", "privacy"]

topic: Topic:  Resilience Design and Assessment Method for Bridges under Multiple Disasters
summary:
Bridges are vulnerable to catastrophic events such as earthquakes, hurricanes, fires, and explosions, which can significantly affect their safety performance and reduce functionality throughout their lifespan. Resilience design aims to develop bridges that can withstand and recover from a wide range of disruptive events, ensuring their functionality, safety, and long-term sustainability. Resilience assessment provides valuable insights into the strengths and weaknesses of a bridge, providing guidance for decision-making, resource allocation, and planning efforts to improve resilience. It helps stakeholders understand the potential impacts of disruptive events and develop strategies to minimize risks, enhance reliability, and ensure the long-term safe operations of bridges. Overall, the study of resilience design and assessment methods for bridges under multiple disasters is essential for creating structures that can withstand and quickly recover from a wide range of challenges, ensuring public safety, economic stability, and sustainable development.


 

The aim of this Special Issue (SI) is to collect and present the latest research development in the field of resilience design and assessment methods, especially for bridges under multiple disasters. The topic includes but is not limited to a combination strategy of multiple disasters, simulation of deterioration and recovery processes, assessment method of safety performance, and resilience index.
keywords: ["Bridge resilience design", "assessment method", "a combination strategy of multiple disasters", "simulation of deterioration and recovery processes", "assessment method of safety performance", "resilience index"]

topic: Topic:  Sino-German Workshops on Early Diagnostic Markers in Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease
summary:
This Special Issue will consist of a selection of manuscripts from the “Sino-German Workshops on Early Diagnostic Markers in Preclinical Alzheimer's Disease” Conference held on Oct 2023 at Hainan, China. The conference is a Mobility research project jointly funded by the China-Germany Science Center and the German Association for German Studies (DFG). Experts and scholars from various disciplines focusing on AD research at home and abroad gathered together to exchange research results on early diagnostic markers for AD, share international advances in this field, and discuss some hot and difficult issues.

This Special Issue aims to present the frontier progress of early diagnosis and intervention of AD from the clinical and mechanism perspectives, strengthen the interdisciplinary and deep integration of clinical research and mechanism of AD, and promote the win-win cooperation and achievement transformation in clinical diagnosis of AD, biomarkers, molecular imaging, non-invasive transcranial brain stimulation technology, and other fields.
keywords: ["Preclinical Alzheimer's disease", "early diagnostic markers", "neurodegeneration", "biomarkers", "molecular imaging"]

topic: Topic: Future Trends in Hand and Wrist Reconstructive Surgery
summary: The future is now - trends in healthcare require validation and re-evaluation to provide the best patient care. The hand is the precision tool of evolution with unique functional anatomy composed of several tissue types condensed in a small but complex organ covered by thin, soft tissue. Future trends in hand and wrist reconstructive surgery may encompass both minor but impactful therapeutic options as well as hand therapy and complex reconstructive procedures. Bone fixation strategies and transplantation techniques are often combined with peripheral nerve surgery, tendon transfers and vascularized soft tissue coverage to address form, function and integrity while considering the quality of life. Patient-reported outcome measures are indispensable to validate future trends in hand and wrist reconstructive surgery and make them accessible to all patients. Back to the future: The issue also addresses the rising academic discussion about the role of surgery in restoring hand and wrist function after severe amputation injuries, which has now developed into a battle between prosthesis with microsurgical signal enhancement vs. replantation, VCA or secondary reconstructive microsurgery. Plastic and orthopedic surgeons in the field of hand surgery have steadily contributed to Future Trends in Hand and Wrist Reconstructive Surgery and will do further, which will be summarized in the present issue of Plastic and Aesthetic Research.
keywords: ["Functional anatomy of the hand", "soft tissue reconstruction", "nerve transfer of the hand", "prosthesis of the wrist", "replantation", "hand transplantation (VCA)", "tendon transfer"]

topic: Topic:  Research Progress of Sleep Disorders in Parkinson's Disease
summary: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a neurodegenerative disease characterized by both motor and non-motor symptoms. Among the non-motor symptoms, sleep disorders are particularly prevalent, including sleep and wake regulation disorders (such as insomnia and daytime sleepiness), parasomnias (mainly REM sleep disorder, but also sleepwalking and overlapping parasomnias, although less common), sleep-related movement disorders (restless legs, RLS), and sleep-disordered breathing (SDBs). Significant progress has been made in the field of PD sleep disorders in recent years. This Special Issue focuses on the characteristics of sleep disorders and the prodromal and early stages of PD; the mechanisms underlying sleep-related changes in PD patients, including PD-related symptoms and the impact of medications; and advances in diagnosing and assessing sleep disorders in PD patients, particularly the application of artificial intelligence technology. Additionally, this issue explores a variety of treatments for PD sleep disorders, including medications, training, various forms of invasive and noninvasive brain stimulation, and circadian disruption-based interventions.
keywords: ["Parkinson's disease", "sleep disorders", "mechanisms", "circadian rhythms", "interventions", "neuromodulation technique"]

topic: Topic: Metabolic Bone Disease: From Basic Science to Clinical Frontier
summary:
Metabolic bone disease is defined as a diverse group of disorders that result in abnormalities of (if) bone mass, (ii) structure mineral homeostasis, (iii) bone turnover, or (iv) growth. The most prevalent form is osteoporosis, characterized by microstructural deterioration of bone tissue and decreased bone mineral density. 55% of the subjects aged ≥50 years worldwide suffer from osteopenia or osteoporosis. The link between the lifetime risk of fracture of the hip, wrist, or vertebral regions and osteoporosis has already been identified. Beyond compromising athletic ability, osteoporotic fractures severely impact daily functionality and mobility, resulting in personal and societal burdens. 

In recent decades, significant progress has been made in pharmaceuticals and treatments for metabolic bone disease,  particularly in bone homeostasis and bone remodeling. However, a substantial knowledge gap remains between the clinical demands and basic research in this field. Translational research, known as “bench-to-bedside” research, holds great potential for bridging this gap. The objective of this Research Topic is to highlight translational studies focused on novel drugs and therapeutic substances for the prevention and treatment of metabolic bone disease, with an emphasis on bone remodeling. The articles in this collection will contribute to a deeper understanding of the mechanisms that underly metabolic bone disease. Furthermore, we aim to explore advanced technologies in drug delivery, targeting methods, release kinetics and distributions within the bone metabolism system.

This Research Topic will primarily focus on metabolic bone disease, spanning from basic science to the clinical frontier. We invite Original Research and Review articles focusing on, but not limited to, the preclinical assessments of novel therapeutic or preventive treatments for metabolic bone disease, and mechanistic studies that enhance our understanding of the disease. Additionally, we welcome evidence-based clinical studies on plant-based natural products and phytochemicals for the treatment of metabolic bone disease.
keywords: ["Metabolic bone disease", "preventive and therapeutic drugs", "plant-based natural products", "phytochemicals", "mechanistic research", "evidence-based clinical study"]

topic: Topic: Microplastics in the Environment: Exposure and Risk to Human and Non-Human Biota
summary: The omnipresence of microplastics (MPs) across environmental matrices and their potential as an emerging pollutant is attracting immense attention from researchers and the media. Studies have reported MPs as carriers of contaminants, including polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, polychlorinated biphenyls, pharmaceuticals, metals, pesticides, and even microbes. Evidence to date, however, suggests that MP concentrations in the environment are markedly lower than those of natural and combustion-derived atmospheric aerosol, along with those of phytoplankton, zooplankton, and suspended particulate matter in the aquatic environment. Hence, concerns in the context of global seafood security and sustainability require careful consideration, and information on MP concentrations and related co-contaminants in the environment needs thorough examination of the contaminant-pathway-receptor relationship. This Special Issue will publish articles that provide data for underpinning the assessment of the risk posed by MPs to human and non-human biota, providing a solid basis to evaluate the overall impact of MPs on human health and food security. Articles that explore the translocation of ingested and inhaled MPs across organs and tissues are also of interest. All submitted articles must address one or more aspects of biota exposure to MPs.
keywords: ["Microplastics", "translocation", "toxicity of MPs", "adsorbed chemicals", "leaching from MPs", "degradation of MPs"]

topic: Topic: Nanoarchitectures and Natural-based Products in Cancer Drug Resistance: Time to Converge Biology and Engineering
summary: Although cancer brought much warning for the community and it caused problems for the public healthcare system, the emergence of chemotherapy improved the prognosis and survival rate of patients and the physicians were hopeful to cure cancer. However, frequent and long-term application of chemotherapy drugs caused the development of drug resistance in the patients and this led to therapy failure and unfortunately, death in the patients. Therefore, researchers focused on findings new solutions for the cancer drug resistance. Although combination of chemotherapy drugs with different mechanisms of actions was suggested as a possibility in improving cancer therapy, the problem is that tumor cells can develop multidrug resistance and moreover, since chemotherapy drugs have concentration-related side effects, the combination of chemotherapy drugs also cause high toxicity on the normal tissues and organs of patients. Hence, new types of therapies should be used for overcoming cancer drug resistance. In the current special issue, the aim is to develop novel therapeutics for reversing chemoresistance. The nanoparticles have been emerged as new therapies for cancer drug resistance and they can suppress drug efflux transporters, provide co-delivery of drugs and genes, and enhance intracellular accumulation of drugs in suppressing cancer drug resistance. Furthermore, the natural products are popular owing to their high anti-cancer activity, pleiotropic function, high biocompatibility and ability of suppressing tumorigenesis. Therefore, the current issue will bring the papers related to application of nanoparticles and phytochemicals in suppressing cancer drug resistance.
keywords: ["Cancer therapy", "Nanoparticles", "Nanobiotechnology", "Phytochemicals", "Natural products", "Cancer drug resistance"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: The Role of the Immune Micro-environment in Tumour Metastasis and Therapeutic Strategies
summary:
Cancer metastasis is one of the most crucial risk factors leading to poor prognosis. The "seed-soil" theory is the most recognized mechanism of cancer metastasis. Besides of cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAF), the tumor microenvironment also contains a variety of immune cells and their secreted products. Large amount of studies have shown that the disorder of tumor immune microenvironment (TIME) plays an extremely important role in tumor metastasis. As we known, tumor-associated macrophages (TAM) is one of the most studied immune cells involved in cancer metastasis in the tumor microenvironment. The transition from M1 macrophages to M2 macrophages can promote cancer metastasis by the secretion of various factors or extracellular vesicles. However, due to the complexity of TIME, our understanding regarding the role of tumor immune microenvironment in cancer metastasis remains limited.

Therefore, the aim of this Special Issue is to provide the latest research achievement related to the role and the underlying molecular mechanisms of tumor Immune microenvironment (TIME) in cancer metastasis, as well as the development of new strategies targeting to immune microenvironment for metastatic cancer therapy or prevention.

Potential topics include but are not limited to the following:
1. The role of immune cells such as macrophage, MDSC and neutrophils in cancer metastasis promotion;
2. The mechanisms underlying TIME promoting cancer metastasis by educating the microenvironment or cancer cells in primary tumor;
3. The mechanisms underlying TIME promoting cancer metastasis by educating the pre-metastatic niche to increase the directional migration and colonization ability of cancer cells;
4. The heterogeneity of immune microenvironment between primary and metastatic tumor;
5. Identification of TIME related key factors in cancer metastasis process;
6. Establishment of TIME related predictive model for cancer metastasis based on multi-omics techniques, including bulk sequencing, single-cell sequencing, spatial transcriptome, gut microbiota, etc.;
7. Novel technologies or clinical applications related to real-time dynamic monitoring of recurrence and metastasis based on TIME factors;
8. Identification of the key therapeutic targets for metastatic cancer;
9. Development of the novel drugs or strategies targeted to TIME for metastatic cancer;
10. The mechanisms underlying TIME promoting cancer metastasis from multi-dimensions, such as abnormal signalling pathway, epigenetic modification including ncRNA/DNA methylation /m6A methylation, protein post-translational modification including phosphorylation /acetylation /succinylation /glycosylation /ubiquitination, metabolic reprogramming, extracellular vesicles, and so on.

Please note: Bioinformatic analysis or computational analysis using online database needs the further biological validation in vitro or in vivo.

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Deep Learning Applications in Cancer Research
summary:
Deep learning has emerged as a powerful tool in various domains, including healthcare and biomedical research. In recent years, its application to cancer research has shown great promise in advancing our understanding of the disease, improving diagnostics, personalized treatment strategies, and patient outcomes. The Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment is pleased to announce a Special Issue focused on "Deep Learning Applications in Cancer Research," inviting researchers and experts to contribute their original research, reviews, and perspectives.

Scope and Topics of Interest:
This Special Issue aims to explore the diverse applications of deep learning in cancer research and foster the dissemination of novel methodologies, techniques, and insights that have the potential to revolutionize cancer diagnostics, treatment, and patient care.

We encourage submissions that cover, but are not limited to, the following topics:
● Deep learning models for cancer detection, classification, and prognosis;
● Deep learning approaches for medical imaging analysis in cancer diagnosis;
● Deep learning-based biomarker identification and prediction;
● Deep learning for tumor segmentation and quantification;
● Integration of multi-modal data for improved cancer diagnosis and treatment.
keywords: ["Deep learning", "cancer detection", "prognosis", "prediction", "treatment"]

topic: Topic: Chiral Nanostructures: Supramolecular Self-assembly and Materials
summary:
The subject of chirality in nanostructures represents one of the most dynamic areas of nanomaterials today and has emerged recently as a new "growth point" of nanoscience. Recent developments in a wide range of chiral nanomaterials, including inorganic, organic, polymer, and supramolecular nanomaterials, have attracted increasing research interest in the field of chemistry, physics, biology and materials, and nanoscience. The typical chiral nanostructures, such as helical nanofibers/nanorods, helical nanotubes, helicoids, etc., have been frequently reported. The chirality transfer from small molecules to impart handedness to nanoscale matter has been well-established. Moreover, chirality at the supramolecular level is of vital importance since it is strongly related to chemistry, physics, and biology and is attracting great attention due to rapid developments in supramolecular chemistry and molecular self-assembly. Despite the great achievements that have been made, the designed synthesis of chiral nanostructures with high uniformity and desirable functionality is still challenging, which is important for designing materials with specific applications, such as enantioselective catalysis, enantioselective recognition, CPL（Circularly polarized luminescence), and biomedical applications. The Special Issue is focused on recent progress in the broad interdisciplinary areas of chiral nanostructures or nanomaterials and their potential applications.    
Specific areas to be covered include, but are not limited to:

● Chiral inorganic/organic/polymer nanomaterials;

● Chiral supramolecular nanostructures;

● Chiral nanomaterials for CPL;

● Chiral nanomaterials for enantioselective catalysis;

● Chiral nanomaterials for enantioselective recognition;

● Chiral nanomaterials for biomedical applications.
keywords: ["Supramolecular nanostructures", "chiral nanomaterials", "enantioselective catalysis", "enantioselective recognition", "CPL"]

topic: Topic: Gut-Brain Axis in Neurodegenerative Disorders
summary: There is growing evidence that an altered gut microbiome, along with perturbations in the gut–brain axis, is implicated in the aetiology and progression of several neurodegenerative disorders, including neuropsychiatric diseases. The role of leaky gut and the involvement of LPS has received significant attention in this area. The interplay of gut neurotransmitters and endocrine signaling molecules with brain metabolomics and the blood-brain barrier has expanded our understanding and tentative interventions. This fascinating area offers ample opportunities to develop new hypotheses and conduct in vitro/in vivo testing. Thus, we welcome both original research and reviews.
keywords: ["Neurodegenerative diseases", "gut microbiota", "gut-brain metabolomics", "blood-brain barrier", "gut permeability", "probiotics", "metagenomics", "gut neurotransmitters", "functional foods", "metabolic syndrome", "cognitive decline", "sleep disorders"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Targeted Therapies and Predictive Biomarkers for Breast Cancer
summary:
Breast cancer represents the prototypical solid tumor where targets have been identified to enable a personalized approach in systemic therapy. Consequently, biomarkers assume a critical role in guiding precision-targeted treatments. Among the essential biomarkers for breast cancer (BC), estrogen and progesterone receptors have been utilized to select patients suitable for endocrine treatment. Moreover, HER2 status has been analyzed for identifying patients who may gain from antiHER2 therapy. Although methodological problems persist in the determination of predictive biomarkers, new emerging factors such as androgen receptor (AR) have been considered a potential therapeutic target in BC.

To expedite advancements in precision treatment for BC patients, it is imperative to explore additional predictive biomarkers, particularly those that enhance the positive predictive value for endocrine and antiHER2 therapies, as well as biomarkers that predict responses to specific forms of chemotherapy.
keywords: ["Biomarkers", "breast cancer", "immunohistochemistry", "hormone receptors", "HER2"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic:  The Biology and Therapeutic Utility of Circulating Tumor Cells (CTC)
summary: Circulating tumor cells (CTCs) comprise a rare and heterogeneous cell population that sheds from tumors and circulates through the peripheral bloodstream at various stages of the carcinogenic process, from early pre-malignant lesions to fatal metastasis. Over the last two decades, the concept of "Liquid Biopsy", which involves interrogating CTCs and other blood analytes (ctDNA, exosomes, microRNAs, etc.) for clinical application as precision medicine tools, has witnessed significant expansion. This growth is attributed to the initial implementation of liquid biopsy, which demonstrated the clinical utility of CTCs as independent prognostic indicators for the progression-free and overall survival of metastatic cancer patients. During this period, the field of "Liquid Biopsy" has experienced remarkable advancements. These include the development and commercialization of multiple platforms and tests, as well as increased knowledge regarding CTC biology, CTC subsets, and CTC biomarkers. Consequently, CTC analyses now employ enrichment platforms that capture CTCs using multiple parameters at single-cell level or identify CTCs based on the expression of cancer-specific biomarkers or the presence of CTC-specific transcripts. Collectively, CTC studies have opened new investigational avenues for detecting and prognosticating cancer progression and therapy responses. This Special Issue aims to cover the latest trends in novel CTC research and their clinical application ("Liquid Biopsy" ). We welcome the submission of Original Research, Review, Mini Review, Methods, Clinical Trial Perspective, and Opinion articles.
keywords: ["Circulating tumor cells (CTCs)", "CTC clusters", "CTC biomarkers", "clinical utility of CTC testing", "translational cancer research"]

source: Journal of Surveillance, Security and Safety
topic: Topic: Software Defined Networking: Security Issues and Challenges Ahead
summary:
Software-defined networking (SDN) is quickly approaching nearly two decades since its inception, at least as a named concept. While the term was most closely associated with the OpenFlow protocol, it is actually a broad concept and has been the source of a foundational transformation in how networks are built and managed. Behind it are three core concepts: 1) Centralized control and automation through APIs; 2) Programmable data planes - both hardware-based and software-based; and 3) Virtualization (covering both isolation and abstractions).

Each of these has led to a substantial impact in the field of networking. Modern data centers are fully software defined as a key technology that has enabled each of the cloud providers to scale and provide the services they have. Wide area networks have been transformed with SD-WAN technology, which is now the standard way to build a network for enterprises. And cellular infrastructure has fully adopted software-defined principles to enable rapid innovation and capabilities in 5G infrastructure.    

In this Special Issue, we focus on the specific impact regarding security, encompassing both the challenges and opportunities. With the advent of new technologies, we also encounter new security concerns. This is evident in the case of SDN, where vulnerabilities in protocols and control software have emerged. However, it's important to note that new technologies also bring about great opportunities. For instance, we can now reduce human errors through infrastructure as code and achieve better scalability with distributed firewalls. Furthermore, we are witnessing the integration of policy-driven approaches and security features directly into applications, as seen in the development of service meshes.

We are seeking innovative research and operational experience papers that cover either the new opportunities or challenges in security, with a focus on several technologies:

● Software-based programmability - e.g., DPDK, eBPF, Linux networking;

● Hardware-based programmability - P4, SmartNICs;

● Virtualization - e.g., NFV;

● Cloud Networking - e.g., abstractions by cloud providers or cloud models;

● Formal verification and programming languages for networks;

● Service Meshes - e.g., Linkerd, Istio;

● Gitops and Infrastructure as code - e.g., Terraform, Pulumi;

● Domains such as 5G, data centers, SD-WAN, edge computing;

● Network observability enabled by SDN.


If you have expertise in any of these areas, we would love to hear about your research and operational experiences. We encourage you to submit papers that explore the latest advancements, discuss security implications, and propose innovative solutions within these domains.
keywords: ["Software-defined networking (SDN)", "programmability", "virtualization", "security issues"]

topic: Topic: Complications and Management in Rhinoplasty
summary:
Rhinoplasty is among the most commonly performed facial cosmetic and reconstructive procedures, but it is also one of the most delicate and technically demanding. As a result, there are numerous possible acute, subacute, and chronic complications associated with rhinoplasty, which can lead to functional or cosmetic nasal deformities, patient dissatisfaction, the necessity for revision surgery, and even legal disputes. The various surgical approaches to the nose, including open versus closed techniques, each carry their own hypothetical spectrums of problems, and the risk of complications can vary based on several factors.

Before undertaking these procedures, the rhinoplasty surgeon must possess a thorough understanding of the potential complications and how to manage them. Informed consent should be comprehensive to establish reasonable expectations about outcomes and to ensure that patients are aware of the potential issues that may arise during the intraoperative and postoperative periods. Topics covered in this Special Issue include issues related to autologous and non-autologous grafts, nasal valve patency, the nasal septum, soft tissue complications, edema, and impairment of the structural integrity of the nose. Complications and management of non-surgical rhinoplasty are beyond the scope of this Special Issue and will not be discussed.
keywords: ["Rhinoplasty", "complications", "nasal valve", "nasal deformity", "revision surgery"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Modulation of the Tumor Microenvironment by Natural Agents
summary: The Special Issue titled "Modulation of the Tumor Microenvironment by Natural Agents" offers a comprehensive exploration of the dynamic interplay between natural agents and the tumor microenvironment (TME) and their potential as effective therapeutic interventions for cancer. The collection of articles within this issue delves into the diverse array of natural agents, including phytochemicals, probiotics, and bioactive compounds from plants and algae, highlighting their multifaceted roles in shaping the TME. These agents exhibit promising immunomodulatory effects by enhancing immune cell responses, manipulating immune checkpoints, and modulating cytokine networks within the TME. Additionally, this Special Issue examines their ability to disrupt angiogenesis and metabolic reprogramming, thereby impeding cancer cell growth and survival. Moreover, it addresses the crucial role of natural agents in sensitizing cancer cells to conventional therapies, potentially overcoming treatment resistance. In summary, this Special Issue presents a wealth of insights and strategies for harnessing the therapeutic potential of natural agents in the fight against cancer, offering hope for more effective and less toxic treatment approaches.
keywords: ["Tumor microenvironment (TME)", "natural agents", "immunomodulation", "angiogenesis", "metabolic reprogramming", "cancer therapy", "treatment", "resistance", "sensitization", "immunotherapy", "clinical translation"]

topic: Topic: New Advances in the Seismic Analysis and Design of Reinforced Concrete Structures
summary:
The purpose of this Special Issue is to provide modern analysis and design methodologies for new reinforced concrete buildings and structures under strong earthquakes. Additionally, it examines new advances for the rehabilitation and strengthening of existing reinforced concrete structures. The rapid international developments in these fields over the past 30 years have been extensively documented in numerous publications. These developments have significantly influenced the regulations of the latest seismic codes and are increasingly being applied in practice. However, a systematic and in-depth investigation is required for their correct implementation.

Recent strong earthquakes worldwide prove that many existing buildings are unsafe. However, with advancements in science and technology, it is now possible to evaluate the behavior of existing reinforced concrete structures during earthquakes in terms of damage progression and potential collapse. New materials, analysis methods, and experience in the application of innovative construction techniques greatly contribute to solving the problem of the seismic resistance of reinforced concrete buildings and structures.

The seismic design of new reinforced concrete structures or the rehabilitation and strengthening of existing ones require more than a simple application of the regulations. These are complex processes that demand deeper knowledge, experience, and imagination. This Special Issue aims to outline the most recent advances in the field of seismic analysis and design of reinforced concrete structures. While the following topics are suggested, the submitted papers are not limited to these subjects:

● Alternative and innovative technologies;

● Novel analysis, design, and practical approaches;

● Experimental and computational modeling of seismic effects on reinforced concrete structures;

● Reinforced concrete structures under extreme conditions;

● Rehabilitation or strengthening of existing structures;

● Seismic code provisions: review, improvement, and assessment.
keywords: ["Performance-based seismic design", "reinforced concrete structures", "modeling of reinforced concrete members", "damage assessment", "rehabilitation or strengthening of existing structures"]

topic: Topic: Coal-related Resources and Their Recovery and Applications
summary:
Coal, possessing vast reserves as a fossil energy source, has historically played a pivotal role in electricity generation and the iron/steel manufacturing sector. Nevertheless, the detrimental environmental consequences stemming from carbon emissions have catalyzed a shift away from coal-generated electricity. This transition is driven by a growing preference for cleaner and more sustainable energy alternatives. Concurrently, the iron and steel industry are witnessing a transformation as it leans towards greener reducing agents and fuels, gradually supplanting coal's dominance in this field. However, beyond its conventional roles as a fuel source and reducing agent, coal possesses a spectrum of untapped properties that can be harnessed for generating economic benefits. Additionally, numerous individuals and communities rely on coal as a vital means of sustenance. Therefore, the need to use coal in sustainable manners is becoming more and more important. The primary aim of this Special Issue is to assemble top-tier studies that make significant contributions to the advancement of this field. The subareas that will be discussed in this Special Issue include:

1. New findings or techniques for recognizing coal organic structure and inorganic components;

2. Advanced coal preparation and cleaning technologies;

3. Critical mineral recovery from coal and coal-related resources;

4. Graphite and other high-value carbon product generation from coal;

5. Other aspects related to the new utilizations of coal and coal-related resources.


This Special Issue welcomes original research articles, short communications, and review articles.
keywords: ["Advanced coal preparation and cleaning", "advanced coal utilization", "critical minerals recovery"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Application of Nanomaterials in Tumor Therapy
summary:
Cancer is one of the most serious health challenges globally, and traditional treatment methods such as surgery, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy, while offering some relief to patients, still face serious side effects and limitations in treatment effectiveness. The incidence of cancer is continuously rising worldwide, posing a significant public health challenge. Despite advancements in medical technology, there is a need to strengthen early screening, precision treatment, and comprehensive preventive measures to alleviate the burden on cancer patients and improve treatment outcomes.

In the rapidly developing field of medicine today, nanotechnology has emerged as a remarkable frontier technology. The application of nanomaterials has brought about a series of exciting breakthroughs in cancer treatment. Nanomaterials, owing to their unique size effects and surface properties, are playing an increasingly crucial role in the field of oncology. Their application in cancer treatment encompasses nanodrug delivery systems, hyperthermia therapy, photodynamic therapy, and their combination with immunotherapy.

The continuous innovation and development of nanotechnology will provide cancer patients with more precise, efficient, and safer treatment options, opening up new prospects in cancer therapy. However, it is important to note that the application of nanomaterials in cancer treatment is still in the research and development stage, necessitating large-scale clinical trials and safety assessments to ensure efficacy and safety. Additionally, different types of tumors may respond differently and exhibit varied adaptability to nanomaterial treatments, thus requiring personalized therapeutic strategies for different tumor types. As technology continues to evolve, the application of nanomaterials in cancer treatment holds the promise of bringing cancer patients more treatment choices and hope.
keywords: ["Nanomaterials", "cancer treatment", "nanodrug delivery systems", "hyperthermia therapy", "photodynamic therapy", "chemodynamic therapy", "sonodynamic therapy", "immunotherapy"]

topic: Topic: Breast Reconstruction with Autologous Tissue Transfer - Advantages and Limitations
summary: Breast reconstruction has made great strides in the last two decades. Autologous reconstruction is more complex than implant-based options and requires a surgeon with specialized training but is often associated with superior long-term patient satisfaction. Though autologous reconstruction is in many ways considered a gold standard reconstruction, its intricacies warrant thorough investigation. Both patient-related and surgeon-related factors play a role in the success of these operations. This Special Issue focuses on providing our readership with up-to-date information on the advantages and limitations of autologous tissue transfer to the breast. The goals are to explore advancements in oncoplastic planning, timing of reconstruction, flap selection, and considerations for optimizing aesthetics at both donor and recipient sites.
keywords: ["Breast reconstruction", "autologous tissue transfer", "outcomes", "microsurgery"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Pro-oxidative Stressors Impacting Cancer Growth and Efficacy of Therapeutic Agents
summary: Humans are exposed to various ubiquitous pro-oxidative stressors, encompassing environmental carcinogens, ultraviolet radiation (UVR), pollutants such as cigarette smoke, and even therapeutic agents such as chemotherapy, radiation therapy, photodynamic therapy, and targeted therapy. These pro-oxidative stressors, by inciting the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), modulate host immune responses and predispose individuals to systemic immunosuppression. This immunosuppression stands as a critical factor,  not only fostering the growth of pre-existing cancer but also impacting the efficacy of therapeutic agents. This impact occurs through mechanisms that give rise to the development of tumor resistance mechanisms. Drawing upon your expertise in related fields, we are pleased to invite you to contribute to a Special Issue topic on “Pro-oxidative Stressors Impacting Cancer Growth and Efficacy of Therapeutic Agents”. This Special Issue aims to publish original research or reviews articles on the roles and mechanisms of a wide range of pro-oxidative stressors, including but not limited to the ones mentioned above, as they relate to impacting tumor microenvironment, cancer growth, and efficacy of therapeutic agents, as well as approaches to mitigate such effects and improve the effectiveness of anti-cancer agents. Importantly, in this Special Issue, the research areas may include, among other topics: investigations into combined chemotherapeutic agents, repurposed drugs, dietary phytochemicals, and targeted therapies utilizing various cancer models.
keywords: ["Pro-oxidative stressors", "cell signaling pathways", "cancer growth and metastasis", "tumor resistance mechanisms", "chemotherapeutic agents", "repurposed drugs", "dietary phytochemicals", "targeted therapy"]

source: Journal of Surveillance, Security and Safety
topic: Topic:  Cybersecurity of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles
summary:
In light of the rapid advancements in information technology and autonomous driving, the utilization of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) has witnessed a remarkable expansion across military, civil, industrial, and consumer domains. Applications such as aerial photography, agricultural precision, express logistics, and remote sensing have showcased the versatile potential of UAVs, further underscoring their market prospects.

This Special Issue aims to comprehensively address the escalating cybersecurity challenges pertinent to UAVs. These challenges predominantly encompass vulnerabilities within both their software and hardware components. Flight control software, communication protocols, sensors, and operating systems are prime examples of susceptible areas. The prevalent utilization of generic chips, open-source operating systems, universal protocols, and cost-effective software architectures, while ensuring user-friendliness and affordability, frequently inadvertently overlooks the critical aspect of security. Consequently, UAVs become prone to exploitable security gaps, rendering them susceptible to malicious interventions. Such vulnerabilities pose a substantial risk to both the integrity of UAV operations and the overall progress of the UAV industry. Therefore, it is of paramount importance to proactively identify and counteract potential threats that could compromise UAV security.

This Special Issue invites researchers and practitioners to contribute their insights and expertise on a diverse array of topics related to UAV security. Some of the key areas this Special Issue will cover include:
● Software and Hardware Vulnerability Analysis: In-depth exploration of vulnerabilities within flight control software, communication protocols, sensors, and operating systems that could be exploited by malicious actors;
● Threat Detection Techniques: Novel methodologies for detecting potential cybersecurity threats, leveraging advanced analytics, artificial intelligence, machine learning, and anomaly detection;
● Security-enhanced Architectures: Design and implementation of robust UAV architectures that integrate security at every level, mitigating risks arising from common chips, open-source systems, and universal protocols;
● Encryption and Data Protection: Strategies for ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of UAV data during transmission and storage;
● Intrusion Detection and Prevention: Development of intrusion detection systems and real-time threat response mechanisms to safeguard UAV operations from unauthorized access;
● Case Studies and Practical Implementations: Real-world case studies, experiences, and best practices in fortifying UAV security within various sectors, including military, civil, industrial, and consumer applications.

We invite researchers, academics, and industry experts to contribute their original research, reviews, and case studies that advance the understanding and enhancement of UAV security. By addressing these critical issues, we endeavor to foster a safer and more resilient environment for the widespread and sustainable deployment of UAV technology.
keywords: ["Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs)", "threat detection techniques", "security issues"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Study on Novel Epigenetics-based Biomarkers for Cancers
summary: The development of cancer and response to cancer treatment are complex phenotypes influenced by various genetic and non-genetic factors. Epigenetic mechanisms, such as DNA methylation, DNA hydroxymethylation, microRNAs, and histone modifications, hold pivotal roles in determining gene expression in cells, which in turn affect the manifestation of complex diseases and traits. Technical advances during the past decades have facilitated the exploration of genome-wide epigenetic modifications and features with unprecedented efficiency across all kinds of biospecimens (e.g., cell lines, fresh tissues, and cell-free DNA). Researchers have adeptly harnessed these advances to make significant strides in discovering biomarkers targeting epigenetic modifications and features. However, challenges persist, and unmet clinical needs remain to be addressed in this field. Cancer remains a substantial healthcare challenge, underscoring the importance of identifying more effective and clinically feasible/convenient biomarkers for cancer diagnosis, prognosis, and early detection. Such breakthroughs have the potential to revolutionize clinical approaches by enabling the early detection of patients with curable tumors and the identification of patients who may benefit from a particular treatment. In this Special Issue, we invite submissions of research articles, research communications, opinions, and mini-reviews exploring the realm of epigenetics-based biomarkers for cancer. We are keen to encompass a spectrum of perspectives, from clinical studies to fundamental research investigations.
keywords: ["Epigenetics", "biomarker", "diagnosis", "prognosis", "early detection", "treatment response"]

topic: Topic: Advances in Research on Skin Metabolism
summary: xx

topic: Topic: Synthesis and Preparation of Novel Porous Organic Materials
summary:
Porous organic materials portray a family of pioneering materials with intricately porous architectures, configurational diversity, and an alterable pore environment. By meticulously designing diverse secondary building blocks and implementing different chemical bonds, a wide array of porous organic materials can be synthesized. The versatility in structures and functionality enables porous organic materials to find a myriad of energy, environmental, and biomedical applications.

Under the guidance of chemical bonding theory, the precise construction of multi-dimensional, multi-level, and multi-scale porous organic materials can be accomplished through the effective adoption of theoretical design in conjunction with experimental approaches. An in-depth study of the thermodynamic and kinetic processes involved in the synthesis and growth of porous organic materials enables the formation of linkages at the atomic scale, precise directional synthesis, preparation, and tailoring of multi-dimensional, porous framework structures. Additionally, it enables the efficient large-scale production of such materials. The novel mechanisms of chemical reactions between synthesis components are systematically analyzed to furnish experimental techniques and fundamental insights for the discovery of new types of porous organic materials. The structure-function interrelationship between the microstructure (atoms, molecules, ions, pore structures, functional groups, etc.) and properties (optical, electrical, magnetic, adsorption, separation, mass transfer, catalytic, etc.) of porous organic materials is disclosed, with a focus on application domains such as gas storage and separation, energy storage and conversion, industrial catalysis, and sensing, enabling effective control of material performance.

The synthesis and preparation of porous organic materials constitute a realm full of challenges and prospects. With continuous breakthroughs and innovations in synthetic techniques, it is believed that this fascinating research field will usher in more significant advancements in materials science and applications.

The following topics will be discussed in this Special Issue:

● Porous organic polymers;

● Hyper-crosslinked polymers;

● Conjugated micropore polymers;

● Porous aromatic frameworks;

● Covalent organic frameworks;

● Hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks;

● Crystalline porous organic salts;

● Porous organic cages;

● Porous liquids;

● Novel synthetic methods and strategies;

● Reaction thermodynamics and kinetics;

● Large-scale preparation.
keywords: ["Porous organic materials", "synthetic methods and strategies", "reaction thermodynamics and kinetics", "large-scale preparation", "structure-function relationship"]

topic: Topic: Recent Advances in Organoid, Organ-on-chip and Microphysiological Systems
summary:
A powerful in vitro model plays an essential role in speeding up drug development and the lack of such models can hamper the discovery of drugs for critical diseases. Advanced in vitro models, such as organoids, organ-on-chip systems, and microphysiological systems, are gaining increasing popularity in the realm of drug discovery. Conventional in vitro models, including cell lines, primary cells, and ex vivo human tissues, hold inherent drawbacks. Accumulating evidence indicates that cell lines are vulnerable to mutations over prolonged culture periods, potentially leading to inconsistent results across different labs using the same cell line. Furthermore, in vitro models containing single cell types fall short of mimicking the complexity of in vivo organs and tissues that are typically composed of multiple cell types. Ethical considerations and resource constraints make it difficult to use human tissues as ex vivo models. Organoids are three-dimensional models harboring organ-like structures comprising various cell types derived from a renewable stem cell source. The organ-on-a-chip model is based on microfluidic devices built using a combination of cell biology, engineering, and biomaterial technology. The microenvironment of the chip mimics that of the organ in terms of tissue interfaces, fluid flow, and mechanical stimulation. These complicated models are considered to be more translatable to in vivo physiology compared to traditional in vitro models including cell line and primary cells. Thus, Organoids, organ-on-chip systems, and microphysiological systems are promising in vitro models for drug discovery. As such, we extend an invitation for valuable contributions that align with the One Health concept. Research articles related to advanced in vitro models, organoids, organ-on-chip models, and microphysiological systems within our topic are all welcome.

We welcome submissions on the selected topics and beyond. The Special Issue covers the following topics, relevant but not limited to:
1. Establishment of new organoid, organ-on-chip, and other three-dimensional models;

2. Gene manipulation within organoid, organ-on-chip, and other three-dimensional models;

3. Disease modeling employing organoid, organ-on-chip, and other three-dimensional models;

4. Drug screening using organoid, organ-on-chip, and other three-dimensional models;

5. Personalized medicine leveraging organ-on-chip and other three-dimensional models;

6. Regeneration and development studies using organ-on-chip and other three-dimensional models;

7. Transcriptomics, proteomics, and other omics studies utilizing organ-on-chip and other three-dimensional models;

8. Mechanistic studies using organ-on-chip and other three-dimensional models.
keywords: ["Organoid", "organ-on-chip", "MPS", "in vitro model", "3D model"]

topic: Topic: Zeolites for Sustainable Catalysis
summary:
Owing to their significant advantages, including high stability, uniform micropores, rigid framework, and strong acidity, zeolite catalysts have been extensively used in multiple important reactions within the petrochemical industry, such as catalytic cracking, isomerization, and aromatization. With the development of non-petroleum chemical reaction processes, zeolite-related catalysts also exhibited unusual catalytic performances in utilizing coal, natural gas, shale gas, and biomass resources. More recently, the conversion of CO2 and plastic wastes has become hot fields that strongly rely on the zeolite catalysts. It is becoming clear that the improvement in these processes will be mainly based on the design and synthesis of improved zeolite materials with controlled morphology, porosity, acidity, and synergism with different metal/oxide catalysts. Indeed, these new reaction processes also efficiently drive the development of eco-friendly zeolite material synthesis techniques.


Specific areas to be covered include, but are not limited to:

● Zeolite synthesis;

● Zeolite catalysts for methane conversion;

● Zeolite catalysts for syngas conversion;

● Zeolite catalysts for CO2 adsorption/conversion;

● Zeolite catalysts for plastic waste upcycling;

● Zeolite catalysts for dehydrogenation;

● Zeolite catalysts for biomass conversion;

● Zeolite catalysts for oxidation;

● Characterization techniques for zeolite catalysts.
keywords: ["Zeolite", "synthesis", "catalysis", "shape selectivity", "green chemistry"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Diagnosis and Treatment of Bladder Cancer
summary:
World-wide, bladder cancer (BC) ranks tenth in terms of incidence and is increasing at a steady rate. It is a heterogeneous disease with widely varying prognoses. Although several attempts are being made to improve understanding of these types of tumors in order to reduce or eliminate the risk of recurrence and progression, bladder cancer remains one of the most expensive cancers to treat during one's lifetime. The high recurrence rate, repeated surgeries, and long-term cystoscopy monitoring and treatment make an extra effort necessary. The diagnosis and prediction of prognosis for BC can be challenging because of the subjective nature of pathological evaluation. Researchers are therefore invited to present the latest advancements in bladder cancer research, from basic research to clinical application.

There are a number of potential topics to consider in this Special Issue, including but not limited to:
1. Novel agents for unresponsive patients to classical-based chemotherapy;
2. Novel technologies or clinical applications related to monitoring of recurrence;
3. Minimally invasive diagnostic approaches;
4. Development of the novel drugs or strategies targeted to treat bladder cancer;
5. Patients' stratification methods;
6. Urinary and peripheral blood biomarkers;
7. Surgical treatment: from the endoscopy to the robotic radical cystectomy;
