topic: Topic: Machine Learning for Materials Development and State Prediction in Lithium-ion Batteries
summary:
We are pleased to announce the Special Issue on "Machine Learning for Materials Development and State Prediction in Lithium-ion Batteries" in the Journal of Materials Informatics (JMI, Online ISSN 2770-372X). This issue will be overseen by Professor Tong-Yi Zhang as Editor-in-Chief and Professor Xingjun Liu as Executive Editor, with a distinguished Editorial Board including Professor Tong-Yi Zhang, Professor Xingjun Liu, Professor Huajian Gao, Professor Gerbrand Ceder, Professor Li-Quan Chen, and Professor Dongying Ju.

This Special Issue will highlight cutting-edge research that applies machine learning (ML) methodologies to critical challenges in developing lithium-ion battery (LIB) materials and monitoring LIB conditions. It will showcase the latest advancements in predictive modeling, data-driven optimization, and real-time monitoring of LIBs. Contributions will come from materials science, electrochemistry, data science, and ML, focusing on novel ML algorithms, computational models, and experimental techniques that enhance battery materials and provide accurate battery health and lifespan predictions under various operating conditions.

Specific topics of interest include, but are not limited to, the following:
● Application of ML algorithms to discover new LIB materials with improved properties;
● Development of ML models to predict the capacity, efficiency, and cycle life of LIBs;
● Utilization of ML for early detection of performance degradation and fault diagnosis;
● Advanced ML approaches for real-time estimation of State of Charge (SOC) and State of Health (SOH) in LIBs;
● Implementation of ML in developing intelligent battery management systems (BMS) for improved efficiency and safety;
● Use of big data analytics for processing and interpreting large datasets from LIB experiments and simulations;
● Combining experimental data with computational ML models for better accuracy and validation;
● Application of ML to model and predict diffusion-induced stress or plastic deformation in LIB electrodes.

We anticipate receiving your contributions to this exciting Special Issue.


Sincerely,

Guest Editors
Prof. Dr. Fuqian Yang

Prof. Dr. Yong Li
keywords: ["Machine learning", "materials development", "state prediction", "computational methods", "diffusion-induced stress"]

topic: Topic: Reliability Optimization and Intelligent Maintenance in Multi-State Systems
summary:
We are pleased to present this Special Issue of Complex Engineering Systems focusing on "Reliability Optimization and Intelligent Maintenance in Multi-state Systems". The growing complexity of modern engineering systems and the demand for higher reliability and cost-effective maintenance has led to a burgeoning interest in developing advanced methodologies and techniques for optimizing reliability and implementing intelligent maintenance strategies in multi-state systems. This Special Issue provides a platform for researchers and practitioners to disseminate their latest findings, innovations, and best practices in this vital area.

This Special Issue welcomes original research articles, review papers, and case studies addressing various subjects related to reliability optimization and intelligent maintenance in multi-state systems. Contributions span diverse application domains, including but not limited to mechanical and electrical systems, aerospace and automotive engineering, power generation and distribution, manufacturing processes, and infrastructure systems. The articles cover a broad spectrum of topics, including:
● Development of novel multi-state modeling techniques for capturing system degradation and performance variability;
● Optimization approaches for enhancing the reliability and availability of multi-state systems;
● Integration of advanced data analytics and machine learning for intelligent condition monitoring and prognostics;
● Decision support systems and maintenance scheduling strategies for optimizing maintenance actions in multi-state systems;
● Case studies and practical applications demonstrating the effectiveness of reliability optimization and intelligent maintenance methodologies in real-world engineering systems.

We believe that this Special Issue will be a valuable resource for researchers, engineers, and industry professionals seeking to stay abreast of the latest developments in reliability optimization and intelligent maintenance in multi-state systems. We extend our sincere gratitude to all the authors, reviewers, and editorial staff who contributed to it.
keywords: ["Reliability modeling", "multi-state systems", "operations management", "maintenance optimization", "safety analysis"]

topic: Topic: Sustainability and Building Energy Efficiency Research for A Low-Carbon City's Future
summary:
Building energy efficiency and decarbonization are regarded as key points for mitigating global climate change. According to the International Energy Agency (IEA), building-related greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions accounted for 21% of global GHG emissions in 2019. With the urbanization process and population growth, especially in developing countries, the final energy demand and carbon emissions are likely to increase steadily. Therefore, it is urgent to promote a clean transition to achieve sustainable and low-carbon buildings.

However, improving building energy efficiency and achieving decarbonization face many challenges, requiring interdisciplinary solutions from engineering and social sciences. Technically, this involves promoting passive building design, electrification, and high-efficiency electrical equipment (e.g., LEDs, heat pumps, and thermal and ice storage technologies), as well as digital and intelligent management systems, and onsite utilization of renewable energy (e.g., photovoltaic, solar thermal, and geothermal energy). Regarding market policies and occupants’ behaviors, it is essential to establish a reasonable energy pricing mechanism (e.g., tiered electricity pricing and demand response), low-carbon technology subsidy mechanisms (such as energy retrofits for existing buildings and high-efficiency equipment), and energy performance contracting. These strategies will require advanced modeling, policy, and financing approaches.

Therefore, this Special Issue primarily focuses on the challenges and obstacles to enhancing building energy efficiency and decarbonization, including but not limited to the following:

● Building energy efficiency and decarbonization pathways and policies.

● Financial mechanisms for promoting building energy efficacy techniques;

● Advanced and typical cases of building energy efficiency;

● Building electrification and onsite utilization of renewable energy;

● Green and low-carbon building design;

● Life Cycle Assessment of green buildings;

● Technology and Evaluation Method of green building and building retrofitting.
keywords: ["Building energy efficiency", "building carbon emission reduction", "low-carbon city", "green and sustainable buildings"]

source: Journal of Surveillance, Security and Safety
topic: Topic: Data-driven Systems and Applications in Digital Investigations, Analytics, and Security
summary:
The digital landscape is constantly evolving, generating ever-increasing volumes of data. This data deluge presents both challenges and opportunities for digital investigations, analytics, and security. Effectively managing and analyzing this data is crucial for uncovering hidden patterns, identifying threats, and conducting thorough investigations. However, traditional methods are often overwhelmed by the sheer scale and complexity of digital data. Consequently, integrating data-driven systems in these fields necessitates the exploration of innovative methodologies, technologies, and applications. This Special Issue aims to bring together cutting-edge research and advancements that address the complexities and potentials of data-driven systems in enhancing digital investigations, analytics, and security measures.

We invite high-quality submissions that present original research, case studies, and review articles related to developing, implementing, and evaluating data-driven techniques and tools in these domains. Topics of interest include, but are not limited to:
● Data-driven forensics: Techniques and tools for acquiring, preserving, analyzing, and interpreting digital evidence using data analytics methods. Advanced forensic techniques and tools. Data mining and machine learning for digital forensics;
● Cybersecurity analytics: Machine learning, artificial intelligence, and other advanced analytics techniques for threat detection, incident response, and risk management. Case studies on digital investigation methodologies;
● Big data for digital investigations: Methods and frameworks for processing, storing, and analyzing large datasets in the context of digital investigations. Big data analytics in cybercrime investigations;
● Network traffic analysis: Leveraging network data for anomaly detection, intrusion prevention, and attribution of cyberattacks;
● Social media analytics for investigations: Utilizing social media data to identify suspects, track criminal activity, and gather intelligence;
● Data visualization for security: Techniques for presenting complex security data in a clear and actionable way;
● Privacy-preserving data analytics: Methods for analyzing digital data while ensuring data privacy and compliance with regulations;
● Emerging applications of data-driven approaches: Novel applications of data analytics in digital investigations, analytics, and security.
keywords: ["Data-driven forensics", "cybersecurity", "privacy-preserving", "investigations", "analytics"]

topic: Topic: Machine Learning for Thermoelectric Materials
summary:
Thermoelectric (TE) materials can directly convert energy between heat and electricity and have been widely applied in power generation and refrigeration. Their performance could be quantified by the figure of merit, ZT = S2σT/κ, where S, κ, σ, and T represent the Seebeck coefficient, thermal conductivity, electrical conductivity, and absolute temperature, respectively. S2σ is the power factor, and thermal conductivity κ comprises the carriers (κe) and lattice (κL) contributions. Generally, the complex coupling among these coefficients, such as S, κ, and σ, hinders significant improvements of TE performance in materials.

Throughout the centuries-long history of human scientific development, three research paradigms have emerged: experimental, theoretical, and computational. However, in complex systems such as biology, astronomy, and materials, the intricate interactions and numerous variables greatly limit the effectiveness of theoretical and computational research models. This limitation necessitates combining big data and artificial intelligence (AI), known as the “fourth paradigm”. With the advancement of big data and AI technologies, the material genome project research, characterized by high-throughput experiments, high-throughput computing, and AI big data analysis, has shown astonishing advantages in various materials fields. The fourth paradigm of materials science relies on machine learning to generate and process massive amounts of data, thus receiving growing attention. Its application in the research and development of new TE materials is expected to greatly accelerate its application and enhance our understanding of the fundamental mechanisms of the electronic and phonon transport properties in materials.

This Special Issue aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the most recent advancements, challenges, and future directions in TE materials, emphasizing the application of machine learning methods.

It will feature original research articles, reviews, and perspectives from leading experts in TE materials and machine learning. Topics covered in this Special Issue include, but are not limited to:
1. Development of high throughput experimental/computational methods for establishing high-fidelity databases of TE materials;
2. Machine learning-based approaches for the design and optimization of TE materials;
3. Application of machine learning or machine learning-assisted simulations in predicting mechanical, heat transport, and electronic transport in TE materials;
4. Advanced machine learning techniques for characterizing the structure, defects, electrical, and heat transport properties of TE materials and for optimizing the manufacturing and service processes for TE devices.
keywords: ["Thermoelectric materials", "computational methods", "machine learning", "high throughput experimental"]

topic: Topic: Sustainable Flotation Reagents : Synthesis, Characterization, Adsorption Simulation and Modeling, and Performance Testing
summary: This Special Issue invites you to explore the synthesis, characterization, adsorption simulation, and testing of sustainable flotation reagents. We call on researchers and industry experts to contribute their latest findings and innovative approaches to this crucial field. From novel synthesis methods to advanced characterization techniques, we seek diverse perspectives to address sustainability challenges in flotation processes. Papers covering topics such as green synthesis routes, biodegradable reagents, and eco-friendly adsorption simulations are encouraged. Additionally, contributions discussing the practical applicability and performance of sustainable reagents in flotation processes are highly valued. Join us in advancing sustainable practices in flotation technology and shaping the future of environmentally conscious mining and mineral processing. Submit your papers and be part of this essential conversation.
keywords: ["Froth flotation", "synthesis", "green reagents", "collectors", "depressants", "frothers", "adsorption", "modeling and simulation", "flotation tests", "solution chemistry", "mineral-liquid interface", "selectivity"]

topic: Topic: Microplastics and Nanoplastics: Determination and Characterization, Toxic Studies, Impact on Human Health and Ecosystem
summary:
Microplastics and nanoplastics persist in the environment and can absorb and transport hazardous chemicals, raising significant concerns. Determining and characterizing these particles are crucial for understanding their distribution, sources, and pathways. Advanced analytical techniques are essential to accurately detect and quantify microplastics and nanoplastics, which vary widely in polymeric composition, size, and shape. Toxicological studies have begun to uncover the harmful effects of these particles on various organisms, indicating potential risks such as physical harm from ingestion and chemical toxicity from associated pollutants.

The impact on human health is an emerging area of concern. Humans are exposed to microplastics through food, water, and air, but the long-term health effects remain largely unknown. Preliminary research suggests potential links to inflammatory responses, oxidative stress, and other health issues. The broader ecological impacts are also alarming, as microplastics can disrupt food chains, harm wildlife, and alter habitats. Understanding these impacts is critical for developing strategies to mitigate the environmental and health risks associated with microplastics and nanoplastics.

The Special Issue titled "Microplastics and Nanoplastics: Determination and Characterization, Toxic Studies, Impact on Human Health and Ecosystem" delves into these burgeoning concerns. This collection of research focuses on advanced methods for detecting and characterizing these tiny plastic particles, highlighting the latest technological advancements and analytical techniques. It also examines the toxicological impacts of microplastics and nanoplastics on various organisms, including their potential health risks to humans. The issue further explores the broader ecological consequences, emphasizing the pervasive nature of these pollutants and their implications for environmental sustainability. Through a multidisciplinary approach, the Special Issue aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the sources, pathways, and effects of microplastics and nanoplastics, offering valuable insights for mitigating their impact on both human health and the ecosystem.
keywords: ["Microplastics and nanoplastics", "determination and characterization", "toxicity studies", "impact on human health", "impact on ecosystems"]

topic: Topic: Exposure to E-wastes and Its Effects on Humans and the Food Chain
summary:
E-waste, generated from the manufacture, use, recycling, and disposal of electrical and electronic goods, is the fastest-growing solid waste stream in the world. In 2019, less than 20% of the >50 million tonnes of e-waste produced globally was formally recorded. As a physically and chemically diverse material, e-waste contains numerous potentially harmful elements and compounds at levels that pose significant risks to human health and the food chain, particularly in regions with weak management practices and dominated by the informal waste sector. Women and children are particularly vulnerable.

We aim to provide a platform to present and review the latest research on exposure risks at all stages of the life cycle of electronic devices and their component materials. This includes informal treatment, extraction of critical materials, exposure assessment, and impacts of component substances on humans and other living organisms. We welcome fundamental empirical studies as well as stakeholder and policy-relevant commentary related to potential exposure (both increases and reductions, along with confounding factors).
keywords: ["Potentially toxic elements", "harmful substances", "e-waste", "WEEE", "informal waste management", "bioaccumulation", "pollutant transport", "exposure assessment", "source-pathway-receptor"]

topic: Topic: The Green Catalytic Conversion and Control Principles and Technologies of PM2.5 Precursors
summary: In recent years, the compound air pollution characterized by PM2.5 has seriously affected human life and production. PM2.5 contains numerous toxic and harmful substances that seriously jeopardize human health and atmospheric environmental quality. Therefore, developing control principles and technologies for the green catalytic conversion of PM2.5 precursors is pressing for sustainable development and environmental protection. This Special Issue focuses on “The Green Catalytic Conversion and Control Principles and Technologies of PM2.5 Precursors”, featuring the state-of-the-art green catalytic conversion and control principle and technology of PM2.5 precursors. We welcome Reviews and Research Articles related to the most relevant results about the conversion and control technologies (catalytic oxidation, photocatalysis, photothermal catalysis, electrocatalysis, etc.) of CO2 and PM2.5 precursors, such as oxynitride (NOx), volatile organic compounds (VOCs), etc.
keywords: ["Green catalytic conversion", "PM2.5 precursors control", "NO<sub>x</sub>", "Volatile organic compounds", "CO<sub>2</sub>", "catalytic oxidation", "photocatalysis", "photothermal catalysis", "electrocatalysis"]

topic: Topic: Laser Manufacturing Processes Development, Industrial Application and Challenges
summary:
The Special Issue "Laser Manufacturing Processes: Development, Industrial Application, and Challenges" offers a comprehensive overview of the latest advancements and applications of laser technology in manufacturing. It covers various laser manufacturing processes, including laser cutting, welding, engraving, additive manufacturing, surface treatment, micromachining, and cladding, focusing on the technical development and optimization of these processes. Innovations that enhance precision, efficiency, and material versatility are highlighted.

Key industrial applications are explored, demonstrating the significant impact of laser technology on sectors such as automotive, aerospace, medical devices, and electronics. The Special Issue also addresses the integration of laser systems into production lines, emphasizing the importance of automation and digitalization in modern manufacturing. Challenges associated with laser manufacturing, including material properties, process control, and the need for specialized knowledge and training, are critically examined. The importance of overcoming these challenges to fully leverage the potential of laser technology in industrial settings is underscored.

This Special Issue serves as an essential resource for researchers, engineers, and industry professionals, offering insights into the state-of-the-art in laser manufacturing and guiding future developments in this dynamic field.
keywords: ["Laser manufacturing", "development and optimization", "laser technology", "production line"]

topic: Topic: Green Supply Chain Management Model and Manufacturing Practices
summary:
In recent years, businesses across industries have faced increasing pressure from consumers, regulators, and stakeholders to minimize their environmental footprint. This has prompted a shift towards adopting green supply chain management principles and practices, which aim to reduce waste, conserve resources, and mitigate environmental impacts throughout the entire product lifecycle.

The Special Issue "Green Supply Chain Management Model and Manufacturing Practices" delves into the intersection of sustainability and supply chain management within the manufacturing sector. It comprehensively explores strategies, models, and practices to reduce environmental impact while enhancing operational efficiency and competitiveness. Topics covered include green sourcing, eco-design, reverse logistics, carbon footprint reduction, etc. Contributors examine innovative approaches to integrating environmental considerations into supply chain decision-making processes, such as life cycle assessment, environmental management systems, and green procurement practices. This Special Issue also highlights case studies and empirical research that showcase successful implementations of green supply chain management practices across various industries. Additionally, it addresses challenges and barriers to adoption, such as regulatory compliance, stakeholder engagement, and the need for collaboration throughout the supply chain.

Overall, this Special Issue provides valuable insights and guidance for researchers, practitioners, and policymakers seeking to promote sustainability in manufacturing through green supply chain management principles and practices.
keywords: ["Green supply chain", "model", "manufacturing practices", "decision-making processes"]

topic: Topic: Biomimetic Catalytic Materials
summary:
Enzymes, nature's masterful catalysts, have long inspired chemists seeking to design efficient, selective, and sustainable synthetic processes. Supramolecular enzyme-mimetic materials represent an exciting convergence of supramolecular chemistry and biomimetic design, offering a powerful platform for creating next-generation catalysts. This Special Issue of Chemical Synthesis is dedicated to exploring the latest advances and future directions in this rapidly evolving field.

Supramolecular enzyme-mimetic materials leverage non-covalent interactions, such as hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces, and metal coordination, to construct well-defined architectures with enzyme-like properties. These materials can mimic the active sites of enzymes, providing precise control over substrate binding and reaction pathways. Their modular nature allows for facile tuning of catalytic activity and selectivity, opening new avenues for tailored catalysts for specific chemical transformations.

This Special Issue will highlight recent breakthroughs in the design, synthesis, and characterization of supramolecular enzyme-mimetic materials, showcasing their diverse applications in catalysis, sensing, and nanomedicine. We invite original research articles, reviews, and perspectives that address the following topics:
● Novel design strategies for supramolecular enzyme-mimetic materials;
● Synthesis and characterization of biomimetic supramolecular architectures;
● Catalytic mechanisms and applications of supramolecular enzyme-mimetic materials;
● Computational and theoretical studies of supramolecular enzyme-mimetic systems;
● Challenges and future directions in supramolecular enzyme-mimetic material.

This Special Issue aims to provide a comprehensive overview of the current state-of-the-art and stimulate further research in this promising area. We anticipate that the insights from this collection of articles will contribute to developing more efficient, selective, and sustainable chemical processes, ultimately benefiting society.
keywords: ["Supramolecular chemistry", "enzyme-mimetic materials", "biomimetic catalysis", "host-guest chemistry", "self-assembly", "molecular recognition", "catalytic mechanisms", "computational modeling", "sustainable chemistry"]

topic: Topic: Solid State Batteries and Solid Oxide Cells: From Materials Research to Design and Applications
summary:
Solid state batteries and solid oxide cells represent cutting-edge advancements in energy storage and conversion technologies. Solid state batteries utilize solid electrolytes instead of liquid ones, offering enhanced safety, higher energy density, and improved thermal stability, making them promising for applications in electric vehicles, portable electronics and energy storage systems. In contrast, solid oxide cells are electrochemical devices that can operate as both fuel cells and electrolyzer models. They use a solid oxide or ceramic electrolyte to conduct ions at high temperatures, providing efficient energy conversion for power generation and long-term energy storage for renewable energy.

This Special Issue aims to bridge the gap between fundamental materials research and practical design and applications, highlighting recent breakthroughs and ongoing developments in these fields. Contributions will explore the synthesis, characterization, and performance optimization of novel solid electrolytes, electrodes, and interface materials. Additionally, it will cover innovative design approaches and engineering strategies to enhance the efficiency, stability, and scalability of solid state batteries and solid oxide cells. By bringing together diverse studies from materials science to applied engineering, this Special Issue seeks to foster a comprehensive understanding of the challenges and opportunities in advancing solid state energy systems.
keywords: ["Solid state batteries", "solid oxide cells", "energy storage", "electrolyte materials", "electrode design", "energy conversion", "energy storage", "materials optimization"]

topic: Topic: Machine Learning Aided Design, Development, and Application of High Entropy Materials
summary: This Special Issue focuses on the application of machine learning in the design, development, and application of high entropy materials. Highlighting the role of machine learning, this field has witnessed remarkable progress in recent years, enabling the discovery of novel material compositions with unprecedented properties. Machine learning algorithms can analyze vast amounts of data, identify patterns, and predict material behaviors, greatly accelerating the traditional trial-and-error process. The contributions in this Special Issue explore using machine learning techniques to enhance the design efficiency, optimize material properties, and facilitate the development of high entropy materials for various applications.
keywords: ["Machine learning", "material design", "high entropy alloys", "multi-objective optimization", "materials genome engineering"]

topic: Topic: Machine Learning for Development and Management in Rechargeable Batteries
summary:
Rechargeable batteries, exemplified by lithium-ion batteries, are playing a pivotal role in contemporary power systems and the electrification of transportation. However, current batteries face significant challenges, including performance degradation and thermal runaway. The intricate materials design space, under-explored electrochemical reactions, and dynamic operating conditions present significant hurdles to developing new battery technologies or management strategies. Machine learning has emerged as an indispensable tool for addressing these challenges.

Here, we invite researchers and practitioners to contribute to our Special Issue focused on the intersection of machine learning and batteries. Topics of interest include, but are not limited to, the following applications of machine learning:

● Battery materials discovery and structure optimization;

● Battery modeling, state estimation, fault diagnosis, and lifetime prediction;

● Battery charging, balancing, and thermal management; 

● Second use and recycling of retired batteries.
keywords: ["Machine learning", "battery materials", "battery management"]

topic: Topic: Risk Management for Complex Engineering Systems in Digital Era
summary:
With the rapid advancement of digital technology, interconnectivity among engineering systems is increasing. The massive amounts of data generated by complex engineering systems, in both volume and variety, may overwhelm traditional risk management processes, leading to challenges of data overload and making it difficult to produce actionable risk management insights. Additionally, integrating digital technologies with the core business of an organization exposes engineering systems to more sophisticated cyber threats that jeopardize system security, data integrity and organizational business continuity. In a fast-paced digital environment, risks to complex engineering systems can emerge and escalate rapidly. To cope with these new challenges and adapt to technological advances, it is necessary to investigate new approaches to risk management in the digital era to effectively prevent and mitigate emerging threats.

This Special Issue aims to explore and address the various risks complex engineering systems face in the digital era. We invite contributions on a range of topics, including but not limited to:
● Risk Assessment for Digital Transformation of Complex Engineering Systems;
● Data-driven Approaches to Risk management for Complex Engineering Systems;
● Interpretable Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Complex Engineering Systems Risk Management;
● Digital Maturity in Risk Management for Complex Engineering Systems;
● Soft Computing-based Risk Assessment for Complex Engineering Systems;
● Risk Identification of Complex Engineering Systems Based on Knowledge Graphs;
● Case Study of Digital Risk Management for Complex Engineering Systems;
● Empirical Studies Related to Risk Management of Complex Engineering Systems.
keywords: ["Risk assessment", "complex engineering system", "risk management practice", "digitalization", "system modeling method", "case study"]

topic: Topic: A New Generation of Heterojunction - S-scheme Heterojunction Photocatalyst
summary: The energy crisis and environmental pollution have become two global challenges facing human society. Photocatalysis technology offers a feasible solution to these problems using the powerful and inexhaustible solar energy as energy input to produce valuable chemicals and remediate the environment by degrading pollutants. However, one of the greatest bottlenecks for photocatalysis is the low photocatalytic efficiency caused by rapid electron-hole recombination. Within this context, S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts comprising reduction and oxidation semiconducting materials have emerged. The unique transfer path of photogenerated charge carriers endows the S-scheme heterojunctions with high charge separation efficiency and strong redox ability. Inspiringly, numerous photocatalysts have been developed to construct S-scheme heterojunctions with enhanced performances for various important energy and environment-related reactions. This Special Issue focuses on “A new generation of heterojunction - S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst”, featuring the latest advances in S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts. We invite original research articles, reviews, and perspectives on designing, synthesizing and applying S-scheme heterojunctions (e.g., hydrogen evolution, H2O2 production, CO2 reduction, NH3 synthesis, biomass and plastic waste conversion, pollutant degradation, etc.).
keywords: ["Photocatalysis", "S-scheme heterojunction", "synthesis", "environment", "energy", "sustainable catalysis"]

topic: Topic: A New Generation of Heterojunction - S-scheme Heterojunction Photocatalyst
summary: The energy crisis and environmental pollution have become two global challenges facing human society. Photocatalysis technology offers a feasible solution to these problems using the powerful and inexhaustible solar energy as energy input to produce valuable chemicals and remediate the environment by degrading pollutants. However, one of the greatest bottlenecks for photocatalysis is the low photocatalytic efficiency caused by rapid electron-hole recombination. Within this context, S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts comprising reduction and oxidation semiconducting materials have emerged. The unique transfer path of photogenerated charge carriers endows the S-scheme heterojunctions with high charge separation efficiency and strong redox ability. Inspiringly, numerous photocatalysts have been developed to construct S-scheme heterojunctions with enhanced performances for various important energy and environment-related reactions. This Special Issue focuses on “A new generation of heterojunction - S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst”, featuring the latest advances in S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts. We invite original research articles, reviews, and perspectives on designing, synthesizing and applying S-scheme heterojunctions (e.g., hydrogen evolution, H2O2 production, CO2 reduction, NH3 synthesis, biomass and plastic waste conversion, pollutant degradation, etc.).
keywords: ["Photocatalysis", "S-scheme heterojunction", "synthesis", "environment", "energy", "sustainable catalysis"]

topic: Topic: Water and Wastewater Emissions: Advances and Challenges
summary:
Dear colleagues,

Water systems, encompassing drinking water treatment, distribution, and wastewater treatment, play a crucial role in public health and environmental sustainability. However, the water sector contributes 1-4% of global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions during the collection, treatment, and distribution of drinking and wastewaters, necessitating a comprehensive evaluation and reduction of their carbon footprints. Government agencies are increasingly recognizing the role the water sector plays in addressing GHG emissions. For example, the U.S. Department of Energy issued a $23 million funding announcement in 2022 to drive innovation aimed at decarbonizing the entire lifecycle of water resource recovery facilities. Given the interdependencies between water, energy, and climate, nexus approaches can lead to more sustainable and resilient water systems, ultimately contributing to global GHG reduction goals. To advance emerging knowledge and develop cutting-edge solutions to reduce water systems-related GHG emissions, we invite you to submit to this Special Issue.

Proposed topics include, but are not limited to:

● The effects of climate change on the resilience and performance of water systems;

● Low carbon technologies for drinking water and wastewater treatment, including quantification of their reduction benefits;

● Emission processes and characteristics of GHGs in different water systems;

● Lifecycle carbon footprint assessment of water infrastructure projects;

● Study of the interlinkages between different environmental impact factors using nexus approaches on water systems;

● Policy and regulatory instruments for water system GHG reductions.
keywords: ["Methane", "nitrous oxide", "energy-water nexus", "aeration", "pumping", "sludge", "greywater footprint", "resilience"]

topic: Topic: Frontier Research on Paper and Fiber Materials in Energy
summary:
Paper and fiber materials, traditionally used in packaging and textiles, are now promising for energy applications due to their lightweight, flexibility, sustainability, and abundance. Advances in material science and nanotechnology have enhanced their properties for integration into various energy devices. This Special Issue highlights the latest advancements in using these materials for energy storage, conversion and generation.

Exciting research includes paper- and fiber-based batteries, supercapacitors, and fuel cells, leveraging their porosity for efficient ion transport and storage. By incorporating conductive materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, or metal oxides, researchers have developed flexible, lightweight devices with impressive performance, ideal for portable and wearable electronics. These technologies offer sustainable alternatives to traditional energy storage systems, broadening the possibilities for practical applications.

In the realm of energy harvesting, paper and fiber materials are being integrated with triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) and piezoelectric materials. TENGs convert mechanical energy from everyday activities into electrical energy using the triboelectric effect, while piezoelectric materials generate electricity from mechanical deformation. Additionally, fiber-based thermoelectric materials, which convert heat into electricity, are being explored to harness waste heat from industrial processes or body heat, adding another renewable energy source. These innovations provide sustainable solutions for capturing energy from environmental movements and temperature gradients, aiming to create efficient, flexible devices for diverse applications.

Despite progress, challenges such as scalability, material optimization, performance and durability remain. This Special Issue aims to provide a comprehensive overview of paper and fiber materials in energy applications, inviting research on innovative fabrication techniques, performance evaluation, and exploration of emerging trends. By showcasing global research, we hope to inspire innovation and collaboration in developing sustainable energy solutions.
keywords: ["Paper materials", "fiber materials"]

topic: Topic: Solid-State Lithium Batteries: Material Innovations and Future Perspectives
summary:
Solid-state lithium batteries (SSLBs) represent a transformative leap in energy storage technology, offering enhanced safety, higher energy densities, and potential for broader application compared to traditional lithium-ion batteries. This Special Issue explores recent material innovations and future prospects in SSLBs, highlighting their capacity to revolutionize energy storage solutions.

SSLBs utilize solid electrolytes instead of flammable liquids, addressing safety concerns and enabling advancements in battery lifespan and energy density. Material innovation, supported by advanced characterization techniques, plays a crucial role in optimizing SSLB performance and scalability.

Integration of SSLBs across various sectors—from electronics to electric vehicles and grid storage—promises to reshape global energy landscapes. This issue gathers contributions from experts, presenting insights into SSLB materials, manufacturing techniques, and theoretical advancements to accelerate their development and adoption.

In summary, this Special Issue aims to catalyze interdisciplinary collaboration and inspire further innovation in SSLB technology, positioning SSLBs at the forefront of sustainable energy storage solutions.
keywords: ["Solid-state lithium batteries"]

source: Journal of Surveillance, Security and Safety
topic: Topic: Security, Privacy, and Trust in Intelligent Cyber-Physical Systems and Internet of Things: Trends, Issues, and Challenges
summary:
The rapid advancement and integration of Intelligent Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and the Internet of Things (IoT) have revolutionized numerous sectors, including smart cities, healthcare, industrial automation, transportation, and home automation. These technologies enable unprecedented levels of interconnectivity and automation, facilitating the collection, analysis, and utilization of vast amounts of data to improve efficiency, decision-making, and user experiences. However, the increasing complexity and pervasiveness of these systems introduce significant security, privacy, and trust challenges that must be addressed to ensure their safe and reliable operation.

Security in CPS and IoT involves protecting systems from malicious attacks, unauthorized access, and data breaches that can compromise the integrity and availability of services. Cyber-physical attacks, such as those targeting industrial control systems or smart grids, can have severe physical consequences, making robust security measures paramount. Privacy concerns are equally critical, as the extensive data collected by IoT devices often includes sensitive personal information. Ensuring that this data is protected from unauthorized access and misuse is essential to maintaining user trust and compliance with regulatory requirements. Additionally, trust in CPS and IoT extends beyond technical security measures to encompass the reliability and dependability of systems and services. Users need assurance that the devices and systems they rely on will perform as expected and that their data will be handled responsibly. This trust is built through transparent practices, robust authentication mechanisms, and effective communication of security and privacy policies.

The convergence of these issues presents a complex landscape that requires a multidisciplinary approach to address. Emerging trends, such as adopting blockchain technology for secure transactions, using machine learning for threat detection, and developing privacy-preserving protocols, offer promising solutions but also introduce new challenges. This Special Issue aims to explore these trends, identify critical issues, and discuss innovative approaches to enhancing the security, privacy, and trust of CPS and IoT systems.

By bringing together researchers, practitioners, and policymakers, this Special Issue seeks to advance the state of the art in CPS and IoT security, foster collaboration, and drive the development of solutions that can effectively address the evolving threats and challenges in this dynamic field.

Topics of interest include, but are not limited to:
● Security architectures for CPS and IoT;
● Privacy-preserving protocols in CPS and IoT;
● Trust management frameworks for intelligent systems;
● Intrusion detection and prevention in CPS/IoT;
● Blockchain and distributed ledger technologies for security and privacy;
● Secure data aggregation and storage in IoT;
● Machine learning and artificial intelligence (AI) for threat detection in CPS;
● Access control and authentication mechanisms;
● Secure communication protocols;
● Network security and reliability for CPS and IoT;
● Risk assessment and management in CPS and IoT;
● Case studies on security breaches and mitigation strategies;
● AI security, privacy, and ethics in CPS and IoT;
● Legal, ethical, and regulatory issues in CPS and IoT security;
● Honeypot strategies for enhanced intrusion detection in CPS and IoT.
keywords: ["Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)", "Internet of Things (IoT)", "security", "privacy", "trust"]

source: Journal of Surveillance, Security and Safety
topic: Topic: Advances in Human Factors in Cybersecurity
summary:
"Human factors in cybersecurity" is a relatively new but rapidly growing discipline. While cybersecurity itself has been a focus since the advent of computers and networks, the examination of human factors began to gain traction in the late 20th century. 

Human factors in cybersecurity encompass the psychological, behavioural and social aspects that influence how individuals interact with systems, technology, and processes. It is a critical aspect of cybersecurity since technology alone often fails to protect humans and nation-states.

We contend that humans are not the weakest link, as is often touted by technical cybersecurity specialists. Instead, understanding humans and how they interact with technology is critical for improving methods and systems to protect individuals and national and international government and non-government organisations from cyber attacks and crime, more broadly.

This Special Issue invites contributions that explore all aspects related to human involvement in cybersecurity. We welcome papers from experts in psychology, sociology, criminology, philosophy and related disciplines, particularly those applying theories and methods to investigate and understand cybersecurity challenges. Topics of interest include:
● Behavioural change in cybersecurity practices;
● Cyberscams;
● Ethics;
● Insider threats;
● Mis/disinformation;
● Organisation management;
● Policy;
● Social engineering;
● Surveillance and monitoring;
● Criminal investigation.
keywords: ["Human factors", "cybersecurity", "security improvement"]

topic: Topic: Economic and Environmental Sustainability of Manufacturing Processes: Integrated Methods, Models And Tools
summary:
The Special Issue "Economic and Environmental Sustainability of Manufacturing Processes: Integrated Methods, Models and Tools" explores the intersection of economic viability and environmental responsibility in manufacturing. This collection of articles focuses on innovative approaches to enhance sustainability through the integration of advanced methods, models, and tools. Topics include developing and applying sustainable manufacturing technologies, optimizing resource use, reducing environmental impacts, and conducting economic analysis of sustainable practices. The issue aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of achieving balanced economic growth and environmental protection in the manufacturing sector. It emphasizes combining economic and environmental considerations into cohesive models and tools that guide decision-making in manufacturing. Key areas include but are not limited to the following five parts:

● Development and application of new methods that enhance the sustainability of manufacturing processes, including life cycle assessment, eco-design, and green supply chain management.

● Advanced modeling techniques to predict and optimize the environmental and economic outcomes of various manufacturing processes.

● Real-world examples and case studies illustrating the practical application of integrated methods and tools in different manufacturing sectors.

● Exploration of policies and regulations supporting the adoption of sustainable practices in the manufacturing industry.

● Discussion on the role of cutting-edge technologies, such as Industry 4.0, in enhancing both economic and environmental sustainability.

Overall, this Special Issue underscores the necessity of a holistic approach to manufacturing that simultaneously addresses economic and environmental challenges, leveraging integrated methods, models, and tools to achieve sustainable development goals.
keywords: ["Sustainable manufacturing technologies", "economic and environmental sustainability", "model", "tools"]

topic: Topic: Frontiers in Advanced Alkali-Ion Cathode Materials
summary:
To meet the surging demand for efficient, sustainable energy storage, it is imperative to develop high-performance cathode materials for next-generation alkali metal ion batteries. The Special Issue on "Frontiers in Advanced Cathode Materials for Alkali Metal Ion Batteries" explores breakthroughs, trends, and future directions in this field.

Cathode materials play a critical role in the performance and stability of alkali metal ion batteries. Conventional oxide cathodes still face challenges such as capacity loss and environmental impact, triggering research into innovative cathode materials. Over the last decades, many high-performance cathode materials for lithium, sodium, and potassium ion batteries have been reported.

To increase energy density and lower cost, many lithium-based cathodes have been developed, such as ultrahigh-nickel-based lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC) and lithium manganese iron phosphate (LMFP). Research also focuses on sodium-ion and potassium-ion batteries for cost-effectiveness and resource availability. Advanced characterization and modeling enhance understanding of the structure-property-performance relationship of cathodes, guiding the rational design of next-generation alkali metal ion cathode materials.

This Special Issue invites contributions on the synthesis, characterization, and applications of advanced cathodes for alkali metal ion batteries. By highlighting advancements and addressing challenges, it aims to advance sustainable energy storage technologies to meet future demands for clean energy transition.
keywords: ["Alkali metal ion batteries", "Cathode materials", "electrochemical performance", "material characterization", "rational design"]

topic: Topic: Preparation, Characterisation and Application of Single Atom Catalysts
summary: The Special Issue "Preparation, Characterisation and Application of Single Atom Catalysts" is dedicated to promoting the research of single atom catalysts (SACs) at theoretical, experimental and application levels. Recently, SACs have demonstrated great potential in various catalytic fields due to their maximum atomic efficiency and controllable coordination environment. Their unique geometric and electronic structure, distinct from bulk metals, results in different catalytic activity. Additionally, the catalytic activity of SACs can be regulated by their composition and coordination, presenting more opportunities for their development and application. Therefore, developing new methods, principles and strategies for the preparation, characterisation and application of SACs is highly desired. This Special Issue invites Original Research Articles, Reviews, Perspectives, and Highlights covering a wide range of topics within the realm of SACs. Topics of interest include but are not limited to, the design and development, representational means, application areas, catalytic principles, and theoretical predictions of novel SACs.
keywords: ["Single atom catalysts", "synthetic strategies", "catalytic activity", "mechanism understanding", "theoretical analysis"]

topic: Topic: Eco-Sustainable and Energy-Efficient Building Materials: Advancements and Scientific Insights
summary:
The Special Issue titled "Eco-Sustainable and Energy-Efficient Building Materials: Advancements and Scientific Insights" explores the latest developments and research in the realm of sustainable building materials and energy efficiency. It encompasses a range of topics crucial to addressing environmental concerns and enhancing building performance, such as innovative materials, eco-friendly construction techniques, energy-efficient designs, and their scientific underpinnings.

Contributions to the Special Issue feature studies on novel materials such as recycled aggregates, bio-based composites, and advanced insulation materials that reduce environmental impact while improving energy efficiency. Researchers delve into the scientific principles behind these advancements, including material properties, life cycle assessment, and their implications for sustainable architecture. Additionally, the issue highlights interdisciplinary approaches integrating engineering, materials science, and environmental studies to foster a comprehensive understanding of sustainable building practices. It emphasizes the importance of technological innovation and policy initiatives in promoting eco-sustainability and energy efficiency in construction.

Overall, the Special Issue serves as a valuable resource for researchers, engineers, policymakers, and practitioners interested in advancing sustainable building practices through cutting-edge materials and scientific insights.

topic: Topic: Innovations and Applications of Fuel Cells
summary:
Fuel cells have gained significant attention as a key technology for sustainable energy solutions. Their ability to convert chemical energy directly into electricity with minimal pollutants makes them crucial for a low-carbon economy. This Special Issue, "Innovations and Applications of Fuel Cells", explores the latest advancements, ongoing challenges, and future prospects of this transformative technology.

Fuel cells offer high efficiency and versatility, suitable for applications from stationary power generation to transportation. Unlike traditional combustion engines, they produce electricity with water and heat as byproducts, significantly reducing harmful emissions. In the transportation sector, fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) offer faster refueling times and longer ranges than battery electric vehicles. In stationary applications, fuel cells provide reliable power, especially in remote locations, and play a potential role in grid stabilization and energy storage by converting excess renewable energy into hydrogen for later use.

Recent advancements in materials science and engineering have improved fuel cell performance, durability, and cost-effectiveness. However, challenges such as high production costs and the need for hydrogen distribution and storage infrastructure remain significant barriers. Enhancing the durability and lifespan of fuel cells is essential for their reliability in demanding applications such as automotive and industrial use. Additionally, sustainable hydrogen production and a robust hydrogen infrastructure are crucial for widespread adoption.

Fuel cells present both challenges and opportunities on the path to a sustainable energy future. This Special Issue highlights the current state of fuel cell technology, ongoing research, and strategies to overcome existing barriers. By fostering innovation and collaboration, we can unlock the potential of fuel cells and advance towards a cleaner energy landscape.
keywords: ["Fuel cells", "hydrogen energy", "solid oxide fuel cells (SOFC)", "proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFC)"]

topic: Topic: Smart Hydrogels for Flexible Electronics
summary:
Smart hydrogels, with their unique physicochemical properties, are becoming key materials in the manufacturing field of flexible electronic devices. Their high water content, excellent biocompatibility, adjustable mechanical properties, and significant microwave absorption capabilities not only provide novel possibilities for biomedical and environmental responsiveness applications but also open new avenues to improve the electromagnetic compatibility and electromagnetic interference shielding capabilities of electronic devices.

This Special Issue will showcase the synthesis methods of smart hydrogels, strategies for performance optimization, and their innovative uses in flexible electronic devices, while exploring the current challenges and future development prospects in this field.

We invite submissions of original research, review articles, and perspectives. Authors are encouraged to share their latest findings in the synthesis of smart hydrogels, performance regulation, and practical implementations in flexible electronic devices. All submissions will undergo a rigorous peer review process to ensure that the published articles are of high quality and innovation.

We look forward to these research results driving the further advancement of smart hydrogels in flexible electronics and promoting the commercialization and practical use of related technologies.
keywords: ["Smart hydrogel synthesis", "conductive hydrogels", "performance optimization strategies", "electromagnetic wave absorption/shielding", "biomedical applications", "electronic skin", "wearable sensors"]

topic: Topic: Flexible Materials for Electromagnetic Wave Absorption and Shielding
summary:
As electronic devices and wireless communication technology advance rapidly, electromagnetic interference (EMI) has become increasingly severe, posing potential threats to human health and the normal operation of electronic equipment. To effectively address this issue, investigating electromagnetic wave absorption and shielding materials has become crucial. This Special Issue aims to compile the latest research findings in this field, exploring the application potential of flexible materials in electromagnetic wave management. It includes the design and preparation methods of flexible electromagnetic shielding materials, electromagnetic performance testing and characterization, analysis of practical application cases, and future development trends and technical challenges.

Through systematic analysis and discussion of these cutting-edge topics, this Special Issue seeks to provide valuable references and innovative ideas for researchers, promoting the further development and application of flexible materials in the realm of electromagnetic wave absorption and shielding.
keywords: ["Flexible materials", "electromagnetic wave absorption", "electromagnetic shielding", "electromagnetic interference (EMI)", "material design", "performance testing", "application cases"]

topic: Topic: Next Generation of Electrochemical Energy Stroage and Conversion
summary:
Electrochemical energy storage and conversion technologies, such as batteries and electrolytic cells, are at the heart of today's energy landscape. They support the development of e-mobility and renewable energy while providing critical support for smart grids. The continuous advancement of these technologies will lead to more efficient and reliable energy storage solutions, promoting the global transition to sustainable energy. This Special Issue invites all types of manuscripts, including Research Articles, Mini-Reviews/Reviews, Short Communications, Highlights, etc. The topics covered are extensive and include, but are not limited to:

1) Progress in the application of solar cells, lithium-ion batteries, flow cells, sodium-sulfur batteries, lead-acid batteries, lead-carbon batteries, zinc-bromine batteries, and other chemical energy storage products;

2) Measures to enhance charge storage and the latest research findings;

3) Applications of energy and electronic materials;

4) The internal mechanisms of energy conversion and storage.

topic: Topic: Disaster Prevention and Resilience Assessment with Artificial Intelligence and the Internet of Things
summary:
Disaster prevention and resilience assessment of urban infrastructure systems (e.g., transportation, water supply, electric power) are crucial for the stability and growth of modern societies, especially given the increasing frequency and severity of natural and human-induced disasters. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and the Internet of Things (IoT) offer innovative solutions for enhancing disaster resilience and prevention strategies. This Special Issue focuses on various aspects of integrating AI and IoT for evaluating and improving the disaster prevention and resilience of urban infrastructure systems. The objective is to present cutting-edge research, methodologies, and applications that utilize these technologies to anticipate, manage, and mitigate the impacts of disasters.

Topics can be, but are not confined to:

● Natural disasters and risk analysis;

● Vulnerability modeling and consequence assessment;

● Risk-informed disaster prevention;

● Risk analysis based on the IoTs;

● Machine learning in risk and resilience analysis;

● Community resilience assessment.
keywords: ["Artificial intelligence", "the Internet of Things", "disaster prevention", "resilience assessment", "natural disasters", "risk analysis", "machine learning", "vulnerability modeling"]

source: Journal of Surveillance, Security and Safety
topic: Topic: Novel Approaches for Software Security: Challenges and Solutions
summary:
Software security is the practice of protecting software systems and data through program reinforcement and formal logic analysis. With the widespread application of the software-defined paradigm in various environments, a large number of malicious behaviors threaten software security from different attack surfaces. The emergence of advanced technologies such as Artificial Intelligence (AI), Natural Language Processing (NLP), Graph Neural Networks (GNN), and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks presents both new opportunities and challenges in the domain of software security.

These cutting-edge technologies have significantly influenced both offensive and defensive aspects of software security. On the defensive side, AI and machine learning models can enhance vulnerability detection, identify malicious code, and improve Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) detection. Conversely, attackers are also leveraging these technologies to develop more sophisticated methods. AI-driven tools can automate the generation of adversarial samples to bypass security mechanisms, and advanced NLP models can help craft more convincing phishing attacks. Backdoor and data poisoning issues also create uncertainty for AI models. The dual-use nature of these technologies underscores the need for innovative and robust security measures.

Based on this demand, the field of information security needs more innovative methods for existing software security. Relevant topics include, but are not limited to:
● Support to the software development process (analysis, architecture, construction, maintenance and evolution);
● Complexity and systems thinking applied to the software security management and the software security engineering;
● The software security platform development and governance;
● Dynamic reconfiguration at different levels of software security (strategic, tactical, operational) to revisit coordination mechanisms and emergent behavior;
● Strategies, relationships, and applications of software security;
● Software security sustainability and diversity;
● Studies relative to software security within specific domains and areas (open source, mobile, embedded, autonomous systems, AI, digital transformations, the Internet of Things (IoT) technologies, telecommunication, etc.);
● Techniques and tools for software security verification/validation/testing;
● Techniques and tools for the vulnerability detection;
● Generative AI applied to the software security testing and adversarial sample generation for related models;
● Large Language Models (LLMs) for adaptive software attack surface management and software asset management;
● LLMs for automatically generating training samples for software security models;
● Malicious code identification using AI and machine learning techniques;
● Studies relative to the APT detection through LLMs;
● Automated security analysis and threat modeling with AI-driven tools;
● Integration of AI in continuous security monitoring and incident response;
● Defensive strategies against AI-driven cyber attacks;
● Case studies and real-world applications of AI in enhancing software security.
keywords: ["Software security", "artificial intelligence (AI)", "large language models (LLMs)", "advanced persistent threat (APT)", "defensive strategies"]

topic: Topic: Chemical Technologies for Hydrogen Storage and Production
summary:
Hydrogen, as a clean and renewable energy carrier, holds significant promise for the future of energy systems. Efficient storage and production of hydrogen are critical challenges that must be addressed to fully realize its potential. This Special Issue on "Chemical Technologies for Hydrogen Storage and Production" aims to gather the latest research and advancements in the chemical technologies that enable hydrogen energy solutions.

This collection will focus on a variety of topics, including but not limited to: Advanced Chemical Materials for Hydrogen Storage, Innovative Chemical Processes for Hydrogen Production, Catalysts and Reaction Mechanisms, Integration with Renewable Energy Sources, Theoretical and Computational Studies, Safety, Efficiency, and Economic Aspects.

Our objective is to showcase cutting-edge research that advances our understanding and capabilities in chemical technologies for hydrogen energy. By addressing both the fundamental and applied aspects of these technologies, we strive to contribute to the development of sustainable and efficient hydrogen energy systems.
keywords: ["Chemical technologies", "hydrogen", "clean energy", "renewable energy", "energy carrier", "hydrogen storage", "hydrogen production", "advanced chemical materials", "innovative chemical processes", "Catalysts", "reaction mechanisms", "integration with renewable energy", "theoretical studies", "computational studies", "economic aspects", "sustainable energy systems", "cutting-edge research", "fundamental aspects", "applied aspects"]

topic: Topic: Frontier Advances in Cathode Materials for Na/ K/ Zn Batteries
summary:
The Special Issue "Frontier Advances in Cathode Materials for Na/K/Zn Batteries" in Energy Materials focuses on the latest research and advancements in developing cathode materials for sodium (Na), potassium (K), and zinc (Zn) batteries. As demand for sustainable and efficient energy storage grows, these alternative battery technologies are emerging as promising candidates to complement and potentially surpass traditional lithium (Li)-ion batteries due to their abundant resources, cost-effectiveness, and enhanced safety.

This Special Issue provides a platform for researchers and industry experts to share innovative approaches in the design, synthesis, and characterization of cathode materials for Na, K, and Zn batteries. We encourage submissions exploring novel material compositions, advanced fabrication techniques, and in-depth performance evaluations. Studies addressing fundamental electrochemical mechanisms, material stability, and scalability are particularly welcome.

Key topics include, but are not limited to:
● Development of high-performance cathode materials with enhanced capacity and cycling stability;
● Innovative synthesis methods for novel cathode materials;
● Structural and compositional optimization to improve electrochemical performance;
● Mechanistic studies on electrochemical processes and degradation pathways;
● Integration of cathode materials into full-cell configurations;
● Comparative analyses with other energy storage technologies;
● Environmental impact and sustainability assessments.

By bringing together diverse studies and perspectives, this Special Issue aims to advance the understanding and application of Na, K, and Zn battery technologies. We invite researchers to submit original research articles, comprehensive reviews, and insightful perspectives that drive progress of this dynamic field.
keywords: ["Na-ion batteries", "K-ion batteries", "Zn-ion batteries", "cathode materials", "energy storage", "material design", "performance optimization", "battery technology advancements", "electrochemical mechanisms"]

topic: Topic: Latest Advances and Challenges in Electrochemical Capacitor Materials
summary:
Electrochemical capacitors have garnered significant attention in recent years due to their unique energy storage capabilities, bridging the gap between conventional capacitors and batteries. They offer high power density, rapid charge/discharge rates, and long cycle life, making them indispensable in various applications ranging from portable electronics to electric vehicles and grid energy storage systems. The performance of electrochemical capacitors is primarily determined by the materials used in their construction, which has driven extensive research into the development and optimization of these materials.

This Special Issue, titled "Latest Advances and Challenges in Electrochemical Capacitor Materials", aims to provide a comprehensive overview of the recent progress and emerging challenges in this dynamic field. We invite researchers to contribute original research articles, reviews, and perspective pieces that explore innovative materials, novel fabrication techniques, and advanced characterization methods that push the boundaries of electrochemical capacitor technology.

Scope and Topics:
● Advanced Electrode Materials;
● Electrolyte Innovations;
● Device Architectures and Fabrication Techniques;
● Performance Enhancement and Optimization;
● Characterization and Modeling;
● Application-Specific Developments.

This Special Issue aims to serve as a comprehensive resource for researchers, engineers, and practitioners in the field of energy storage, providing insights into the latest advancements and identifying future research directions. We look forward to your contributions and to advancing the field of electrochemical capacitor materials together.
keywords: ["Electrochemical capacitors", "energy storage", "advanced materials", "performance enhancement"]

topic: Topic: Structural Regulation and Application of Two-Dimensional Metal Oxides
summary: Two-dimensional Metal Oxides have aroused increasing attention because of their unique properties such as high carrier mobility, tunable bandgap, high stability, high microfabrication, high transparency, mechanical flexibility, and strong quantum confinement effect. Therefore, they have been widely investigated for electro- and photocatalytic applications, including field-effect transistors (FETs) and logic circuits, photodetectors, piezotronics/piezo-phototronics, memristors, batteries, and more. Nevertheless, the physicochemical properties of two-dimensional metal oxide materials and their excellent performance in applications are closely related to their microstructure. Consequently, it is vital to clarify the relationship between microstructure regulation (various oxidation-state forms, polymorphism, crystalline, anisotropy, point defects, grain boundary, etc.) and properties of two-dimensional metal oxides and their influence on their performance. We welcome contributions from research scientists in academia, industry, and government institutes.
keywords: ["Two-dimensional metal oxides", "microstructure regulation", "performance optimization"]

topic: Topic: Key Materials for Aqueous Flow Battery
summary: As the demand for large-scale energy storage solutions grows, the need for environmentally benign and high-performance electrolytes and electrode materials becomes increasingly critical. This Special Issue will cover a wide range of topics, including novel electrolyte formulations, advanced electrode materials, and their integration into flow battery systems. We welcome original research articles, reviews, and perspectives that address the challenges and opportunities in enhancing the efficiency, stability, and environmental impact of flow batteries. Contributions focusing on the synthesis, characterization, and performance evaluation of new materials and studies on the fundamental mechanisms underlying their operation are highly encouraged. By highlighting cutting-edge research in this field, this Special Issue aims to advance the development of next-generation flow batteries that are both efficient and environmentally sustainable.
keywords: ["Flow battery", "large-scale energy storage", "environmentally friendly electrolytes", "high-performance electrode materials", "novel electrolyte formulations", "advanced electrode materials", "efficiency and stability", "environmental impact", "synthesis and characterization", "performance evaluation", "fundamental mechanisms", "environmentally sustainable solutions"]

topic: Topic: Sophisticated Construction and Application of High-Performance Hydrogenation Efficient Catalyst
summary:
The research on the “High-performance hydrogenation catalysis” aims to develop the efficient catalysts and elucidate the structure-performance relationship in a series of hydrogenation reactions.

The main research directions include:
● Optimizing the structure of active sites for hydrogenation;
● Exploring the interactions between metals and supports through interface engineering to enhance catalyst stability and efficiency;
● Refining synthesis processes to achieve efficient catalyst preparation;
● Combining experimental and theoretical approaches to clarify the mechanisms of hydrogenation reactions, thereby guiding the design and improvement of catalysts;
● Applying these high-performance catalysts in chemical, energy, and environmental fields to assess their industrial potential.

This research is dedicated to advancing hydrogenation catalysis technology, providing technical support for more efficient and energy-saving industrial processes.
