topic: Topic: Soft Microfluidics for Biomedical Applications
summary:
Microfluidics has attracted increasing interest over recent years due to its advantages, including low device cost, high integration, and diverse functionality. Commonly, the microfluidic devices are made in soft materials (e.g., polydimethylsiloxane and polymer films) through microfabrication techniques. The use of these soft materials has facilitated the widespread application of soft microfluidics in wearable biomedical devices, soft robotics, flexible sensors, and more.

This Special Issue aims to showcase advancements and innovations in developing soft microfluidics for various biomedical applications. We invite researchers to contribute original research articles, reviews, and perspectives displaying novel functionalities, new fabrication techniques, innovative applications, and fundamentals of soft microfluidics.

Topics of interest include but are not limited to:
● Novel functions enabled by soft microfluidics;
● New fabrication techniques for soft microfluidics;
● Fundamentals of soft microfluidics;
● New applications of soft microfluidics;
● Soft matters in soft microfluidics.
keywords: ["​Soft microfluidics", "novel applications", "fabrication techniques", "new functions"]

topic: Topic: Global Perspectives on Air Pollution Exposure and Human Health Implications
summary:
The Special Issue aims to address the critical environmental and public health challenges posed by atmospheric pollutants, such as particulate matter, inorganic pollutants, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), and semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs). These pollutants originate from various sources, including industrial emissions, vehicular exhaust, and agricultural activities, and can persist in the environment, posing significant health risks due to their toxicity and ability to accumulate in living organisms. The Special Issue will explore key topics such as the characterization of pollutant sources, human exposure pathways, exposure and effect biomarkers, and health risk assessments. It will also highlight advancements in analytical methods and discuss effective mitigation strategies and policy implications to reduce exposure for the protection of public health.

By consolidating cutting-edge research, this issue seeks to enhance scientific understanding of the behavior and impacts of pollutants in the atmosphere, support evidence-based policymaking, and foster interdisciplinary collaboration. We invite contributions from researchers and practitioners in environmental science, toxicology, public health, chemistry, and policy studies, with an emphasis on providing new insights into exposure pathways and health risks. The ultimate goal is to promote a healthier and more sustainable environment through comprehensive assessments and innovative solutions to manage and mitigate the risks associated with atmospheric pollutants.

All submissions MUST consider the implications for human and/or wildlife exposure to contaminants. Please see the Aims and Scope of JEEA for further guidance.
keywords: ["Atmospheric pollutants", "emerging organic contaminants", "source apportionment", "biomarkers", "health risk assessment"]

topic: Topic: Fundamentals, Materials, and Applications of Batteries
summary:
Fundamentals, Materials, and Applications of Batteries encompass a broad spectrum of research and development. At its core, understanding the Fundamentals involves electrochemical processes, including charge/discharge mechanisms and energy storage principles. Materials innovation plays a pivotal role, focusing on electrode materials, electrolytes, and separators to enhance performance, lifespan, and safety. Application-wise, batteries power diverse technologies from portable electronics to electric vehicles and grid-scale energy storage, driving sustainability and energy independence initiatives globally. Advances in battery technology continually push boundaries in efficiency, environmental impact, and cost-effectiveness, shaping the future of renewable energy integration and transportation electrification. This Special Issue invites manuscripts of all types, including Research Articles, Mini-reviews/Reviews, Short communications, Highlights, Perspectives, etc. The topics covered in our publication are extensive, including but not limited to:

● Secondary batteries;

● Fuel cells;

● Supercapacitors;

● Aqueous batteries;

● Energy storage materials.
keywords: ["Electrochemical processes", "advanced materials", "energy storage", "renewable energy", "batteries"]

topic: Topic: Antibiotic Resistance: Exposure Pathways and Human Health Risk Assessment
summary:
This Special Issue, entitled "Antibiotic Resistance: Exposure Pathways and Human Health Risk Assessment," focuses on the critical and growing issue of antibiotic resistance. Antibiotic resistance has been recognized for decades as a major threat to public health, as it compromises the effectiveness of antibiotics and poses significant risks to treatment outcomes for infectious diseases. Despite this awareness, there are still substantial gaps in our understanding of the pathways through which humans are exposed to antibiotics, antibiotic-resistant bacteria, and antibiotic resistance genes, as well as the associated health risks.

Antibiotics, antibiotic-resistant bacteria and antibiotic resistance genes can be found in various environments, including soil, water, and food, leading to potential exposure through multiple routes. These pathways can include direct contact with contaminated surfaces, consumption of contaminated food and water, and interaction with environments where resistant bacteria are prevalent, such as healthcare settings and agricultural sites. Understanding these exposure pathways is crucial for assessing the risks to human health and developing effective mitigation strategies.

This Special Issue will delve into the diverse exposure pathways of antibiotics, antibiotic-resistant bacteria, and antibiotic resistance genes, and the subsequent human health risks. Research that integrates exposure data into health risk assessments, regulatory considerations, or communication strategies is of particular interest.

Potential topics include:
1. Pathways of exposure to antibiotics, antibiotic-resistant bacteria, and antibiotic resistance genes from environmental sources (e.g., water, soil, food);
2. Assessment of human health risks associated with these exposure pathways;
3. Spatial and temporal distributions of antibiotic resistance in various environments;
4. Interpretation of exposure data in health and/or regulatory contexts;
5. Risk/benefit analyses related to antibiotic use and resistance;
6. Approaches to communication regarding antibiotic resistance and its impacts on public health use.
keywords: ["Antibiotic resistance", "exposure pathways", "spatial and temporal distributions", "exposure data; human health risk", "public health"]

topic: Topic: Synthesis and Application of Functional Carbon Materials
summary:
Carbon materials are pivotal in both scientific research and industrial applications due to their diverse structures and distinctive properties. With the rapid progress in nanotechnology and materials science, advanced functional carbon materials—such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, and carbon quantum dots—have emerged as a major research focus. These materials are renowned for their exceptional mechanical strength, superior electrical and thermal conductivity, and remarkable chemical stability.

This Special Issue is dedicated to exploring the synthesis and application of advanced functional carbon materials, emphasizing their role in various sub-topics including:
● Synthesis Techniques: Innovative methods for the fabrication and modification of advanced carbon materials;
● Energy Storage: Applications in batteries, supercapacitors, and other energy storage technologies;
● Catalysis: Use of carbon materials in enhancing catalytic processes;
● Biomedicine: Applications in drug delivery, imaging, and other medical fields;
● Environmental Protection: Solutions for pollution control, water purification, and environmental remediation;
● Electronic Devices: Integration of carbon materials in electronic components and devices.

We invite the submission of original research articles, reviews, short communications, and other contributions that provide new insights or significant advancements in these areas. The goal of this Special Issue is to foster academic exchange and collaboration, ultimately driving forward the development and application of advanced carbon materials.
keywords: ["Advanced functional carbon materials", "synthesis techniques", "energy storage", "catalysis", "biomedicine", "environmental protection", "electronic devices"]

topic: Topic: Emerging Water Contaminants: Innovative Analytical Approaches for Detection and Quantification
summary: The presence of emerging contaminants in water sources is a critical environmental and public health issue. These contaminants, including pharmaceuticals, personal care products, pesticides, and microplastics, are increasingly detected in various water bodies due to advancements in analytical techniques. This Special Issue focuses on the development and application of innovative analytical approaches for the detection and quantification of emerging water contaminants. The featured research addresses the challenges posed by these contaminants, such as their low concentrations, complex matrices, and diverse chemical properties. It highlights cutting-edge methodologies, including advanced chromatographic and spectroscopic techniques, biosensors, and nanotechnology-based approaches, which enhance detection sensitivity and accuracy. Additionally, the issue explores the integration of data analytics and machine learning to improve the interpretation of complex data sets. By presenting the latest advancements in analytical science, this Special Issue aims to provide valuable insights and tools for researchers, policymakers, and environmental professionals to effectively monitor and manage emerging water contaminants, ultimately protecting public health and the environment.
keywords: ["Emerging water contaminants", "analytical techniques", "chromatography", "spectroscopy", "biosensors", "nanotechnology", "data analytics"]

topic: Topic: Wastewater Epidemiology: Emerging Contaminants, Human Health, and Ecosystems
summary: The increasing prevalence of emerging contaminants in wastewater is a growing concern due to their potential impacts on human health and ecosystems. Wastewater epidemiology is an innovative approach that analyzes wastewater to monitor and assess public health, providing critical insights into the presence and effects of these contaminants. This Special Issue explores the multifaceted aspects of wastewater epidemiology, focusing on the detection, characterization, and implications of emerging contaminants. It aims to present state-of-the-art research on the sources, fate, and transport of these contaminants, their toxicological effects on humans and ecosystems, and the advanced analytical methods employed for their detection and quantification. Additionally, it examines the role of wastewater treatment technologies and the effectiveness of various management strategies in mitigating the risks associated with these contaminants. By bringing together the latest findings from researchers around the world, this Special Issue seeks to enhance our understanding of the complex interactions between emerging contaminants, human health, and environmental sustainability.
keywords: ["Emerging contaminants", "wastewater epidemiology", "public health", "toxicological effects", "analytical methods", "wastewater treatment", "environmental sustainability"]

source: Journal of Surveillance, Security and Safety
topic: Topic: Wireless Network Security and Privacy
summary:
With the rapid development of wireless communication technology and the popularity of mobile smart terminal devices, Wireless Networks (WNs) are gradually being used in various fields, such as smart cities, e-healthcare, smart agriculture, smart grid, and autonomous vehicles, and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). They are becoming an integral element of daily life for activities in which a sender may deliver critical data to a receiver without the usage of any physical media. Recent technological advancements in WNs provide several benefits such as ubiquitous high data rates, low latency, and high bandwidth. However, these advancements also introduce several limitations, such as challenges in security, privacy, reliability, authenticity, integrity, and scalability, which can impede the performance of WN-based applications. Although the scientific community has implemented effective radio resource management and cutting-edge technologies, such as artificial intelligence (AI), blockchain, and quantum communication, to improve network performance in wireless networks, the motivations of the attackers are constantly changing. New attacks are emerging, and the increasingly diverse demands for wireless networks, as network technologies evolve, present a challenge to existing solutions. Therefore, it is urgent and necessary to study security and optimization issues in the context of wireless networks. This Special Issue aims to collect the latest developments in the study of security and privacy of wireless networks.

Topics of interest include, but are not limited to:
● Secure and Flexible Blockchain Frameworks in WNs;
● Secure Communication Architectures in WNs;
● Fine-grained Access Control Design in WNs;
● Encrypted Solutions in WNs;
● Various Types of Encrypted Data Search in WNs;
● Machine Learning-based Intrusion Detection Techniques in WNs;
● Computation of Encrypted Data Based on Private Computing in WNs;
● Licensed and Unlicensed Bands for 5G and Beyond;
● Distributed and Federated Learning for Privacy in WNs;
● Safe and Secure AI-based Communications for UAVs;
● Blockchain Technology-based Solutions in WNs;
● Secure Data Integrity and Validation Techniques for WNs;
● Emerging Security and Privacy Challenges in WNs.

Academics, research students and professionals are invited to submit their original work. Extended conference papers are also welcome, provided they have been revised and contain at least 50% new content.
keywords: ["Wireless networks (WNs)", "blockchain technology", "machine learning", "security issues", "privacy"]

topic: Topic: Advances in Energy Materials for Zero Carbon
summary: This Special Issue aims to highlight novel ideas, cutting-edge research, and promising developments in the field of energy materials with a focus on zero carbon technologies. We invite high-quality original contributions, both theoretical and experimental, that delve into various aspects of energy materials relevant to achieving zero carbon goals. Topics of interest include, but are not limited to, advancements in solid oxide fuel cells, electrolysis cells, hydrogen production, CO<sub>2</sub> reduction, and energy transmission technologies. We encourage submissions that address recent developments in material science, including new materials and preparation methods, electrochemical behavior of electrodes and electrolytes, energy efficiency enhancements, and modeling of energy conversion systems. Contributions exploring single-cell, stack, and hybrid systems, as well as numerical simulations and mechanisms of material degradation, are also welcome. This issue seeks to bring together innovative research that contributes to the advancement of energy materials and technologies critical for achieving sustainable and efficient zero carbon solutions.
keywords: ["Solid oxide fuel cells (SOFCs)", "solid-oxide electrolysis cells (SOECs)", "oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)", "oxygen evolution reaction (OER)", "design of novel materials", "energy conversion materials", "catalysis", "hydrogen production", "carbon dioxide removal", "(photo)electrochemistry"]

topic: Topic: Research Progress in the Design, Fabrication and Application of Heterojunction Photocatalysts
summary:
Photocatalysis, as one of the most promising strategies for addressing current tense energy and environment challenges, has attracted significant global attention. The key to achieving practical application is photocatalysts. In recent years, various tactics have been developed to design and prepare highly efficient photocatalysts. Among these, heterojunction is demonstrated as one smart strategy to simultaneously improve light absorption, charge separation and transfer, and spatially separated redox reactions, such as type-I, type-II, p-n junction, Z-scheme, S-scheme, relative p-n junction and configuration transformation. Heterojunction photocatalyst has received widespread and sustained attention.

The Special Issue focuses on recent progress in the design, fabrication and application of heterojunction photocatalysts. Specific areas to be covered include, but are not limited to:
● Heterojunction photocatalysts: Conductor-semiconductor, semiconductor-semiconductor, semiconductor-nonconductor and cocatalyst-photocatalyst heterojunctions, etc.
● Photocatalytic applications of heterojunctions: Photocatalytic degradation, water splitting, hydrogen production, carbon dioxide reduction, nitrogen fixation, photodynamic therapy, bacteria inactivation, coupled photoredox reactions, photosynthesis of hydrogen peroxide, urea and organics, etc.
keywords: ["Heterojunction", "nanocomposites", "photocatalysis", "photocatalytic mechanism", "photogenerated charge carriers", "photocatalytic degradation", "photocatalytic hydrogen evolution", "photoredox", "photosynthesis", "reduction of carbon dioxide"]

topic: Topic: Triboelectric Nanogenerators for Self-Powered Biomedical Devices
summary: Triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs), leveraging the triboelectric effect to convert mechanical energy into electrical power, present a novel solution for powering biomedical devices autonomously. These devices offer high energy conversion efficiency, flexible design, and excellent environmental adaptability, making them particularly well-suited for self-sustaining biomedical applications such as implanted sensors, smart medical devices, and wearable health monitors. By optimizing TENG design and materials, it is possible to significantly enhance device performance and stability while reducing reliance on external power sources. This Special Issue aims to explore the applications of TENGs in the biomedical field, examining their practical performance, challenges, and future prospects, highlighting their potential to advance long-term, stable medical monitoring and treatment.
keywords: ["Triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs)", "energy harvesting", "biomedical devices", "implantable sensors", "power IoT systems", "wearables"]

topic: Topic: Resistance Challenges of Antibody-Drug Conjugates (ADCs) in Cancer Therapy
summary:
Antibody-Drug Conjugates (ADCs) represent a promising class of targeted cancer therapies, combining the specificity of antibodies with the potency of cytotoxic drugs. Despite their potential, the clinical success of ADCs is often hampered by the development of resistance mechanisms. This Special Issue aims to explore these challenges and develop strategies to overcome ADC resistance, thereby improving their efficacy and expanding their therapeutic applications in cancer therapy.

Proposed topics include, but are not limited to:
1. Mechanisms of resistance to ADCs in cancer therapy;
2. Strategies to overcome resistance in ADC-based cancer treatments;
3. Clinical trials and outcomes of ADCs in combating drug-resistant cancers;
4. Innovations in ADC design and delivery to enhance efficacy against resistant cancers;
5. Combination of ADCs and other therapies against resistant cancers;
   etc.
keywords: ["Drug resistance", "ADC design innovations; mechanisms of resistance", "combination therapies with ADCs"]

topic: Topic: Design and Synthesis of Single-Atom Catalysts
summary:
The development of single-atom catalysts (SACs) represents a groundbreaking advancement in catalysis, characterized by the dispersion of individual metal atoms on supports. These catalysts exhibit extraordinary catalytic performance due to their maximum atomic efficiency and unique electronic properties. Stabilizing single atoms to prevent aggregation is critical and is achieved through innovative techniques such as atomic layer deposition and advanced supports.

SACs have demonstrated remarkable potential in various catalytic processes, including energy conversion, environmental remediation, and fine chemical synthesis. Their applications in reactions such as CO oxidation, hydrogen evolution, and CO2 reduction show significant improvements in activity and selectivity over traditional nanoparticle catalysts. Understanding the interactions between single atoms and supports, along with reaction mechanisms, is crucial for further advancements. Advanced characterization techniques, such as aberration-corrected transmission electron microscopy and X-ray absorption spectroscopy, provide insights into the atomic structure and catalytic behavior of SACs.

As research on SACs evolves, integrating theoretical and experimental approaches will be essential for the rational design of next-generation catalysts. The goal is to develop SACs that are highly efficient, stable, economically viable, and environmentally friendly. Through continued innovation and collaboration, single-atom catalysis holds the promise of revolutionizing industrial processes and contributing to a sustainable future.
keywords: ["Single-atom catalysts (SACs)", "dispersion of individual metal atoms", "catalytic performance", "maximum atomic efficiency", "electronic properties", "stabilization of single atoms", "prevention of aggregation", "atomic layer deposition", "advanced supports", "applications in energy conversion", "environmental remediation", "fine chemical synthesis", "significant improvements in activity and selectivity", "CO oxidation", "hydrogen evolution", "CO2 reduction", "importance of understanding atom-support interactions", "advanced characterization techniques (aberration-corrected TEM", "X-ray absorption spectroscopy)"]

topic: Topic: Structure Regulation and Performance Optimization of Organic Semiconductor Photocatalysts
summary: The photocatalytic technique offers a promising solution to the ever increasing environmental and energy challenges. It is critical to develop photocatalysts that meet the demands of practical applications. Over the past decade, various materials have been explored for photocatalytic uses. Organic semiconductor photocatalysts have gained great attention in recent years due to their easily tunable structure and function through molecular design. These organic photocatalysts include carbon nitrides, covalent organic frameworks, polymers, triazine-based frameworks, and conjugated materials, as well as their hybrids and composites. This collection aims to highlight the structure regulation and performance optimization of these materials as photocatalysts and showcase their diverse applications such as environmental remediation, water splitting, hydrogen production, organic synthesis, carbon dioxide reduction, nitrogen fixation, hydrogen peroxide photosynthesis, bacterial inactivation, tumor treatment, etc.
keywords: ["Photocatalysts", "organic semiconductors", "microstructure regulation"]

topic: Topic: New Hydrogen Storage Materials: Advance, Development and Utilization
summary: This Special Issue mainly explores the latest advancements in hydrogen storage materials, emphasizing their development and utilization in energy applications. Hydrogen storage is pivotal for the transition to sustainable energy systems, offering solutions for renewable energy integration and emission reduction. The issue covers novel materials such as metal-organic frameworks, nanostructured composites, and solid-state hydrogen storage technologies. It also addresses challenges in material stability, hydrogen capacity, and kinetics. Contributions highlight breakthroughs in synthesis methods, characterization techniques, and practical applications, providing insights into the future direction of hydrogen storage research and its role in a clean energy future. We welcome all types of manuscripts, including Research Articles, Mini-reviews/Reviews, Short Communications, Highlights, Perspectives, etc.
keywords: ["Hydrogen-related catalytic materials", "hydrogen storage materials", "the state-of-the-art nanomaterials", "catalytic mechanism", "scale application"]

topic: Topic: Computational Tools and Databases in Materials Informatics
summary:
With the proposal of the Materials Genome Initiative, computational materials science, especially machine learning, has experienced rapid advancement, which promotes materials design into the fourth paradigm (Data-driven materials science) and even the fifth paradigm (Artificial intelligence-driven materials science, AI4M). Consequently, materials design increasingly relies on the development of computational tools. However, many materials scientists lack programming skills and require well-developed tools to advance their work1. Just as Jos Vermaseren (the author of FORM, a crucial computer program for particle physics) said, "I have seen over the years, consistently, that people who spend a lot of time on computers don't get a tenure job in physics". A similar situation exists within the materials community, where the effort for developing new computational tools is not well-recognized. To address this, we have established this Special Issue based on two points: (1) Materials scientists lacking programming expertise need specialized computational tools; (2) The developers and their efforts for developing the computational tools need to be recognized.

This Special Issue aims to provide a platform for publishing advancements in the computational tools and databases in materials science. Topics to be covered include, but are not limited to, the following:
• Computational tools/software in materials science;
  • New atomic/microscopic/mesoscopic/macroscopic/multiscale computational tools, such as new first-
     principles/phase field/CALPHAD codes;
  • Pre- and post-processing tools for known codes, such as high-throughput computational workflows;
• Machine learning models, including large language models (LLMs), in materials science;
• Computational or experimental database in materials science.


References
1. A Crucial Particle Physics Computer Program Risks Obsolescence. Available from: https://www.wired.com/story/a-crucial-particle-physics-computer-program-risks-obsolescence/. [Last accessed on 1 Jan 2023]
keywords: ["Computational tools", "software", "models", "database", "machine learning"]

topic: Topic: Application of Soft Robots in Medical Field
summary:
Soft robots, characterized by their adaptable structures and dexterous capabilities, have emerged as a dynamic solution to intricate challenges encountered in minimally invasive procedures, patient rehabilitation, targeted drug delivery, and beyond. Their unique ability to navigate complex anatomical pathways and confined spaces offers unparalleled opportunities to enhance medical interventions, ultimately leading to improved outcomes and patient well-being. The issue will cover a wide range of topics, including but are not limited to:

● Novel designs and mechanisms of soft medical robots;

● Advanced control strategies for precise manipulation and navigation;

● Real-time imaging and sensing techniques for improved situational awareness;

● Human-robot interaction and collaboration in medical scenarios;

● Clinical evaluations showcasing the effectiveness and safety of soft robots.
keywords: ["Soft robots", "minimally invasive procedures", "targeted drug delivery", "real-time imaging", "human-robot interaction", "clinical application"]

topic: Topic: Exploring Urban Carbon Emissions: The Impact of Economic Development on Environmental Sustainability
summary: This Special Issue aims to delve into the intricate relationship between economic development and environmental sustainability. It explores how economic growth affects carbon emissions in urban areas and examines the multifaceted consequences for environmental sustainability. It brings together researchers from diverse disciplines, including economics, environmental science, and policy studies, to address the challenges and opportunities arising from this complex interplay. The articles examine the trade-offs between economic prosperity and urban carbon emissions, assess the effectiveness of various policies and interventions designed to reduce carbon footprints and foster green growth, and evaluate the long-term implications of current economic trajectories on urban environmental systems. The issue highlights the urgent need for sustainable development strategies that balance economic progress with carbon reduction, emphasizing the importance of collaboration and innovation in tackling this global challenge.
keywords: ["Economic growth", "environmental sustainability", "green growth", "trade-offs", "policy interventions", "urban carbon emissions", "ecological systems", "sustainable development"]

topic: Topic: Assessment of Welded Joints and Their Impact on Complex Engineering Systems Reliability
summary:
Welding is a common technique for fabricating complex structures or large dimension components in engineering systems. However, welded joints are often the most vulnerable points, potentially compromising the overall reliability of complex engineering systems. Therefore, assessing these joints and understanding their impact on system reliability is crucial. Welded joints are widely used in various industries, including power plants, fuel cells, chip manufacturing, and new energy vehicles. Their evaluation involves analyzing their structure, properties, modeling, processing and the interrelationships among these factors. Additionally, the physical and chemical properties of welded joints, determined by chemical composition and microstructure, are affected by the forming process, which is critical for reliability of complex engineering systems in practical applications.  

This Special Issue aims to provide a platform for research on the assessment of welded joints and their influence on system reliability to explore more possibilities for addressing intractable challenges complex engineering systems face.  

Topics of interest include, but are not limited to, the studies mentioned above. Other relevant areas, such as the design of novel welded constructions or modifications of welded joints, structural integrity assessment, machine learning applied to welded structures, and innovative welding methods, are also encouraged as they may expand the scope of possible applications. We warmly welcome research articles and reviews in these fields. 

We look forward to your contributions. 

Guest Editor
keywords: ["Welded joint", "engineering system", "reliability", "microstructure", "structure design", "assessment of welded joints", "modeling and processing of welding", "structural integrity assessment"]

topic: Topic: Thermoelectric Materials: Current Status and Future Challenges
summary: Thermoelectric materials have garnered significant attention due to their ability to directly convert heat into electricity, offering promising solutions for energy harvesting, waste heat recovery, and cooling applications. The development of advanced thermoelectric materials with high efficiency, stability, and scalability is crucial for enhancing the performance of thermoelectric devices. This Special Issue aims to provide a comprehensive overview of the latest progress in thermoelectric materials, including the synthesis, characterization, theoretical modeling, and device integration. We welcome contributions that explore novel materials, innovative fabrication techniques, and breakthrough applications that push the boundaries of thermoelectric technology.
keywords: ["Thermoelectric materials", "energy harvesting", "waste heat recovery", "thermoelectric efficiency", "material synthesis", "thermal conductivity", "Seebeck coefficient", "device integration", "nanoscale materials", "power generation"]

topic: Topic: Comprehensive Advances in Urologic Oncology
summary: This Special Issue focuses on the latest advancements in urologic oncology, highlighting both innovative diagnostic and therapeutic strategies. As the field evolves, emerging technologies and methodologies are increasingly enhancing the precision and efficacy of urologic cancer treatments. This issue will spotlight significant updates in surgical techniques, particularly minimally invasive and robotic-assisted surgeries, which are transforming patient outcomes through improved precision, reduced morbidity, and shorter recovery periods. Additionally, we will delve into the integration of new imaging modalities and AI-assisted technologies that are crucial for enhancing diagnostic capabilities and refining treatment planning. The collection also features research on novel therapeutic approaches and pharmacological innovations aimed at treating various urologic malignancies. Contributions will examine the clinical outcomes, safety profiles, and cost-effectiveness of these newer treatments, all aimed at providing healthcare professionals with a comprehensive overview to enhance patient care through surgical and technological innovations in urologic oncology.
keywords: ["Prostate Cancer", "bladder cancer", "renal cancer", "testicular cancer", "penile cancer", "urologic oncology", "robotic surgery", "minimally invasive surgery", "surgical innovation", "robotic platforms", "imaging modalities", "AI-assisted surgery", "clinical outcomes"]

topic: Topic: Advanced Manufacturing: Big Data and Analytics in Industry 4.0
summary:
In the context of the digital transformation within the industrial sector, Industry 4.0 has ushered in a new era characterized by the fusion of physical and digital systems. The exponential growth of data, coupled with advancements in analytics, has positioned big data as a cornerstone for innovation and efficiency.

This Special Issue, "Advanced Manufacturing: Big Data and Analytics in Industry 4.0", provides a scholarly examination of the intersection between big data and analytics within the Industry 4.0 framework. It recognizes the urgent need for sustainable and intelligent industrial practices that address the environmental challenges confronting the 21st century. The issue elucidates the instrumental role of analytics in cultivating a circular economy, promoting eco-design principles, and facilitating the transition towards environmentally benign manufacturing processes. It demonstrates the capacity of analytics to serve as a vanguard for innovation, extending beyond technological progress to include the development of business models that embrace sustainability and circularity.

In conclusion, this Special Issue presents a prospective analysis on the integration of big data and analytics within the Industry 4.0 paradigm. It delivers strategic insights for industry leaders, policymakers, and researchers, advocating for a future where industrial growth is in harmony with environmental conservation and the achievement of sustainable development goals.
keywords: ["Big data", "Industry 4.0", "environmental sustainability", "supply chain optimization", "energy efficiency", "smart maintenance"]

topic: Topic: Intelligent Construction and Operation of Urban Tunnels and Pipelines
summary:
Urban tunnels and pipelines are critical infrastructures, serving as vital lifelines for urban transportation, energy supply, flood prevention, and communication. They play a crucial role in addressing the challenges of high-density urban living and are essential for achieving sustainable urban development. Currently, urban tunnels and pipelines are transitioning into a phase where incremental construction and quality improvement of existing infrastructure are equally important. 

Given the complexities of urban underground environments, long spans of structures, and lag in digitization, managing the entire lifespan of urban tunnels and pipelines presents numerous challenges. However, with the advancement of key information technologies, such as advanced sensing, big data, and artificial intelligence, advanced construction methods can enable the integrated and efficient collaboration of design, construction, and operational services. 

This Special Issue aims to showcase recent research progress related to the smart construction and operation of the urban tunnels and pipelines. Topics of interest include (but are not limited to):

● Intelligent perception and data fusion of urban tunnels and pipelines;

● Intelligent construction and assembly construction of urban tunnels and pipelines;

● Three-dimensional modeling, digital twinning and performance deduction of urban tunnels and pipelines;

● Toughness improvement of service performance of urban tunnels and pipelines;

● Intelligent operation and maintenance of urban tunnels and pipelines.
keywords: ["Tunnels", "pipelines", "intelligent construction", "smart operation and maintenance", "artificial intelligence", "digital twin", "machine learning", "deep learning", "computer vision"]

topic: Topic: Catalytic Transformation of Biomass to High-Valuable Chemicals
summary:
The catalytic transformation of biomass into high-value chemicals represents a critical area of research of sustainable and renewable resource utilization. In recent years, with the depletion of traditional fossil fuels and the ongoing development of the global economy, the need for alternative resources has become increasingly urgent. Biomass, characterized by its diverse nature and abundance, has emerged as a particularly promising alternative for producing various chemicals, fuels, and materials. Current research focuses on developing efficient catalytic materials for converting biomass selectively into high-value chemicals, which is essential for reducing dependence on fossil resources and lowering carbon footprints.

This Special Issue aims to highlight the latest advancements and research findings in catalytic conversion of biomass into valuable chemical products through selective cleavage of C-C and C-O bonds and the construction of new C-N, C-H bonds. We invite contributions in the form of original research and review articles on the following themes, among others:

● Selective cleavage of C-O and C-C bonds of biomass or platform molecules;

● Heterogeneous/homogeneous catalytic conversion of biomass ;

● Photocatalytic/electrocatalytic/thermal catalytic conversion of biomass;

● Comprehensive mechanistic studies;

● Green reaction routes for producing valuable compounds; 

● Exploration of different biomass sources (lignocellulosic materials, agricultural residues, and organic waste).
keywords: ["Biomass", "high-value chemicals", "catalyst", "catalytic mechanism", "reaction kinetics", "sustainable resources", "renewable energy", "environmental sustainability"]

topic: Topic: Flexible Strain Sensors in Human-Machine Interaction
summary: Flexible strain sensors are revolutionizing human-machine interaction by enabling machines to more accurately interpret and respond to human movements. These sensors, integral to wearable devices that monitor physical activity to robotic systems that collaborate with humans, offer high sensitivity and flexibility, making them critical in enhancing machine responsiveness and user experience. This Special Issue invites contributions that explore the latest research and technological innovations in flexible strain sensors for human-machine interaction, covering topics from material science to device integration and applications.
keywords: ["Flexible strain sensors", "human-machine interaction (HMI)", "wearable devices", "motion detection", "sensor integration"]

topic: Topic: Microscopic Insights into Photocatalytic Hydrogen Production: Mechanisms, Materials, and Cutting-Edge Technologies
summary:
Photocatalytic hydrogen production is promising for green hydrogen generation. Developing efficient photocatalysts is crucial to achieving high solar-to-hydrogen (STH) conversion efficiencies. Although numerous photocatalysts have been developed in the past decades, low-cost, efficient and robust photocatalysts are still lacking, which hinders the development of this important field. A deeper understanding of light absorption, charge separation and surface charge transfer behaviors at the microscale is essential to further enhance the photocatalytic activity of these materials. The advancement of cutting-edge microscopy and spectroscopy technologies can provide direct evidence of the photocatalytic water splitting reaction mechanisms, which is significant for research into photocatalytic hydrogen production. This collection aims to highlight the microscopic insights of photocatalytic hydrogen production to provide a deeper understanding of the mechanisms. The development of new materials and advanced technologies is also important for achieving efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production. The Special Issue will include (but is not limited to) the following subjects:

● Novel photocatalysts with high hydrogen production efficiencies;

● Mechanism understanding of photocatalytic procedures;

● Cutting-edge technologies to promote the development of photocatalytic hydrogen production.
keywords: ["Photocatalysts", "hydrogen production", "mechanisms", "microscopic insights"]

topic: Topic: The Health Risks of Heavy Metal Exposure
summary:
Metals, being non-biodegradable, persist indefinitely in the environment, facilitating their entry into the human food chain and their ubiquitous presence in our diet. Food safety monitoring programs consistently detect cadmium (Cd), lead (Pb), and mercury (Hg) in common foods. These metals have no nutritional value or physiological role in the body and are highly toxic. They enter cells and disrupt many essential metal-dependent biological processes through molecular mimicry. Despite the indisputable health threats posed by dietary exposure, there is no consensus on safe exposure levels or critical endpoints necessary to establish a realistic benchmark dose limit (BMDL). Multiple studies have reported the health risks associated with even very low-level metal exposure. It is imperative to redefine acceptable exposure levels, aligning with the no-observed-adverse-effect level (NOAEL).

The goal of this Special Issue is to provide a platform to present and review the latest cutting-edge research on the exposure risks of metal pollutants throughout all stages of life, from prenatal exposure to older age. Exposure can occur through breast milk, infant formulas, common foods, food supplements, medications, and polluted air and water. We welcome both fundamental and empirical studies, as well as stakeholder and policy-relevant commentary on potential increases and reductions in population exposure. Manuscripts focusing on vulnerable groups, including women and children, are particularly encouraged.
keywords: ["Heavy metals", "food-chain transfer", "cadmium", "Itai-itai disease", "mercury", "Minamata disease", "lead", "child intelligence", "safe exposure level", "benchmark dose"]

topic: Topic: Human-machine Interface for Robotic Systems
summary:
A human-machine interface for robotic systems is a technology or system that allows humans to interact with and control robots effectively. This interface serves as a bridge between the human operator and the robotic system, enabling communication, command input, data visualization, and feedback exchange.

Human-machine interfaces can take various forms, including physical control panels, touchscreens, gesture recognition systems, voice commands, or brain-computer interfaces. The primary goal of these interfaces is to simplify the operation of complex robotic systems, making them more intuitive and user-friendly for human operators.

Effective human-machine interfaces are essential for a wide range of applications, from industrial automation and manufacturing to healthcare, entertainment, and even everyday consumer products such as smart home devices. They play a crucial role in enhancing productivity, safety, and overall user experience when interacting with robotic technologies.
keywords: ["Control methods", "sensory feedback", "cognitive assistance", "human-robot collaboration", "adaptive interfaces"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Oral Cancer, Precancerous Lesions, Oral Disease, and Oral Pathology
summary:
Oral cancer and precancerous lesions represent critical areas of concern in dental and oral pathology. Early detection and accurate diagnosis are paramount for effective management and improved patient outcomes. This Special Issue, focusing on "Oral Cancer, Precancerous Lesions, Oral Disease, and Oral Pathology," invites submissions from dentists, researchers, and clinicians who are engaged in the study and treatment of oral lesions.

The issue aims to provide a comprehensive exploration of recent advancements in the diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment of oral cancer and related conditions. Topics of interest include the identification of early-stage precancerous lesions, innovative diagnostic tools, and the latest therapeutic approaches. Contributions on the molecular and genetic underpinnings of oral cancer, risk factors, and strategies for prevention are also encouraged. Furthermore, this issue will examine the role of interdisciplinary approaches in improving patient care and outcomes.

We welcome original research, reviews, and case studies that deepen our understanding of oral cancer and its precursors, helping to shape the future of oral pathology and disease management.
keywords: ["Oral cancer", "precancerous lesions", "oral pathology", "oral disease", "diagnostics", "early detection", "dental research"]

topic: Topic: Antibiotics in Aquatic Environment Status and Challenges
summary: The pervasive presence of antibiotics in aquatic environments has become a critical issue, impacting the health of both aquatic ecosystems and humans. This Special Issue delves into the current status of antibiotic contamination, exploring its sources, pathways, and ecological ramifications in water bodies. It features cutting-edge research on the prevalence, distribution, and environmental impact of antibiotics, alongside discussions on the rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. Additionally, the issue addresses advancements in wastewater treatment technologies, including the innovative use of enzymatic degradation to break down antibiotic compounds. It also highlights the regulatory challenges in curbing antibiotic discharge and underscores the need for new strategies, such as enzymatic processes, to mitigate antibiotic pollution. From a forward-looking perspective, this issue calls for interdisciplinary action, policy reforms, and greater public engagement to safeguard aquatic ecosystems and public health.
keywords: ["Antibiotic contamination", "aquatic ecosystems", "antibiotic resistance", "enzymatic degradation", "environmental monitoring", "wastewater treatment", "technological advancements"]

topic: Topic: Prospects of Novel Nanostructures in Enhancing Lithium Metal and Li-Sulfur Battery Performances
summary:
Lithium-sulfur (Li-S) batteries have garnered significant attention due to their high theoretical energy density and the potential to revolutionize energy storage technology. However, the commercialization of Li-S batteries faces several challenges, including the shuttle effect of polysulfides, low conductivity of sulfur, and dendrite formation of lithium metal anodes. These obstacles necessitate the development of innovative nanostructures to enhance battery performance and stability.

In recent years, significant progress has been made in utilizing nanostructures to address these issues. Novel nanomaterials such as carbon-based composites, metal oxides, and sulfides have shown promise in improving sulfur utilization, mitigating the shuttle effect, and enhancing the safety and longevity of Li-S batteries. This Special Issue aims to explore the latest advancements in nanostructure design and their impact on the performance of these batteries.

We invite contributions that focus on the synthesis, characterization, and application of novel nanostructures in Li-S batteries. The scope of this Special Issue includes but is not limited to the following topics:
● Design and development of nanostructured cathodes and anodes for Li-S batteries;
● Strategies to suppress the polysulfide shuttle effect;
● Nanostructure-enabled enhancements in sulfur loading and utilization;
● Safety and stability improvements through nanotechnology;
● Theoretical studies and modeling of nanostructures in Li-S batteries.

We believe that the insights gathered in this Special Issue will pave the way for future innovations in Li-S battery technology, ultimately contributing to the development of high-performance, sustainable energy storage solutions.
keywords: ["Lithium-sulfur batteries", "nanostructures"]

topic: Topic: Recent Advancements in Vehicle Control Design & Automation
summary:
Car companies are under increasing pressure to produce cleaner, more fuel-efficient vehicles that also excel in safety, security, comfort, handling, and drivability. Meeting these broad and ambitious requirements is a challenging task, necessitating significant technological advances. To meet these demands, innovation within the auto industry is accelerating, and connectivity, automation, and electrification are increasingly being leveraged in ways that will transform the next generation of transportation systems. However, realizing this transformation hinges on overcoming complex vehicle control design and automation problems. 

This Special Issue invites original applied or theoretical contributions, as well as comprehensive tutorial and survey articles, involving advanced, elegant control design and automation methods (model-based, optimization-based, learning-based, or hybrid approaches) for challenging automotive applications, including, but not limited to, vehicle automation and driver assistance systems, powertrain control, energy management systems, battery management systems, thermal and Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC) control, active suspension, chassis control, resilient and secure control, platooning, multi-vehicle coordination, and other complex vehicle control challenges.
keywords: ["Vehicle automation and driver assistance systems", "powertrain control", "energy management system", "battery management system", "thermal and HVAC control", "active suspension", "chassis control", "resilient and secure control", "platooning", "and multi-vehicle coordination."]

source: Rare Disease and Orphan Drugs Journal
topic: Topic: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: Early Diagnosis and Management Strategies
summary: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) is a complex, chronic autoimmune disease that affects multiple organs and systems throughout the body, including the skin, joints, heart, kidneys, serous membranes, nerves, blood vessels, etc. The wide range of clinical manifestations associated with SLE makes its diagnosis, management, and treatment particularly challenging. The 2019 EULAR/ACR SLE classification criteria for SLE are commonly used for diagnosis, requiring a combination of clinical and immunological factors, with an entry criterion of antinuclear antibodies at a titer of ≥1:80 to confirm autoimmune involvement, and a score of 10 or more points is necessary for diagnosis. Early diagnosis and timely initiation of treatment are crucial, as delaying intervention until severe organ involvement occurs can significantly worsen outcomes. The 10-year survival rate for SLE is 70-90%, a concerning statistic considering the typical age of onset, which ranges from the 20s to the 40s. Infection remains the leading cause of death among SLE patients. For more than half a century, non-specific glucocorticoids have been the cornerstone of SLE treatment, but are associated with serious side effects, including infection, and are the biggest determinant of prognosis. In recent years, however, the development of effective immunosuppressive drugs and biological agents has changed the treatment strategy. EULAR’s 2023 Recommendations for the Management of SLE advocate for the use of glucocorticoids as a bridging therapy during periods of high disease activity, with the aim of achieving and maintaining remission, mainly with antimalarials, biologics, and immunosuppressive drugs – except in cases of active renal impairment. Nonetheless, the therapeutic efficacy of existing drugs is insufficient in some cases, prompting the rapid development of new biologics, kinase inhibitors, and other small-molecule compounds. Furthermore, adoptive T cell transfer therapy - engineered to target the CD19 antigen via chimeric antigen receptors (CAR-T therapy) - has been applied to refractory SLE. This therapy works by rapidly and sustainably reducing circulating B cells, thereby resetting the immune system through the reorganization of autoreactive B cells and inducing sustained clinical remission. This Special Issue aims to highlight the latest advancements in understanding the pathogenesis of SLE, innovative therapeutic approaches, and the development of personalized medicine for managing this multifaceted disease. Through this collection, we hope to contribute to the ongoing efforts to improve patient outcomes and quality of life for those affected by SLE. We anticipate that the emerging trends in SLE treatment presented here will inspire and motivate young clinicians and researchers.
keywords: ["SLE", "diagnosis", "management", "treatment", "recommendations", "biologics", "immunosuppressants"]

topic: Topic: Green Manufacturing in Consumer Electronics
summary:
The consumer electronics industry, a cornerstone of modern life, is witnessing unprecedented growth, driven by rapid technological advancements and rising consumer demand. However, this surge in production and consumption has raised significant environmental concerns, including resource depletion, energy consumption, and electronic waste. In response, the industry is increasingly prioritizing sustainable practices, particularly green manufacturing, to minimize its environmental footprint. Green manufacturing integrates eco-friendly materials, energy-efficient processes, and waste reduction strategies, paving the way for a more sustainable future in consumer electronics.

The Special Issue on "Green Manufacturing in Consumer Electronics" focuses on the integration of environmentally friendly practices within the production of consumer electronics. This collection of articles addresses key challenges and advancements in mitigating the environmental impact of manufacturing electronic devices. Topics covered include the development and implementation of sustainable materials, energy-efficient production techniques, and waste reduction strategies. The issue also explores the role of policy and regulation in promoting green manufacturing, as well as the economic benefits of adopting such practices. Through case studies and research findings, the Special Issue provides insights into the innovative technologies and practices that are paving the way for a more sustainable future in the consumer electronics industry. The goal is to highlight the importance of green manufacturing not only for environmental preservation but also for enhancing corporate responsibility and consumer satisfaction.

Proposed topics include, but are not limited to:
1. Sustainable materials and alternatives to hazardous substances;
2. Energy-efficient manufacturing processes;
3. Design for environment (DfE) and life cycle assessment (LCA) in consumer electronics;
4. Innovations in recycling and waste management of electronic products;
5. Green supply chain management and circular economy approaches;
6. Regulatory frameworks and industry standards promoting green manufacturing;
7. Case studies and best practices in implementing green manufacturing technologies.
keywords: ["Green manufacturing", "electronics", "environmentally friendly", "energy-efficient production", "waste reduction strategies"]

topic: Topic: Small-scale Magnetic Soft Robotics
summary:
Magnetic soft robotics is a fascinating and emerging field that explores soft robots capable of deforming and navigating in response to external magnetic fields. When these robots are further downscale to small scales, i.e., millimeters and below, their unique ability to navigate tortuous, confined and complex spaces with unprecedented adaption, agility and safety promises unparalleled benefits across various engineering domains, including space exploration, additive manufacturing, biomedicine, and so on. Research into small-scale magnetically actuated soft robots is highly active within both academic and industrial communities, which involves a variety of disciplines, such as materials, physics, mechanics, mechanical engineering, and control science. Although the past decade has witnessed a rapid evolution of this field, a noteworthy gap still lies in the way toward real-world applications. We believe miniaturized magnetic soft robots hold vast potential for the near future, though achieving this potential will require considerable research efforts.

This Special Issue focuses on the current state of the art in materials synthesis, manufacturing technologies, control theories and functional applications of small-scale magnetic soft robots. It aims to attract contributions from both academic and industrial researchers to advance existing knowledge and present innovative ideas for future developments and technologies. We invite authors to submit original research and review articles covering the potential topics related to small-scale magnetic soft robotics.
keywords: ["Soft robotics", "magnetic actuation and control", "magnetic composite materials", "3D printing; photolithography", "mechanical and deformation model", "control methodology and automation", "engineering applications"]

topic: Topic: Clinical Challenges in Regenerative Fat Grafting
summary:
Since Coleman reported the first systematized technique for fat grafting in 1992, the procedure has remained unpredictable, with varying degrees of fat resorption and the need for multiple procedures. These challenges have prompted extensive research aimed at improving fat graft survival. It was not until 2001 that the discovery of adipose tissue as the largest source of autologous adipose-derived stem cells led to the widespread acceptance of new applications of fat grafting.

Advancements such as cell-assisted lipotransfer, stromal vascular fraction isolation, and recipient site enhancement have significantly improved fat grafting results. Today, fat grafting has become a major tool in Plastic and Aesthetic surgery.

The goal of this Special Issue is to highlight key aspects of successful fat grafting techniques, explore their broader applications, and present major recent developments and future trends.

Areas covered include:
● Harvesting techniques;
● Volume of fat transferred;
● Processing techniques;
● Injection techniques;
● Fat graft supplementation;
● Recipient site enhancement;
● Nutritional enhancement;
● Novel devices and techniques.
keywords: ["Fat grafting", "fat injection", "fat transfer", "autologous stem cell transplant", "micro-fat", "nano-fat", "external expansion", "cell-assisted transfer", "micronizer"]

topic: Topic: Functional Coordination Polymers: Advances in Design, Synthesis, and Application
summary:
Functional coordination polymers, including metal-organic frameworks, porous coordination polymers, metal-organic materials, etc., are characterized by their tunable structures, high porosity, and multifunctionality. Over the past two decades, these materials have evolved significantly, making them ideal for diverse applications such as gas storage, catalysis, sensing, and drug delivery. This Special Issue highlights the latest progress and pioneering research in functional coordination polymer materials, showcasing recent advancements in their design, synthesis, and applications. We invite submissions of Research Papers, Reviews, Perspectives, Mini Reviews, and other contributions that provide comprehensive insights into this rapidly developing field.

Topics to be covered include, but are not limited to:
1. Advanced and sustainable synthetic strategies for functional coordination polymers;
2. Structural and multifunctional design of porous coordination polymers;
3. Developments in gas storage/separation, catalysis and biomedicine applications, etc.
keywords: ["Metal-organic frameworks", "coordination polymers", "design and synthesis", "gas storage and separation", "catalysis"]

topic: Topic: Urban Seismic Risk and Resilience Analysis
summary:
Seismic risk and resilience analyses at the urban scale provide essential information for public authorities and policymakers (e.g., for planning earthquake disaster response and mitigation actions, the development of urban planning and land use management). These analyses also benefit insurance and real-estate industries and structural engineers. This Special Issue welcomes articles on the latest research and cutting-edge technological advancements, including the use of artificial intelligence and machine learning, to enhance the assessment and management of risk and resilience in urban systems. In this context, urban systems encompass both the built environment and all related, interacting infrastructures. Socio-economic aspects may also be considered, given their significant role in risk and resilience assessment.

Potential topics covered in this Special Issue include, but are not limited to:

● Systemic risk assessment of urban systems;

● Ground motion and site amplification modeling at large scales;

● Spatial variability of ground motion; 

● City effects on ground motion; 

● Soil-Foundation-Structure interaction for different aboveground and underground systems at the urban scale;

● Consideration of aging effects; 

● Best practices for strengthening and upgrading structures and infrastructures at the urban scale;

● Artificial intelligence-aided urban risk and resilience assessment;

● Rapid loss and resilience quantification;

● Resilience assessment and management at the urban scale;

● Life-cycle risk and resilience evaluation;

● Multi-hazards and cascading effects.
keywords: ["Seismic risk", "urban environment", "infrastructures and utilities", "systemic approach", "resilience", "vulnerability and losses", "spatial variability of ground motion", "site effects", "SFSI and city effects", "socio-economic impact", "AI", "life-cycle"]

topic: Topic: Adaptive Event-Triggered Control and Optimization for Complex Systems
summary:
Complex systems are widely present in modern industries, energy, communications, transportation, and other fields, characterized by multi-dimensionality, nonlinear and dynamic behavior. In recent years, the importance of seeking efficient control and optimal strategies has become increasingly prominent. The control and optimization of complex systems must address inherent uncertainties while maintaining efficiency and stability in resource-constrained environments.

From the perspective of control science, event-triggered control has been widely applied in areas such as network control systems, multi-agent system collaborative control, and communication. Its advantage relies on system dynamics and the current triggering moment to calculate the next triggering moment, and has played an important role in reducing control costs, minimizing communication resource consumption, and enhancing system stability.

From an intelligent optimization standpoint, achieving optimal performance in complex environments while adhering to constraints on resources, time, and cost requires advanced optimization techniques. These techniques not only need to search for optimal solutions in a multi-dimensional design space but also handle uncertainties, randomness, and multi-objective requirements.

This Special Issue aims to explore the latest research advancements and practical applications of adaptive event-triggered control and optimization methods in complex systems, driving the field towards more intelligent and efficient directions, and providing new ideas and solutions to meet future control and optimization challenges in complex systems. The scope encompasses theoretical developments and practical applications in domains such as smart grids, complex networks and multi-agent systems.

Potential topics to be covered in this Special Issue include, but are not limited to:

● Adaptive event-triggered control of nonlinear dynamic systems;

● Modeling of complex systems with event-triggering mechanisms;

● Event-triggered control of networked control systems;

● Event-triggered control and its application in game theory;

● Event-triggered control of multi-agent systems;

● Applications of event-triggered control in other control fields;

● Applications of artificial intelligence in control and optimization;

● Optimization of complex systems and complex networks;

● Optimization methods and applications with artificial intelligence.
keywords: ["Complex systems", "complex networks", "adaptive event-triggered control", "optimization control", "intelligent optimization", "dynamic games", "swarm intelligent control", "networked control system", "multi-agent systems", "artificial intelligence"]

topic: Topic: The Race to Decarbonization in the Next Phase of Globalization
summary:
Climate change leads to increasingly more frequent extreme climate events, prompting a shift from global consensus to global action on the low-carbon transition. While the global economy is currently experiencing a period of reverse globalization, the concept of low-carbon globalization is gaining growing acceptance and agreement. In this context, various stakeholders remain interconnected and embedded in global supply and value chains to varying extents, with production, technology, trade, and investment all playing interdependent roles in the processes of globalization. Low-carbon globalization, much like economic globalization, operates as a "double-edged sword". Although more countries are accepting responsibilities for the transition, many fail to fulfill their obligations, resulting in an uneven dislocation of the benefits and burdens of a low-carbon economy. As interactions occur across multiple scales, global challenges increasingly manifest through regional clusters, while regional responses often have global ripple effects. The widespread adoption of renewable energy technologies and carbon capture and storage (CCS) methods has led to varying impacts on the carbon footprints of countries and regions in both the Global North and South. Rising international disputes and geopolitical conflicts are reshaping the understanding of de-globalization, regionalization, and re-globalization, necessitating a deeper exploration of the characteristics of the next phase of globalization. There is also an urgent need to assess the potential advantages and challenges that lie ahead in the race toward decarbonization.

Proposed topics include, but are not limited to:
● Impacts of carbon tariffs on international trade and carbon emissions;
● Trade-offs in low-carbon technologies and CCUS deployment;
● The growing carbon footprint of artificial intelligence;
● Carbon outsourcing from the perspective of developing countries;
● Spatial spillover effects of carbon emissions and carbon footprints;
● Shared responsibilities for carbon footprints between the Global South and North;
● Cost-benefit allocation in collaborative carbon emission reduction efforts;
● Pathways and challenges for Belt and Road countries in the low-carbon transition;
● Inequality and optimization in the global low-carbon transition;
● Measurement and implications of low-carbon globalization.
keywords: ["Low-carbon transition", "carbon neutrality", "carbon footprint", "decarbonization", "levelized cost of energy", "low-carbon technologies"]

topic: Topic: Robust Localization and Mapping for Robots in Dynamic Environments
summary:
As the demand for autonomous robots in real-world environments grows, the realization of simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) for robots in dynamic environments has become a challenging and much-anticipated research area. Traditional SLAM methods mainly target static environments, while the variability of real-world environments, such as moving objects and dynamic obstacles, greatly affects the accuracy and stability of SLAM systems. Therefore, investigating how to achieve robust localization and mapping techniques in non-static environments is crucial for the wide application of autonomous robots.

This Special Issue aims to bring together and explore the latest research advances and technological breakthroughs in realizing robust SLAM in dynamic environments. It will focus on innovative SLAM algorithms, sensor fusion techniques, light detection and ranging (LiDAR) and visual SLAM methods, and strategies to improve the robustness of SLAM systems in dynamic environments. By highlighting these cutting-edge technologies, the Special Issue seeks to promote the application and development of SLAM technology in dynamic settings and further enhance the adaptability and operational efficiency of autonomous robots in real-world scenarios. Potential topics of this Special Issue include but are not limited to the following:
● Sensor Fusion in Dynamic Environments;
● Application of Vision and LiDAR in SLAM;
● Real-time Optimization of SLAM Performance;
● Detection and Avoidance of Dynamic Obstacles;
● Localization and Tracking for Robots in Non-Static Environments.
keywords: ["SLAM", "sensor fusion", "LiDAR", "motion tracking", "obstacle detection", "path planning"]

source: Extracellular Vesicles and Circulating Nucleic Acids
topic: Topic: Isolation and Characterization of Extracellular Vesicles: From Current Methods to Emerging Technologies
summary:
Extracellular vesicles (EVs) have experienced ever increasing interest over recent years, with advancements in isolation and characterization technologies driving new insights into their biological functions and their potential for clinical translations. As the field continues to expand and the unique complexity of EVs is revealed, the need for efficient and broadly applicable methodologies to study EVs becomes critical. This special issue aims to provide a snapshot of current state-of-the-art methodologies for the isolation and characterization of EVs and to highlight cutting-edge technologies that are foreseen to unlock EVs use in clinical applications. 

We invite researchers to contribute original papers, (mini)reviews, or critical perspectives that provide innovative solutions to both longstanding and emerging challenges in the EV field. By fostering a collective understanding of the most powerful and promising methodologies, this issue seeks to propel the field forward and realize the full clinical potential of EVs.
keywords: ["Extracellular vesicles", "isolation and separation", "EVs characterization", "EVs phenotyping", "omics", "liquid biopsy", "biomarker discovery", "drug delivery"]

source: Journal of Surveillance, Security and Safety
topic: Topic: Information and Communication Systems Security
summary:
The rapid adoption of Information and Communication Technology (ICT) has brought opportunities for innovation, efficiency, and connectivity. However, this swift development has also introduced significant security challenges. The fast pace of technological change often outstrips the ability of society and system operators to keep up, leading to inevitable security issues. As organizations and infrastructures increasingly depend on ICT, the potential risks associated with cyber threats have grown exponentially. Concerns have risen about the adequacy of secure and resilient procedures, as well as the extent to which information security and user privacy can be guaranteed.

The Special Issue on “Information and Communication Systems Security” aims to provide an international forum for researchers and practitioners to exchange information on the latest advancements in security, privacy, and resilience within ICT. This issue covers a wide range of topics, including network security, secure software development, and risk management, among others. We welcome contributions on all aspects of Information and Communication Systems Security, including but not restricted to:
● Access Control;
● Adversarial machine learning;

● Incident response and digital forensics;
● Information Hiding;
● Identity Management;
● Information Flow Control;
● Information Security Governance and Management;
● Intrusion Detection;
● Malware and Unwanted Software;
● Network Security;
● Phishing and Spam Detection and Prevention;
● Risk Analysis and Management;
● Security architecture designs;
● Security Economics and Metrics;
● Security in Cloud / Fog Systems;

● Security in Crowdsourcing;
● Security in IoT, Cyber-Physical Systems, and industrial control systems;
● Security in Location-based Services;
● Security information, event management, and analytics;

topic: Topic: Exosomes in Combating Cancer Drug Resistance
summary:
Exosomes have emerged as promising vehicles in the fight against cancer drug resistance due to their unique ability to deliver therapeutic agents directly to target cells. These small extracellular vesicles can encapsulate drugs, genetic materials, or therapeutic proteins and transport them efficiently to cancer cells. This targeted delivery system has the potential to overcome some of the limitations of traditional drug delivery methods, such as poor drug penetration and off-target effects. By utilizing exosomes to transport therapies specifically designed to counteract resistance mechanisms, researchers aim to enhance the effectiveness of cancer treatments and address the challenge of drug-resistant tumors more effectively.

However, the role of exosomes in drug resistance extends beyond their therapeutic applications. Exosomes can also contribute to the development of drug resistance by carrying and disseminating resistance factors among cancer cells. These vesicles can transport molecules that help cancer cells evade the effects of chemotherapy or targeted treatments, thereby exacerbating the problem of resistance. Understanding how exosomes facilitate this process is crucial for developing strategies to counteract their role in drug resistance and improve patient outcomes. By studying both sides of the exosomal impact, researchers are better equipped to harness their potential while mitigating the challenges they pose in cancer therapy.

Subtopics suggested by our Editorial Office are listed below but not limited to:

1. Generation and secretion of exosomes in cancer drug resistance;

2. Mechanisms of exosome generation in different cancer types;

3. Mechanism of exosomes transferring drug resistance-related proteins;

4. Exosome-based approaches in the management of Cancer Drug resistance;

5. The role of exosomes in sequestration and delivery of chemotherapeutic drugs;

6. Exosomal delivery in cancer therapy;

7. Contribution of exosomes in drug resistance to targeted therapies;

8. Milk exosomes as delivery vehicles for small and macromolecules; 

    etc.
keywords: ["Exosomes", "cancer", "drug resistance", "drug-resistance-related proteins", "chemotherapeutic drug delivery", "targeted therapy resistance"]

topic: Topic: Processing and Applications of Nanocellulose
summary:
Nanocellulose, derived from natural sources such as wood, plants, and algae, is a promising material known for its exceptional mechanical properties, biodegradability, and versatility. Its unique characteristics, including a high surface area and customizable surface chemistry, have enabled its use in a wide range of applications, spanning biomedicine, packaging, electronics, and environmental protection. This Special Issue delves into the latest processing techniques, composite material development, and innovative applications of nanocellulose across multiple industries, offering a comprehensive technical overview while promoting a broader understanding and adoption of this material as a sustainable choice. 

By exploring mechanical, chemical, and enzymatic processing methods, along with innovations in nanocellulose-based composites, we aim to present the forefront of nanocellulose technology and facilitate its optimized use in industrial applications.
keywords: ["Nanocellulose", "biodegradability", "mechanical properties", "processing techniques", "biomedical applications", "packaging", "environmental protection", "sustainable materials"]

topic: Topic: Innovations in Robotic Surgery for Localized Renal Cancer
summary:
The Special Issue focuses on the latest advancements in robotic surgery techniques and technologies used in the management of localized renal cancer. As minimally invasive surgery continues to evolve, robotic-assisted approaches offer significant benefits, such as enhanced precision, reduced patient morbidity, and shorter recovery times.

This issue brings together a collection of articles that explore novel robotic surgical techniques, including partial nephrectomy, radical nephrectomy, and ablation procedures, as well as the development of new robotic platforms, imaging modalities, and AI-assisted technologies that are reshaping the surgical landscape.

Additionally, it covers clinical outcomes, safety profiles, cost-effectiveness analyses, and future perspectives of robotic surgery in renal cancer. The aim is to provide a comprehensive overview for surgeons, researchers, and healthcare professionals interested in the integration of innovative robotic technologies in urologic oncology to improve patient outcomes and advance clinical practice.
keywords: ["Robotic surgery", "localized renal cancer", "minimally invasive surgery", "partial nephrectomy", "radical nephrectomy", "surgical innovation", "urologic oncology", "robotic platforms", "imaging modalities", "AI-assisted surgery", "clinical outcomes", "surgical techniques"]

topic: Topic: Pioneering Sustainable Solutions for Pharmaceuticals Removal from Wastewater
summary:
Pharmaceuticals are a significant class of emerging contaminants that pose serious concerns because their presence in natural waters can cause adverse physiological effects in non-targeted individuals. The production and consumption of these compounds have surged during the last decades, leading to their increased presence in wastewater. Unfortunately, wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) have not been able to keep pace with this increase. Conventional WWTPs were not designed to remove pharmaceuticals effectively, making their effluents a main source of environmental contamination. With the anticipated continued increase in pharmaceutical use due to global population growth, aging, and improved access to healthcare, it is crucial to address their release to prevent detrimental impacts on the environment, ecosystems, and human health. Therefore, new regulations for wastewater treatment are expected. In this context, developing and implementing efficient treatments for removing pharmaceuticals from wastewater is imperative.
