In a world with finite natural resources, such treatments should strive to meet sustainability criteria at environmental, economic, social, and technical levels.

This Special Issue focuses on innovative and sustainable treatments for removing pharmaceuticals from wastewater. It aims to cover a wide range of topics, including novel and sustainable solutions, the assessment of their performance and/or (toxic) effects on the environment and human health, and compliance with future regulatory standards.

Topics

We invite researchers to contribute their findings on sustainable wastewater treatments for pharmaceuticals removal to this Special Issue. Potential topics include, but are not limited to:

● Sustainable processes and/or materials;

● Advances in bioremediation and phytoremediation-based treatments;

● Novel insights into advanced wastewater treatments;

● Case studies and real-world applications of innovative treatments; 

● Impacts on the environment and human health;

● Ecotoxicological risk assessments of treated wastewater;

● Toxicity approaches to evaluate pharmaceuticals removal efficiency;

● Life cycle assessments of processes and/or materials;

● Wastewater safety and reuse.
keywords: ["Contaminants of emerging concern", "active pharmaceutical ingredients", "advanced wastewater treatments", "wastewater reuse", "safe water", "sustainability"]

topic: Topic: The Occurrence, Exposure, and Health Challenges of Flame Retardants and Plasticiser
summary:
Environmental contaminants have been surrounding us for many years. Specifically, Bisphenol A and phthalates, also known as plasticizers, have been widely used in the plastics industry for many years. On the other hand, flame retardants have been applied to a wide range of industrial and consumer materials with the aim of reducing their flammability. Given their wide range of applications, their manufacture and use has increased over the years, making them ubiquitous in the environment. Due to the high exposure to these compounds as well as their physicochemical and endocrine-disrupting properties, they can have harmful effects on the environment and humans, particularly in susceptible populations, such as pregnant women and children.

With this special issue, we intend to address the latest research regarding environmental contamination with flame retardants and plasticizers and the consequent challenges for One Health. This encompasses the production and occurrence of these compounds, as well as exposure assessment, metabolism and their consequences for human health. We welcome all types of studies on this matter, experimental and epidemiological studies, up-to-date reviews, meta-analyses, systematic reviews, as well as commentaries.
keywords: ["Plasticizers", "flame retardants", "health impacts", "exposure assessment", "One Health"]

topic: Topic: Innovations in Aortic Surgery and Valve Repair
summary: The rapid advancements in aortic surgery and valve repair have significantly reshaped the treatment landscape for aortic and valvular heart diseases, making it crucial to set up a Special Issue dedicated to "Innovations in Aortic Surgery and Valve Repair." This Special Issue will serve as a comprehensive platform to highlight the latest breakthroughs in surgical techniques, minimally invasive approaches, biomaterials, and imaging technologies that are driving improved patient outcomes and expanding treatment possibilities. By bringing together cutting-edge research, clinical studies, and expert reviews, this issue will not only disseminate valuable knowledge to the medical community but also inspire further innovation and collaboration in this dynamic field. This focused exploration of recent advancements will help to establish new standards of care, address current challenges, and pave the way for future developments in aortic and valvular heart surgery.
keywords: ["Aortic surgery", "valve repair", "aortic and valvular heart diseases", "aortic and valvular heart surgery"]

topic: Topic: Robust Control and Optimization of Complex Dynamic Systems
summary:
Robust control schemes have attracted significant attention over the past few decades. Adaptive control models are known to be powerful tools for representing complex dynamical systems, and many robust control methods have been successfully applied to the engineering field. Recently,  new advancements in modeling, control, and monitoring have emerged within adaptive control systems, including stochastic control, intelligent control, and data-driven control, among others. We invite authors to contribute original research and review articles related to all aspects of modeling, control methods, optimization theories, monitoring, signal processing and fault detection for complex dynamical systems with adaptive control algorithms. All submissions should present original ideas and innovative approaches. Particular interest will be given to papers focusing on the development of engineering applications. Submissions addressing computational issues, search strategies, and modeling and solution techniques for practical problems are also welcome.

Topics of interest include, but are not restricted to:
● Robust control and optimization of intelligent systems;
● Robust and adaptive learning control schemes;
● Robust neural network modeling, control and optimization;
● Robust fuzzy modeling, control and optimization;
● Robust Control and Signal Processing;
● Robust estimation and fault detection algorithms;
● Robust fault tolerant control and optimization.
keywords: ["Robust control", "stochastic control", "fuzzy modeling", "fault detection", "optimization"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Harnessing Antibody-Drug Conjugates for the Treatment of Solid Tumors: Challenges and Opportunities
summary: Antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) are the result of intense research aimed at developing novel cancer therapies with improved targeting capabilities and reduced side effects. ADCs are targeted immunoconjugate compounds consisting of three main components: a monoclonal antibody, a cytotoxic drug (known as the payload), and a linker molecule. The monoclonal antibody is engineered to bind specifically to antigens that are overexpressed on cancer cells. The payload is a potent chemotherapeutic agent that effectively kills cancer cells. The linker molecule attaches the cytotoxic drug to the antibody and releases the cytotoxic payload within the cancer cells through enzymatic cleavage or chemical degradation. Once released, the drug induces cancer cell death by selectively targeting cancer cells while sparing normal cells. This strategy allows the delivery of higher doses of chemotherapeutic agents with increased efficacy, while the monoclonal antibody can also trigger an immune response, further improving the antitumor activity. In the last years, there has been a surge in scientific publications and patents related to ADC research, with the USA, China, Japan, the United Kingdom, Germany, and South Corea leading the field. ADC research is particularly focused on solid tumors such as breast, ovarian, prostate, lung, and pancreatic cancers, as well as immunotherapy or combination chemotherapy. Currently, over 50 organizations worldwide - mostly from the USA, Europe, and Asia - are engaged in research on more than 160 ADC preclinical candidates. This clearly highlights the growing significance of ADCs in the field of cancer research.
keywords: ["ADC", "monoclonal antibody", "targeted cytotoxic drug", "immunotherapy", "combination therapy", "solid tumors"]

topic: Topic: Catalysis for CO<sub>2</sub> Utilization
summary: This Special Issue mainly explores the latest and emerging strategies to develop new and enhanced materials for CO<sub>2</sub> activation and adsorption, and the catalytic reactions involving CO<sub>2</sub> (including electrochemical, photochemical and biological conversion of CO<sub>2</sub>), together with the integrated processes for CO<sub>2</sub> conversion and reduction. The worsening global warming over the years has driven researchers to explore methods for CO<sub>2</sub> utilization to minimize net CO<sub>2</sub> emissions. For years, many catalysts have been developed for CO<sub>2</sub> utilization, accompanied by an increasing number of publications in this field. The issue covers recent advancements in catalyst synthesis for the conversion of CO<sub>2</sub> into synthetic fuels, polymers, carboxylic acids, organic carbonates, and fine chemicals. Both experimental and theoretical analyses are welcomed. In addition to full-length articles and short communications, we encourage review papers from experts in this area. Contributions that present breakthroughs in synthesis methods, characterization techniques, and practical applications will offer insights into the future development of catalytic systems for CO<sub>2</sub> utilization.
keywords: ["CO<sub>2</sub> utilization", "nanomaterials", "electrocatalysis", "photocatalysis", "biocatalysis"]

topic: Topic: Convenient, Reliable, Green Innovation of Assembly Technology in the Age of Artificial Intelligence
summary:
Since the 21st century, the relationship between social development and environmental change has become an important topic of common reflection for humankind. The environmental impact of the manufacturing industry has aroused strong concern. In response, the environment-friendly manufacturing principle urges enterprises to mitigate environmental pollution and resource waste caused by the manufacturing process, promoting a shift towards green and sustainable manufacturing. Assembly is the final part of the equipment manufacturing process. For large-scale equipment such as aircraft and ships, the assembly process can account for over half of the entire production cycle. With the requirements of green manufacturing, assembly technologies are undergoing transformative innovation. The emergence and rapid development of artificial intelligence (AI) provides a strong basic support and means of realization for the change of assembly technology.

The Special Issue, "Convenient, Reliable, Green Innovation of Assembly Technology in the Age of Artificial Intelligence", explores the innovation of assembly technology brought about by AI in the manufacturing industry. The featured papers cover a range of topics, including but not limited to assembly process design methods using machine learning, virtual assembly experiments using digital twins, and AI-driven monitoring and optimization of assembly results. Together, these contributions highlight innovations that make assembly more efficient, reliable, and environmentally friendly. Overall, this Special Issue provides valuable insights and practical guidance to researchers, practitioners, and decision-makers seeking to realize cost reduction, reliability enhancement, and green transformation of assembly technologies.
keywords: ["Assembly process design", "assembly quality control", "system reliability", "non-destructive testing", "digital twin", "virtual reality interaction"]

topic: Topic: Advanced Building Materials and Seismic Design for Building Resilience and Disaster Prevention
summary:
Advanced building materials, such as high-performance concrete, fiber-reinforced composites, and smart materials, provide enhanced performance, durability, and sustainability, meeting the demands of modern construction for enhanced seismic resilience and reduced environmental impact. This Special Issue explores cutting-edge materials and innovative design strategies that enhance the resilience of buildings against seismic events. It also examines seismic design methodologies, retrofitting techniques, and disaster prevention strategies aimed at minimizing damage and ensuring the safety of structures. This collection of articles provides valuable insights for engineers, architects, and researchers focused on sustainable and resilient building practices.

The scope of this issue covers, but is not restricted to: 

• Advanced building materials; 

• Response, analysis and design of structures under seismic conditions; 

• Experimental and computational simulation of seismic effects on structures; 

• Seismic vulnerability assessment; 

• Development of seismic code guidelines and evaluation; 

• Methods for earthquake-resistant design; 

• Repair and strengthening of structures; 

• Passive and active systems for earthquake protection; 

• Damage assessments;

• Seismic risk assessment of structures including multi-hazards (earthquakes and other hazards such as wind, tsunami, storm surge, fire, blast, impact, etc.); 

• Performance-based seismic design considering damage minimization, repairability, functional recovery, etc.; 

• Earthquake disaster mitigation and emergency planning.
keywords: ["Advanced materials", "seismic design", "seismic vulnerability assessment", "seismic risk assessment", "performance-based seismic design", "seismic design codes"]

topic: Topic: Innovative Treatments for Skin Pigmentation Disorders
summary:
Skin pigmentation disorders, encompassing conditions such as vitiligo, melasma, and post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, affect millions worldwide and significantly impact individuals’ quality of life and psychological well-being. Current treatments, including topical agents, phototherapy, and surgical interventions, offer varying degrees of efficacy but often present limitations such as incomplete repigmentation, recurrence, and adverse side effects. Despite advancements in dermatological research, achieving consistent and sustained therapeutic outcomes remains challenging.

 

The objective of this Special Issue is to showcase cutting-edge research and innovative approaches in the treatment of skin pigmentation disorders that hold promise for clinical application. We aim to highlight breakthrough therapies, emerging technologies, and novel insights that can enhance treatment efficacy, safety, and accessibility. Additionally, this issue seeks to address the barriers hindering the translation of promising treatments from the laboratory to clinical practice.

 

This Special Issue will feature comprehensive studies and reviews on a wide range of topics, including but not limited to:

● Advanced pharmacological treatments targeting melanocyte function and pigmentation pathways;

● Novel phototherapy techniques and light-based devices for more effective pigment modulation;

● Biotechnological innovations, such as stem cell therapy and gene editing, for restoring pigmentation;

● Combination therapies that synergize existing treatments to improve outcomes;

● Biomarker discovery and personalized medicine approaches for tailored treatment strategies;

● Long-term clinical studies assessing the efficacy and safety of new treatments;

●Patient-centered research focusing on quality of life and psychological impacts of pigmentation disorders.
keywords: ["Skin pigmentation disorders", "vitiligo", "melasma", "post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation", "advanced pharmacological treatments", "phototherapy", "stem cell therapy", "gene editing", "combination therapies", "personalized medicine", "melanocyte function", "pigment modulation", "clinical efficacy", "treatment safety"]

source: Rare Disease and Orphan Drugs Journal
topic: Topic: Advances in Neurofibromatosis - The Future is Bright
summary:
Neurofibromatosis (NF) is a rare genetic disorder that predisposes patients to develop central and peripheral nervous system tumors. The types of NF include neurofibromatosis type 1 (1:2500), and all types of schwannomatosis (1:70,000), which includes neurofibromatosis type 2-related schwannomatosis (1:25,000). Formerly understudied and underfunded, this genetic cancer predisposition syndrome has caught the attention of basic science researchers and clinicians since the US FDA approval of the first MEK inhibitor for neurofibromatosis type I-related plexiform neurofibromas.

There remains a substantial unmet need for therapeutics that improve the quality of life for those affected by NF. As we continue to unravel the mysteries of tumor biology and the signaling pathways involved, the development of personalized therapies, especially in this era of precision medicine, holds great promise. The integration of genomic information into NF diagnostic criteria has helped clinicians diagnose patients earlier, improve prognostication, and inform treatment decisions.

In this edition of the rare disease journal, we invite authors to discuss the latest advances in neurofibromatosis research and highlight the importance of equitable care for patients from diverse backgrounds.

We welcome Original Research Articles, Review Articles, and Brief Communications. Topics planned for coverage include:

● Plexiform neurofibromas – What is next? 

● NF-2-related schwannomatosis - What is in the pipeline?

● Cutaneous neurofibromas - An unmet need for therapeutics;

● Neurofibromatosis type I-related Optic Pathway Gliomas – The past, present, and the future;

● Cell-free DNA for NF-related CNS tumors;

● NF1 and learning difficulties; 

● Preimplantation diagnosis for neurofibromatosis; 

● Gene therapy; 

● Reproductive issues in NF1; 

● Inclusive care for all, including LGBTQ patients.
keywords: ["Neurofibromatosis (NF)", "Schwannomatosis (SWN)", "NF-2 related schwannomatosis", "plexiform neurofibromas", "optic pathway gliomas", "rare disease", "cancer predisposition syndrome", "MEK inhibitor"]

topic: Topic: Atomic- and Nano-Scale Electrocatalysts for Energy Sustainability
summary:
In the pursuit of sustainable energy, atomic- and nano-scale (ANS) electrocatalysts are pivotal in driving innovations in energy conversion, storage, and fuel technologies. In particular, single-atom catalysts (SACs), featuring isolated metal atoms, maximize catalytic activity while reducing material usage. These catalysts enhance key reactions across various clean energy technologies, making them essential for advancing sustainable energy solutions.

This Special Issue explores the role of ANS electrocatalysts, including SACs, in advancing fuel cells, water splitting, CO₂ reduction, hydrogen storage, metal-air batteries, lithium-sulfur batteries, and other sustainable energy applications.

In fuel cells, ANS electrocatalysts improve the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR), reducing the need for precious metals such as platinum while maintaining high activity. This makes fuel cells more scalable and cost-effective for large-scale energy production. In water splitting, ANS electrocatalysts drive the hydrogen (HER) and oxygen evolution reactions (OER), enhancing the efficiency of proton exchange membranes and alkaline electrolyzers for hydrogen production. ANS electrocatalysts also play a critical role in CO₂ reduction, converting CO₂ into valuable fuels and chemicals while contributing to carbon capture and utilization. In hydrogen storage, ANS electrocatalysts optimize adsorption and desorption processes, improving storage capacity and making hydrogen storage safer and more efficient for fuel applications. In energy storage systems, ANS catalysts enhance the performance of metal-air and lithium-sulfur batteries by improving ORR/OER efficiency, sulfur utilization, and cycle stability, resulting in longer battery life and higher energy densities.

We invite contributions that cover:

1. Synthesis and Characterization: Developing methods to synthesize ANS catalysts and characterize their properties;

2. Mechanistic Studies: Investigating catalytic mechanisms and using computational methods to predict behavior;

3. Application and Performance: Integrating catalysts into various applications and evaluating real-world performance;

4. Sustainability and Scalability: Exploring the environmental impact and cost-effective production of these catalysts.
keywords: ["Atomic-electrocatalysts; nano-scale electrocatalysts; single-atom catalysts (SACs)"]

topic: Topic: Application of Neutron and Synchrotron X-ray Diffraction in Microstructural Analysis
summary:
Advanced materials provide solutions to present and future challenges in energy, communications, nanotechnology, security, and transportation, and the development of new materials will promote the growth of future world economy. Precise and rapid characterisation in microstructures is critical in designing next generation materials. 

Neutron scattering techniques are widely used to address the fundamental questions about the crystal structure and chemical composition of materials and their dynamic properties. Neutrons can also be used as an effective tool for exploring the behaviour of materials under applied stress or thermal, magnetic, or electrical fields; characterizing materials under real-life operating conditions with moving engineering components; and extending the service life of critical engineering structures. Neutrons penetrate most materials to depths of several centimetres depending on the specific attenuation properties of that material which make neutron scattering/radiography/imaging as a powerful non-destructive inspection method, which can favourably be applied for solving fundamental scientific issues and practical, industrial-related problems.

Synchrotron radiation sources provide indispensable instruments with tuneable, highly-polarized and intense X-rays for a wide range of research in materials science and engineering. The rapid progress of synchrotron-based X-ray diffraction/imaging methods with a high real and reciprocal spatial resolution provides powerful tools for characterizing multiscale microstructures in crystalline materials. It has led breakthroughs in fundamental and application aspects of diverse areas, including structural materials, catalysis, battery, photovoltaics, and fuel cells.  

This Special Issue will focus on the application of neutron/synchrotron scattering techniques in material science and engineering research, which includes materials characterisation, new technologies and advanced computational methodologies. Both Original Research and Review articles are welcome in this Special Issue.
Areas of interest include but are not limited to:

● Neutron/Synchrotron diffraction on crystal structural analysis;

● Neutron/Synchrotron imaging;

● Small angle scattering;

● Residual stress measurement;

● Texture analysis;

● Mechanical behaviour;

● Time resolved in-situ phase transformation;

● Determination of magnetic structures;

● Hydrogen storage materials.
keywords: ["Diffraction and scattering", "crystal structure", "imaging", "residual stress", "texture", "in-situ measurement", "computation"]

topic: Topic: Decarbonization via Resources Recovery
summary:
In response to the climate crisis, resource recovery has emerged as a vital strategy for sustainable decarbonization. This Special Issue, Decarbonization via Resources Recovery, focuses on cutting-edge research that transforms underutilized materials from various sectors—such as seawater, minerals, industrial by-products, and solid waste—into valuable carbon-sequestering resources. By leveraging innovative technologies, including electrochemical methods, this issue explores how the recovery and repurposing of resources can contribute to global carbon reduction efforts.

A key focus of this issue is the extraction of carbon-absorbing materials such as magnesium (Mg) and calcium (Ca) from non-traditional feedstocks. Electrochemical processes offer an exciting avenue for efficiently capturing these elements from sources like desalination brine, mining tailings, and waste streams, enabling the creation of sustainable materials that can be used in construction, manufacturing, and other industries. While electrochemical methods are highlighted for their potential, the issue encompasses a broader spectrum of resource recovery technologies, from carbon capture and utilization (CCU) to waste-to-energy conversion, all of which play a critical role in reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

Beyond technical innovations, this Special Issue also addresses the broader ecological, economic, and policy dimensions of resource recovery. Topics such as circular economy frameworks, resource efficiency, and policy mechanisms that incentivize low-carbon technologies will be explored, providing a holistic view of the decarbonization landscape.

Decarbonization via Resources Recovery offers a platform for interdisciplinary research that bridges the gap between scientific discovery and real-world application. By focusing on the sustainable recovery of resources, this Special Issue not only advances the field of carbon mitigation but also presents scalable solutions that have the potential to reshape industries and economies as we move toward a low-carbon future.
keywords: ["CO<sub>2</sub> removal", "decarbonation", "resources recovery", "sustainability", "waste", "energy", "materials"]

topic: Topic: Microstructure Modulation and Optimization of Aqueous Batteries
summary:
Electrochemical energy storage plays an indispensable role in sustaining consumer electronics, electric vehicles, and smart industry grids, serving as a cornerstone for a greener and more efficient future. The global shift toward low-carbon energy is driving up demand for advanced secondary batteries. Among these, aqueous electrolyte-based batteries are believed to play significant roles in large-scale energy storage due to their superior safety performances and potentially lower cost. Recent years have witnessed remarkable progress in the dynamic field, alongside the emergence of a multitude of innovative concepts for next-generation aqueous batteries, driven by microstructure modulation and optimization. Importantly, enhancing the performance of aqueous batteries by modulating and optimizing the microstructures of electrode materials, separators and electrolytes is highly desirable. 

This Special Issue will spotlight the development of advanced and sustainable aqueous battery technology while prompting the community to incorporate more new ideas and perspectives. It offers a thorough examination of material microstructure design, innovative surface modifications, novel electrolytes, strategic regulation of the electrode-electrolyte interface, and an in-depth mechanism exploration through advanced characterization techniques and theoretical studies.  We welcome original research articles and reviews. Topics of interest include but are not limited to:

● Aqueous sulfur-based batteries; 

● Aqueous sodium/potassium/zinc/aluminium-ion batteries;

● Aqueous dual-ion batteries;

● Aqueous ammonium-ion batteries;

● Metal-air batteries.
keywords: ["Aqueous batteries", "electrode materials", "electrolytes", "electrode-electrolyte interface", "separators", "metal-air batteries", "advanced characterizations", "electrochemical simulation and calculation", "battery management"]

topic: Topic: Recovery of Energy Materials
summary:
This Special Issue addresses critical challenges and innovative solutions related to the recovery, recycling, and reuse of materials within the energy sector. As global demand for sustainable energy rises, efficient recovery of materials used in batteries, fuel cells, solar cells, and other energy conversion and storage technologies has become increasingly critical. This issue will highlight cutting-edge research that spans the development of advanced recovery techniques, the design of recyclable materials, and the theoretical understanding of material degradation and regeneration processes.

We invite submissions that explore novel synthetic methods, fundamental theories, and advanced characterization techniques aimed at improving material recovery processes. Topics of interest include, but are not limited to, material recovery from batteries and supercapacitors, solar cells, fuel cells, and thermoelectric devices, as well as the recycling of materials used in other energies such as hydrogen generation, bioenergy, and hydrocarbon conversion.

Contributions should offer significant insights with the potential to shape the future of energy materials recovery and align with the journal's focus on innovative research at the nexus of energy conversion, storage, and sustainability. We welcome both experimental and theoretical studies with the potential to drive technological advancements and inform global energy strategies.
keywords: ["Energy materials recovery", "recycling of energy materials", "sustainable energy technologies", "battery material reuse", "fuel cell recycling", "solar cell materials recovery", "thermoelectric device recycling", "advanced recovery techniques", "material degradation and regeneration", "renewable energy sustainability"]

topic: Topic: The Clinical Complexities and Ingenious Solutions within Gender-Affirming Surgery
summary:
Gender-affirming surgery (GAS) has emerged as one of the most prevalent surgical disciplines in recent decades. An increasing number of transgender surgeons have reported their personal findings in scientific journals. Despite the fact that psychosexual and psychosocial evaluations, as well as hormonal replacement, constitute the first in gender transitioning, surgery remains the key step, aiming to alleviate gender dysphoria related to phenotypical gender incongruence. Recently, gender-affirming surgery encompasses not only genital reconstruction. A wide range of procedures is divided into three groups: genital surgery, top surgery and various facial procedures. The new Standards of Care presented under the World Professional Association for Transgender Health (WPATH) advocate new recommendations for supporting candidates with gender dysphoria. The latest data confirmed a higher incidence of gender dysphoria, ranging from 0.08 to 0.41% depending on the region of the World. This is the main reason for more frequent discussions and the development of new and modified surgical techniques to minimize complications after gender-affirming surgery. 

Our Special Issue is designed to collect recent data from leading centers worldwide, with the main objective of enhancing our knowledge and providing better solutions for our candidates. We believe that our contributors will present the latest research either in operative techniques or possible complications and treatment modalities.
keywords: ["Transgenderism", "gender dysphoria", "gender-affirming surgery", "vaginoplasty", "phalloplasty", "metoidioplasty"]

topic: Topic: Motion Control for Intelligent Swarm Systems in Complex Environments
summary:
The rapid development of intelligent technology has unveiled immense potential for intelligent systems across various fields, including military operations, rescue missions, and environmental monitoring. The motion control of intelligent swarm systems in complex environments is particularly crucial, as it directly influences the flexibility, effectiveness, and safety of these systems. However, most existing systems still face challenges such as inefficiency and rigidity when dealing with complex environments. This limits the efficiency and reliability of intelligent swarm systems in practical applications.

To address these issues, this Special Issue seeks to explore the challenges intelligent systems face in various complex environments. It encourages research and application of transparent and efficient motion control algorithms to bridge the gap between theory and practice, enhancing the adaptability and stability of intelligent swarms in complex environments. Research in this area not only involves coordination and control of multiple agents but also plays a vital role in improving automation efficiency and decision-making quality.

This Special Issue will present a diverse array of research outcomes based on the latest theoretical frameworks and practical case studies, thus promoting deep collaboration between academia and industry. This will drive the integrated development of intelligent swarm systems, better meeting future technological demands.

We invite contributions covering a range of theoretical and experimental research that promotes the application of intelligent swarm systems in complex scenarios. Topics include, but are not limited to, the following:

● Formation control;

● Collective intelligence;

● Autonomous obstacle avoidance;

● Swarm system modeling;

● Path planning;

● Swarm coordination;

● Motion control;

● Dynamic environment adaptation;

● Environment modeling and perception;

● Robot intelligence systems.
keywords: ["Motion control", "intelligent swarm systems", "complex environment", "multi-agent coordination", "obstacle avoidance", "dynamic adaptation"]

topic: Topic: Advances in Flexible and Stretchable Biomedical Devices
summary:
Flexible and stretchable biomedical devices represent a rapidly growing field with transformative potential for healthcare applications. Designed to seamlessly conform to the human body, these devices offer unparalleled advantages in wearable health monitoring, implantable diagnostics, and therapeutic interventions. Recent advances in materials science, device fabrication techniques, and system integration have enabled the development of highly flexible and stretchable devices that maintain mechanical integrity while providing accurate and reliable biomedical functions.

This Special Issue aims to highlight cutting-edge research on the design, fabrication, and application of flexible and stretchable biomedical devices. The topics can include the development and application of new materials, equipment design and manufacturing technology, application field expansion and case analysis. We welcome original Research Articles, Reviews, and Perspectives that explore the challenges and opportunities in developing next-generation biomedical devices capable of improving patient care and advancing precision medicine.
keywords: ["Flexible biomedical devices", "stretchable electronics", "wearable health monitoring", "implantable sensors", "digital healthcare"]

topic: Topic: Nanomaterials for Water Pollutant Remediation: Advanced Applications and Environmental Impacts
summary:
This Special Issue, Nanomaterials for Water Pollutant Remediation: Advanced Applications and Environmental Impacts, focuses on the emerging role of nanotechnology in addressing water pollution, one of the most critical environmental challenges of our time. 

With increasing industrialization and urbanization, water contamination by heavy metals, organic pollutants, and other hazardous substances has become a pressing global issue, posing a severe threat to both human health and ecosystems. Conventional waste treatment methods often fail to adequately remove these pollutants from water, due to their complex structure and persistent nature. Nanomaterials, with their unique physicochemical properties, offer promising and sustainable solutions for efficient water remediation technologies. Thus, advanced nanotechnology-based approaches are being developed to tackle water treatment, contributing to water sustainability and circularity. 

This Special Issue aims to cover a wide range of cutting-edge research on the design, synthesis, and application of diverse nanomaterials for the remediation of water contaminants. Additionally, it intends to cover the environmental impacts of using nanomaterials for water remediation, ensuring that advancements in technology are balanced with ecological safety. This issue seeks to foster a deeper understanding of how nanotechnology can contribute to cleaner water systems while minimizing potential environmental risks and ensuring water circularity.

Researchers are encouraged to contribute to this Special Issue with research papers, short communications, and review articles.
keywords: ["Advanced wastewater treatments", "adsorption", "photodegradation", "water reuse", "sustainability", "emerging contaminants"]

topic: Topic: Advances in Supercapacitors and Hybrid Ion Capacitors
summary: Supercapacitors, also known as electrochemical capacitors, include electrochemical double-layer capacitors, which rely on ion adsorption, and pseudo-capacitors, which are based on fast redox reactions. Although the energy densities of supercapacitors are much lower than those of secondary batteries, they are several orders of magnitude higher than those of conventional dielectric capacitors. Supercapacitors typically exhibit high power density, long cyclic stability, enhanced safety, and a broad operational temperature range. Consequently, they are a viable alternative or complement to rechargeable batteries in applications that require high power delivery or fast energy harvesting. In recent years, hybrid ion (super)capacitors have been developed using ions such as Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, NH4+, etc., to extend the energy density of traditional supercapacitors. Various nanomaterials, including porous carbon materials, conducting polymers, and traditional metal compounds, have been developed for capacitive energy storage devices through advanced nanotechnologies such as solvothermal reactions, electrospinning, chemical vapor deposition, electrodeposition, etc. This Special Issue aims to provide an overview of recent advances and future prospects in the field of supercapacitors and hybrid ion capacitors. We invite submissions of original research articles, perspectives, and reviews, encouraging authors to contribute their latest experimental and theoretical work.
keywords: ["(Micro)Supercapacitors", "hybrid ion capacitors", "carbon nanomaterials", "transition metal compounds", "conducting polymers", "MOF/COF", "electrolyte", "devices", "integrated systems", "nanotechnologies"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Innovative Approaches and Prognostic Insights in Oral Cancer Research
summary:
Oral cancer remains a significant global health challenge, with high morbidity and mortality rates. The complex nature of oral cancer, shaped by diverse genetic, environmental, and lifestyle factors, underscores the need for innovative research approaches and prognostic insights to improve patient outcomes. This Special Issue, "Innovative Approaches and Prognostic Insights in Oral Cancer Research," aims to explore cutting-edge developments and emerging trends in the field of oral cancer.

Recent advances in molecular biology, immunotherapy, and precision medicine have opened new avenues for early detection, personalized treatment, and improved prognosis. The integration of technologies such as next-generation sequencing, liquid biopsy, and advanced imaging techniques has revolutionized the identification of biomarkers and the understanding of tumor biology. Additionally, novel therapeutic strategies, including targeted therapies and immunotherapies, are showing promising results in combating oral cancer.

This Special Issue seeks contributions that address:

● Advances in molecular profiling and biomarker discovery in oral cancer;

● Novel diagnostic tools and imaging technologies for early detection;

● Prognostic models and risk stratification approaches;

● Emerging therapeutic strategies, including immunotherapy and targeted treatments;

● Integrative approaches to personalized care and patient management.

Through this collection, we aim to foster a deeper understanding of oral cancer and inspire new strategies to improve patient care.
keywords: ["Oral cancer", "biomarkers", "early detection", "precision medicine", "immunotherapy", "targeted therapy", "prognosis"]

topic: Topic: Structural Regulation and Performance Optimization of Two-Dimensional (2D) Materials
summary:
Two-dimensional (2D) materials, including graphene, transition metal dichalcogenides, layered double hydroxides, and phosphorene, have emerged as a promising class of materials with remarkable properties and vast potential across multiple fields. With a thickness of only a few atomic layers, 2D materials exhibit unique electronic, optical, and mechanical characteristics that differ significantly from their bulk counterparts. These distinctive properties make them ideal for applications in nanoelectronics, energy storage, sensors, and catalysis.

The performance of 2D materials is intricately linked to their structure, which can be tailored at the atomic level to enhance their inherent properties. Structural regulation—such as defect engineering, strain modulation, and the controlled stacking of 2D layers—plays a crucial role in optimizing electronic conductivity, mechanical flexibility, and chemical reactivity. As research progresses, understanding the relationship between structural modifications and material performance has become a central focus for developing next-generation technologies. 

Achieving optimal performance in practical applications also requires addressing challenges such as stability, reproducibility, and integration into existing systems. This necessitates the development of advanced synthesis techniques, scalable production methods, and innovative strategies for interface engineering.

Research in this field seeks to bridge the gap between theoretical predictions and experimental realizations of high-performance 2D materials. By focusing on structural regulation and performance optimization, we can unlock the full potential of 2D materials, enabling breakthroughs in next-generation technologies such as flexible electronics, quantum devices, and energy-efficient systems.
keywords: ["2D materials synthesis", "2D materials for electronics applications", "2D materials for energy storage", "2D materials for sensors", "2D materials for catalysis", "structural engineering of 2D materials", "performance optimization of 2D materials", "theoretical modeling and simulation of 2D materials"]

source: Extracellular Vesicles and Circulating Nucleic Acids
topic: Topic: The Functions of Extracellular Vesicles in Reproduction
summary: Recent reviews have highlighted the crucial role of extracellular vesicles (EVs) in paracrine communication during various reproductive processes, including oogenesis, oocyte maturation, fertilization, embryo-maternal crosstalk in the oviduct, and embryo implantation. For example, follicular EVs secreted by granulosa cells promote oocyte maturation and increase the expression of key genes. Additionally, EVs from male reproductive tracts contribute to a complex pool of EVs that assist in sperm maturation and influence the physiology of female reproductive tract cells, thereby supporting reproductive success. Successful pregnancy requires a molecular dialogue between the embryo and the female reproductive tract, which begins in the oviduct and continues until placenta formation. During embryo migration from the oviduct to the uterus, the embryo undergoes distinct metabolic stages (cleavage, morula, blastula, gastrula), with the oviduct positively impacting embryo development and quality. Furthermore, EVs from embryo can regulate embryo development and implantation. This Special Issue aims to collect original research articles, reviews, short papers, mini reviews, communications, and perspectives on all aspects of reproductive biology in light of new insights into paracrine communication mechanisms through EVs. It will also consider the morphological and molecular responses of gametes and preimplantation embryos to environmental stressors that might contribute to adverse reproductive outcomes.
keywords: ["Reproduction", "gametes", "embryos", "extracellular vesicles", "microRNA", "biomarker", "fertility"]

topic: Topic: Design, Preparation, and Application of Metal Nanocatalysts
summary:
Metal nanocatalysts have attracted considerable attention due to their distinctive properties. These materials are pivotal in numerous catalytic processes, ranging from energy conversion and environmental remediation to chemical synthesis. The development and fabrication of these nanocatalysts involve precise control over their size, shape, and composition to achieve optimized catalytic performance tailored for specific applications. Progress in nanotechnology has facilitated the emergence of highly efficient and selective nanocatalysts, revolutionizing fields such as fuel cell technology, hydrogen generation, and contaminant decomposition.

This Special Issue aims to explore the cutting-edge advancements in the design, synthesis, and deployment of metal nanocatalysts, emphasizing their capacity to confront global energy and environmental challenges. We welcome submissions of original research papers, review articles, short communications, and other scholarly contributions that offer novel perspectives or substantial progress within these domains.
keywords: ["Metal nanocatalysts", "atomically dispersed catalysts", "catalyst design", "energy conversion", "environmental remediation", "chemical synthesis", "nanotechnology", "hydrogen production", "pollutant degradation", "catalytic activity"]

topic: Topic: Robotic-Assisted Colorectal Cancer Resection
summary: The chapter on "Robotic-Assisted Colorectal Cancer Resection" will explore advanced surgical techniques employed in robotic-assisted procedures for colorectal cancer. It will provide a detailed overview of key operations, including right colectomy with complete mesocolic excision, transverse colectomy, left colectomy, and low anterior resection with total mesorectal excision. The chapter will highlight the advantages of robotic platforms, such as enhanced precision, improved visualization, and greater dexterity, which contribute to better outcomes when compared to traditional laparoscopic methods. Additionally, it will discuss the specific technical steps of these procedures, perioperative considerations, potential complications, and strategies to improve safety and performance. The review will also evaluate the impact of robotic assistance on patient recovery, oncological outcomes, and future innovations in colorectal cancer surgery.
keywords: ["Robotic-assisted surgery", "colorectal cancer resection", "minimally invasive surgery", "surgical technique", "robotic platforms", "perioperative care", "robotic colorectal surgery", "enhanced visualization", "precision surgery"]

topic: Topic: Applications of Machine Learning for Intelligent Control and Systems
summary:
In an era of rapid technological advancements, the integration of machine learning (ML) into control systems has emerged as a transformative force, reshaping the landscape of automation and intelligent systems. Traditional control systems rely heavily on predefined models and rules to operate effectively. However, as systems grow more complex and dynamic, the limitations of these approaches become apparent. ML, with its ability to learn from data and adapt to new situations, offers a powerful solution to these challenges. By leveraging ML algorithms, control systems can enhance their performance, robustness, and adaptability, enabling them to handle uncertainties and non-linearities more effectively. The integration of ML and control systems represents a transformative trend in the field of automation and intelligent systems. By harnessing the power of data and adaptability, ML algorithms are enabling control systems to achieve unprecedented levels of capabilities. Despite the promising applications of ML in control systems, several challenges remain. As automatic control applications expand, technical shortcomings, decision biases, and safety concerns have led to a crisis of trust. Thus, the research community is actively exploring the most advanced ML techniques to improve reliability in these applications.

This Special Issue aims to bring together researchers and practitioners from academia and industry to present their latest findings, discuss recent advances, and exchange ideas on “Applications of Machine Learning for Intelligent Control and Systems”. The focus will be on the advanced theories and practical applications of artificial intelligence, control science, network systems, mathematics, communication engineering, computer science, electronic information, industrial engineering, and other fields. We kindly invite researchers and practitioners to contribute their high-quality original research or review articles discussing current cutting-edge research topics in artificial intelligence and control theory. Analytical, numerical, and experimental works that contribute to the development of ML applications in control systems are welcome.
keywords: ["Machine learning", "control systems", "reinforcement learning control", "signal processing", "mathematical optimization", "complex networks", "human interaction", "big data and data mining", "information security", "modeling and simulation", "neural networks"]

topic: Topic: Piezoelectric and Electrostatic Soft Robotics
summary:
Piezoelectric and electrostatic soft robotics represent a cutting-edge domain that spans a multitude of applications within various sectors and scientific inquiries, including search and rescue missions, environmental surveillance, healthcare and rehabilitation, among others. The exploration of these robotics draws on multiple disciplines, ranging from materials science to physics, mechanics, mechanical engineering, and control theory.

This Special Issue is dedicated to highlighting the latest advancements in manufacturing methodologies, control strategies, and practical applications of piezoelectric and electrostatic soft robotics. It seeks to attract contributions from both academic and industrial experts, aiming to refine current understanding and introduce groundbreaking concepts that will shape future developments and technological innovations. We invite authors to submit original research papers and comprehensive reviews on topics pertinent to this Special Issue.
keywords: ["Soft robotics", "piezoelectric", "electrostatic", "maneuverability", "environmental adaptability", "functionality"]

topic: Topic: Soft Materials for Aqueous Energy Storage Applications
summary: As the search for sustainable and efficient energy storage solutions intensifies, soft materials are attracting increasing attention for their potential applications in aqueous energy storage systems. These materials, characterized by their flexibility, lightweight nature, and biocompatibility, can enhance the performance of aqueous batteries and supercapacitors. This Special Issue aims to explore the latest advancements in the design, synthesis, and application of soft materials tailored for aqueous energy storage. We welcome contributions that investigate novel soft material formulations, electrochemical performance, stability, and innovative device architectures. Additionally, we invite research submissions on smart aqueous energy storage, including sensing, battery sensor systems, self-healing and artificial intelligence. By fostering interdisciplinary collaboration, this issue seeks to advance the understanding and development of next-generation aqueous energy storage technologies.
keywords: ["Soft materials", "aqueous energy storage", "electrochemical performance", "supercapacitors", "batteries"]

topic: Topic: Innovative Designs and Applications of Polymer Adhesives
summary: Polymer adhesives play a crucial role in consumer goods, industrial manufacturing, and medical applications due to their diverse compositions and properties that meet a wide range of demands. However, as market demands evolve and environmental requirements intensify, traditional polymer adhesives face challenges related to versatility, sustainability, and mechanical robustness. Existing adhesives are often designed for specific uses, limiting their adaptability across varying scenarios, and many are derived from non-renewable resources, placing pressure on the environment. In practical applications, numerous adhesives are also plagued by hassles of mechanical failures (e.g., weak adhesion, interfacial debonding and fatigue fracture) in mechanical demanding environments. This Special Issue focuses on the innovative applications of polymer adhesives, aiming to explore novel, sustainable adhesive solutions, showcase their broad potential across various fields, and drive the future development of materials science and industrial applications.
keywords: ["Polymer adhesives", "sustainable materials", "innovative applications", "structural adhesives", "crosslinked polymers", "medical devices", "environmental sustainability"]

topic: Topic: “Unlocking the AI Future of Materials Science”: Selected Papers from the International Workshop on Data-driven Computational and Theoretical Materials Design (DCTMD)
summary: The rapidly evolving domain of data-driven science marks a transformative shift across various scientific disciplines, establishing itself as a cornerstone alongside traditional pillars such as experimentation, theoretical analysis, and computation. At the heart of this paradigm shift is Artificial Intelligence (AI), which offers unparalleled opportunities for unveiling the intricate relationships between composition, structure, and the properties or performance of materials. This revolution paves the way for accelerated materials discovery and innovation, propelling the field into a new era of research and development. In response to the burgeoning impact of AI on materials science, the International Workshop on Data-Driven Computational and Theoretical Materials Design (DCTMD2024) emerged as a pivotal event aimed at “Unlocking the AI Future of Materials Science”, which was held successfully in Shanghai, China from October 9-13, 2024. Nearly 200 researchers from more than 20 countries attended and there were 46 talks and 36 posters covering various aspects of AI-assisted computational and experimental materials design. This Special Issue aims to capture and build on the lively discussions and insights gained from the workshop in the diverse areas of AI for Materials Design from data management to advanced computing and autonomous experiment laboratories.
keywords: ["Artificial Intelligence (AI)", "machine learning", "materials design", "materials database", "AI materials computation and experiment", "self-driving laboratory", "from data to knowledge"]

source: Journal of Translational Genetics and Genomics
topic: Topic: Genetic and Molecular Advances in Colorectal Cancer Therapy
summary: Currently, the treatment of colorectal cancer is based on comprehensive therapy with surgery as the cornerstone. Molecular targeted therapy has introduced a precision treatment model for colorectal cancer. While drug resistance induced by KRAS-mutations limits effectiveness of anti-EGFR therapy. In recent years, colorectal cancer treatment has entered the era of immunotherapy, immune checkpoint inhibitors that block PD-1 has been shown highly effective in patients with dMMR/MSI-H colorectal cancers. Furthermore, the combination of neoadjuvant radiotherapy and immunochemotherapy achieved significant breakthroughs in pMMR/MSS locally advanced rectal cancer. However, a portion of patients remain unresponsive to multimodal therapy The varying molecular characteristics of colorectal cancer are closely related to drug sensitivity, and a deeper understanding of the molecular mechanisms behind the development, progression, and treatment resistance of colorectal cancer will help improve treatment outcomes. For this Special Issue in JTGG, we welcome submissions that investigate the molecular mechanisms that have led to poor responses to therapy in colorectal cancer, particularly to immunotherapy. Based on this, targeted intervention strategies are proposed to enhance the therapeutic efficacy for specific molecular subtypes of colorectal cancer.
keywords: ["Colorectal cancer", "precision treatment", "immunotherapy", "molecular"]

topic: Topic: New Energy Surfaces for Solar Cells, Thermosolar Power, and Hydrogen Generation
summary:
The degradation of energy surfaces significantly affects the performance of systems that harvest, convert, or store energy, such as thin film solar cells, batteries, solar selective coatings, and bipolar plates. Energy surfaces, particularly at material interfaces, are critical to key processes such as charge transfer, catalysis, and energy conversion. Advances in materials, especially in the deposition and growth of new surfaces for energy applications, include innovations such as thin film deposition nanostructured oxides (e.g., ZnO, WO, and CdS), noble metal catalysts, and solar energy absorbers (e.g., multilayer oxynitrides and metal and non-metal dielectric absorbers). However, these surfaces are prone to degradation over time due to factors such as thermal stress and other degradation mechanisms that reduce system efficiency and lifespan. Therefore, understanding both the growth of these materials and methods to prevent degradation is essential for improving the stability and sustainability of energy technologies.

Scope of the Special Issue:
This Special Issue aims to showcase new developments around the degradation of energy surfaces, covering a broad spectrum of topics, including but not limited to:
● Development of new material surfaces used in hydrogen production;
● Advances in nano-multilayered materials, nanostructured oxide thin films and noble metal catalysts;
● Surface oxidation and metal degradation in gas turbines at high operating temperatures;
● Deposition and growth of new critical materials for fuel cells and their durability;
● Simulation of advanced surfaces for organic photovoltaics (OPV) and thin film photovoltaics;
● Surface degradation caused by high-temperature thermal cycling and long-term exposure to heat in thermosolar components;
● Prospects and future directions in solar cell surfaces and their degradation.
keywords: ["Photovoltaics", "thermal storage", "fuel cell", "SOEC", "surface degrade"]

source: Journal of Cancer Metastasis and Treatment
topic: Topic: Novel Therapeutic Targeting Approaches to Cancer Therapy Resistance Mechanism
summary:
Therapy resistance presents a significant pharmacological challenge in cancer treatment. Recent insights into the molecular determinants of acquired therapy resistance play a crucial role in understanding the primary causes of genetic evolution and non-genetic adaptive mechanisms. Therapy resistance mechanisms involve a complex interplay of clonal dynamics, tumor microenvironment, and immune surveillance, all of which influence tumor phenotypes. Cancer cells adapt to therapeutic pressure by employing non-genetic mechanisms to evade drug action. The diverse array of resistance mechanisms poses new challenges and necessitates innovative strategies to target these pathways. In this Special Issue, we invite research articles focusing on therapy resistance mechanisms, small molecule inhibitors, and the effects of combination chemotherapeutic drugs on various cancers. 

This Special Issue welcomes contributions on the following sub-topics:

●  Immunotherapy-based strategies for targeting resistant tumors;

●  Cancer stem cells/stem-like cells as targets in resistant tumor treatment;

●  Approaches based on the tumor microenvironment and metabolism for targeting resistant tumors;

●  Small molecule therapeutics for targeting cancer-resistant cells; 

●  Combination therapies for therapy-resistant cancer models.


Through this collection, we aim to foster a deeper understanding of strategies for targeting resistant tumors and to find new approaches to improve patient care.
keywords: ["Therapy resistance", "chemotherapeutic drugs", "small molecule inhibitors", "acquired resistance", "combination therapy"]

topic: Topic: Integrated Systems of Nanogenerators: Next-Generation Applications in Energy and Sensing
summary: The rapid advancement of integrated nanogenerator systems marks a transformative shift in energy harvesting, energy storage, and self-powered sensing technologies. Acting as sustainable, autonomous energy sources, nanogenerators are uniquely suited to meet the demands of next-generation applications across various fields that prioritize efficiency, resilience, and adaptability. This Special Issue is dedicated to advancing the design, materials, and integration of nanogenerators, emphasizing their critical role in developing self-powered systems that blend energy and sensing capabilities. By delving into novel material innovations, groundbreaking device architectures, and sophisticated integration techniques, this collection aims to enhance the energy efficiency, reliability, and versatility of nanogenerators in complex, real-world scenarios. Contributions that highlight breakthroughs in material science, environment monitoring, and device engineering, as well as interdisciplinary approaches to integrating nanogenerators into real-world applications, are particularly welcome.
keywords: ["Nanogenerators", "energy harvesting", "energy storage", "self-powered sensing"]

topic: Topic: Innovations in Hernia Repair and Mesh
summary:
Potential topics (but not limited to):
● Advances in Mesh Materials: An exploration of novel materials, including lightweight and hybrid meshes, designed to enhance biocompatibility, flexibility, and strength.
● Mesh Design and Fixation Techniques: Innovations in mesh designs, such as self-fixating meshes and those requiring no sutures or tacks, aimed at reducing operative time and postoperative pain.
● Bioabsorbable and Biologic Meshes: The use of bioabsorbable and biologic meshes in complex hernia repairs, particularly in patients at high risk of infection or with compromised tissue quality.
● Mesh Coatings and Anti-Adhesion Properties: Developments in mesh coatings that minimize the risk of adhesions and other complications, enhancing the safety of intraperitoneal placement.
● Customized Mesh Solutions: The rise of patient-specific and 3D-printed meshes tailored to individual anatomical and surgical needs, potentially improving fit and integration. This includes mesh sutures or other mesh related gadgets/materials.
● Long-term Outcomes and Recurrence Rates: Comparative studies on the long-term performance of different mesh types in minimally invasive hernia repair and their impact on recurrence rates.
● Complication Management: Strategies for managing complications associated with mesh, such as infection, chronic pain, and mesh migration.
● Robotic-Assisted Hernia Repair: The role of robotic systems in optimizing mesh placement and improving surgical precision in complex hernia cases.
● Training and Skill Development: The importance of training in new mesh technologies and techniques to enhance surgical proficiency and patient outcomes.
● Future Directions in Mesh Technology: Emerging trends and potential breakthroughs in mesh innovation, including smart meshes with integrated sensors for monitoring post-surgical outcomes.
keywords: ["Hernia", "mesh", "robotic", "repair", "MIS"]

topic: Topic: AI-Empowered Materials Science
summary:
Artificial Intelligence (AI)-Empowered Materials Science is an emerging field that accelerates the discovery and development of new materials through the use of AI technologies. Although still in its infancy, this field holds tremendous potential to revolutionize how we design and manufacture materials. Here are some specific issues that AI Materials Science can address:

(1) Accelerating Material Discovery: Traditional material discovery processes are time-consuming and costly. By leveraging AI, we can efficiently screen large-scale materials databases and rapidly identify potential new materials with desired properties. (2) Designing New Materials with Special Properties: AI technologies can assist in designing materials with specific attributes, such as high strength, low weight, or corrosion resistance, to meet a wider range of application needs. (3) Optimizing Material Processing: Through AI, we can improve material processing techniques, such as casting, forging, and machining, to enhance efficiency and product quality. (4) Predicting Material Performance: AI can predict material behavior under various environmental conditions, such as at specific temperatures, stress levels, or corrosive environments, providing essential references for material applications.

In this Special Issue, we will emphasize the challenges in accelerating material discovery and development using AI. We welcome original articles and topical reviews. The topics to be covered include, but are not limited to:
● High-throughput experimental/computational methods for building high-fidelity databases;
● AI-driven structural characterization of materials;
● Machine learning applications in additive manufacturing design;
● AI-guided applications of materials;
● AI optimization of material processing methods;
● AI-based predictions of material performance;
● AI-driven design of new materials with special properties.
keywords: ["AI", "materials science", "design and prediction", "manufacturing design"]

topic: Topic: Recent Research Progress and Application of Porous Organic Materials
summary:
Porous organic materials are a series of noble emerging porous materials that have attracted growing research interest because of their tunable structures, high specific surface areas and low densities. Many porous organic materials have been developed such as hypercrosslinked polymers (HCPs), covalent organic frameworks (COFs), and conjugated microporous polymers (CMPs). These materials hold great potential in many applications, such as gas adsorption/separation, heterogeneous catalysis, chemical sensing and water treatment. Current research efforts focus on developing large-scale/simple synthesis strategies and exploring their potential applications. Thus, this Special Issue aims to highlight recent progress in the broad interdisciplinary areas of porous organic materials and their applications.

Specific areas to be covered include, but are not limited to:

● Porous organic polymers, such as CMPs, HCPs, and COFs;

● Porous organic molecular materials, including porous organic cages (POCs) and hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks (HOFs);

● Porous organic membranes;

● Applications for porous organic materials in adsorption, catalysis, energy storage, separation, etc.
keywords: ["Porous organic molecular materials", "porous organic polymer", "covalent organic frameworks", "catalysis", "gas adsorption/separation"]

topic: Topic: AI for Space Information and Related Applications
summary:
Recent progress in artificial intelligence (AI) brings more potential opportunities and probabilities for data mining and problem-solving in science and engineering for space and air fields. In particular, the advances in deep learning techniques further accelerate the construction of large or foundation AI models to better approximate the complex systems in geoscience, air traffic and space physics.

This Special Issue focuses on the hot topic of space and air information processing with AI. Our scope not only encompasses scientific topics and issues in space- and air-based remote sensing and communications but also extends to space physics observations and data processing. We encourage the application of popular methods and frameworks in machine learning and deep learning to tackle challenging problems in space information and related areas. We aim to explore the potential applications of foundation models and multimodal frameworks in spaceborne and air-based remote sensing data and space and ground-based observations for space weather analysis. Additionally, we will investigate the potential role of AI in Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS), Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) and other space- and air-based techniques.
keywords: ["Space information", "artificial intelligence", "remote sensing", "deep learning", "foundation model"]

topic: Topic: Chemical Synthesis and Modulation of Advanced Materials for Energy Conversion and Storage Applications
summary:
The growing demand for sustainable energy solutions has made the development of advanced materials for energy storage and conversion critical to addressing future energy challenges. Chemical synthesis is pivotal in developing high-performance materials that improve the efficiency, capacity, and lifespan of energy storage systems, including batteries, supercapacitors, and fuel cells. Additionally, the precise modulation of material properties is essential for efficient energy conversion in devices such as solar cells and catalytic processes for hydrogen production, advancing clean energy pathways.

This Special Issue focuses on cutting-edge chemical synthesis techniques that target energy storage and conversion materials. A key highlight is the integration of advanced characterization methods, including in situ and operando techniques, which are instrumental in monitoring material synthesis and development, especially for structurally well-defined systems. These advanced techniques provide vital insights into material behavior throughout synthesis and operation, facilitating the precise modulation of material properties for optimized performance.

We invite contributions that explore innovative synthesis strategies for material design, functionalization, and fabrication to enhance energy system performance. Studies addressing challenges in scaling up material production for practical applications, as well as integrating multifunctional materials into devices, are also welcome. Furthermore, submissions discussing the modulation of material structures to achieve superior energy storage and conversion capabilities are encouraged.

Looking ahead, advancements in this field will rely on interdisciplinary approaches that integrate nanotechnology, electrochemistry, materials science, and modern characterization techniques. Emerging trends include the chemical synthesis of organic-inorganic hybrids, two-dimensional materials, and nanostructures with tailored properties for higher energy densities and faster charge/discharge rates. The integration of machine learning and artificial intelligence into material design and optimization represents a promising direction to accelerate discovery and application.

This Special Issue will accept original research articles, review articles, communications, and highlights. Topics of interest include, but are not limited to:
● Chemical synthesis and modulation of novel battery materials (e.g., lithium-ion, solid-state, and flow batteries);
● Design and chemical modulation of supercapacitor materials;
● Catalytic materials for hydrogen production and fuel cells;
● Photocatalysts for solar energy conversion;
● Synthesis of hybrid and composite materials for multifunctional energy systems;
● Advanced characterization techniques in chemical synthesis for energy materials;
● Scaling up and commercializing new energy storage and conversion materials.
keywords: ["Chemical synthesis", "energy storage materials", "modulation", "advanced characterization techniques", "batteries and supercapacitors", "catalysts for energy conversion", "hydrogen production", "photocatalysis", "nanomaterials for energy", "multifunctional energy materials", "2D materials", "organic-inorganic hybrids"]

topic: Topic: Unraveling the Dynamic Interplay between Microstructure and Functional Characteristics in Advanced Electroceramics
summary: Electroceramics exhibit a plethora of outstanding properties, including high mechanical strength, superior insulation resistance, exceptional temperature and humidity tolerance, radiation resilience, and a broad range of dielectric constants. Consequently, they are extensively employed across various sectors such as electronics, telecommunications, automotive industries, renewable energy, aerospace, and defense. Recognizing the crucial role of electroceramics in advancing economic and societal development, we are organizing a Special Issue titled "Unraveling the dynamic interplay between microstructure and functional characteristics in advanced electroceramics". This issue will encompass but not be limited to the following topics: (1) Manufacturing and processing techniques for electroceramics; (2) Dielectrics, piezoelectrics, ferroelectrics, and ferroics; (3) Electrical, optical, and magnetic characteristics of electroceramics; (4) Device applications and innovations; (5) Advanced characterization and simulation methodologies. We cordially invite scholars to submit their manuscripts for consideration.
keywords: ["Ceramics", "piezoelectrics", "dielectrics", "ferroelectrics", "devices", "manufacturing", "performances", "characterization", "simulation"]

topic: Topic: Emerging Imaging Biomarkers in the Diagnosis and Prognosis of Cardiovascular Diseases
summary: The rapid advancement of imaging technologies has significantly deepened our understanding of cardiovascular diseases, driving the discovery of new biomarkers that enhance diagnostic and prognostic capabilities. In recent years, innovative imaging biomarkers have become essential tools for uncovering disease pathophysiology, enabling clinicians to detect subclinical changes earlier and improve patient risk stratification. Advances in imaging modalities now permit provide quantitative insights that surpass traditional measures, capturing complex interactions within cardiovascular diseases. These developments have not only improved diagnostic precision but have also strengthened our ability to customize therapeutic approaches based on individual disease profiles. This Special Issue on "Emerging Imaging Biomarkers in the Diagnosis and Prognosis of Cardiovascular Diseases" delves into pioneering developments in imaging technology and their pivotal role in identifying biomarkers for early disease detection and patient stratification, ultimately advancing precision medicine.
keywords: ["Cardiovascular diseases", "coronary arteries", "carotid arteries", "computed tomography", "magnetic resonance imaging", "cardiomyopathy"]

topic: Topic: Microstructure-Driven Performance Improvements in Lithium-Sulfur Batteries
summary: The Special Issue "Microstructure-Driven Performance Improvements in Lithium-Sulfur Batteries" seeks to address the challenges associated with lithium-sulfur batteries, an emerging high-energy storage technology. It will focus on novel microstructured material designs and approaches that improve battery stability, cycling life, and sulfur utilization efficiency. By emphasizing the role of material architecture, synthesis techniques, and electrochemical performance optimization, this collection aims to push the boundaries of current lithium-sulfur battery research. This issue will explore recent progress in the field, with an emphasis on underexplored aspects of microstructural design and its direct impact on battery performance. It will highlight how advanced material modifications, including multi-scale porous structures and hybrid material strategies, can resolve key limitations such as shuttle effect mitigation and polysulfide trapping. Contributors are encouraged to share insights into fundamental mechanisms, scalable synthesis methods, and novel characterization techniques that bridge the gap between theoretical models and practical applications. Ultimately, this Special Issue aims to inspire new research directions and technological innovations, offering insights that can drive the commercial development of high-performance lithium-sulfur batteries.
keywords: ["Lithium-sulfur batteries", "high energy density", "microstructured materials", "material architecture optimization", "cycling stability", "sulfur utilization efficiency", "advanced synthesis techniques", "polysulfide management", "electrochemical reaction kinetics"]

topic: Topic: Heavy Metal(loid)s exposure and their adverse effects on Human Health
summary:
Heavy metal pollution is a globally recognized environmental issue, threatening human health. The increasing population and rising food demands contribute to the excessive release of various metal(loid)s into the environment, e.g., soil and water, which subsequently contaminate food crops. The adverse effects of exposure to these contaminants on human health often depend on the speciation of metal(loid)s. Therefore, speciation assessment of metal(loid)s in soils, food plants, human body fluid, hair, nails, and other associated biological tissues is pivotal to understanding key aspects of heavy metals'  health risks, including their bioavailability, species-specific toxicity, and various biological responses. Knowledge of speciation is crucial for defining risk assessment of toxic metals and their excretion from the human body in cases of overexposure. Elucidation of the composition and speciation of metal(loid)s will help scientists to accurately assess their human health risks and detrimental effects. Consequently, this research topic aims to explore state-of-the-art techniques and methods for speciation analysis within environmental and biological matrices relevant to human exposure and associated health impacts.

All submissions MUST consider the implications for human and/or wildlife exposure to contaminants. Please refer to the Aims and Scope of JEEA for further guidance.
keywords: ["Heavy metal(loid)s", "speciation analysis", "bioaccessibility and bioavailability", "health risks assessment", "cytotoxicity"]

source: Journal of Translational Genetics and Genomics
topic: Topic: Molecular Innovation in Forensic Genetics
summary:
This Special Issue focuses on the latest advancements in molecular innovation in forensic genetics, a field that has seen rapid growth in recent years due to technological progress in genetic analysis and molecular biology. Forensic genetics, which applies genetic techniques to support criminal investigations, legal disputes, and individual identification, has become an indispensable tool in modern forensic science. Recent breakthroughs in molecular techniques, such as next-generation sequencing (NGS), advanced DNA profiling methods, and the use of epigenetic markers, are enhancing the accuracy, sensitivity, and applicability of forensic genetic analysis.

In this issue, we invite original research articles, reviews, and case studies that explore:

● The development of novel molecular techniques and technologies for forensic DNA analysis;

● Applications of next-generation sequencing and other high-throughput methods in forensic genetics;

● New approaches for analyzing complex DNA samples, including degraded, mixed, or trace samples;

● The integration of epigenetics, microbiome analysis, and other emerging molecular markers in forensic investigations;

● New methods, strategies and algorithms for complex kinship identification, especially for relationships spanning three or more degrees;

● Advances in databases, algorithms, and panels for forensic investigative genetic genealogy in the genomic era;

● Ethical, legal, and social implications of using advanced molecular techniques in forensic contexts.


By bringing together cutting-edge research in genetics, molecular biology, and forensic science, this Special Issue aims to provide a comprehensive overview of how molecular innovations are transforming forensic genetics, offering new possibilities for solving criminal cases, identifying missing persons, and advancing justice.
keywords: ["Forensic DNA", "forensic genetics", "forensic investigations", "ethical", "legal"]

source: Journal of Translational Genetics and Genomics
topic: Topic: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy in the Genomic Era: Advances in Diagnosis and Therapeutics
summary:
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is one of the most common genetic cardiovascular disorders, characterized by left ventricular hypertrophy in the absence of other causative conditions.
